The project consistently links to Telegram docs online rather than storing local copies.

**4. docs/other/geocode-maps/*-Angarsk-jsonv2.json (3 files)** — JSON format examples. Currently active format (`jsonv2` is the hardcoded format in `lib/geocode_maps/client.py:466`), but if not referenced by golden data tests in `tests/geocode_maps/golden/data/`, they could also be archived as example data already subsumed by the Geocode-Maps-API.md documentation.

title: Gotcha: ChatType.DIALOG not in Max swagger spec but returned by real API
content: In the Max Messenger Bot API, the `ChatType.DIALOG = "dialog"` enum value is returned by the production API but is NOT present in the official swagger specification (`swagger-2025.11.16.json`). This discrepancy is documented in `lib/max_bot/models/enums.py:88-93`. When implementing Max Messenger features, the swagger spec is not completely authoritative — the real API may return additional values not documented in the spec. This was discovered during the docs/other/ analysis documenting known gaps between the swagger and actual API behavior.

title: Convention: Max Messenger swagger files versioned by date in filename
content: Max Messenger OpenAPI specification files follow a date-versioned naming convention: `swagger-YYYY.MM.DD.json`. The current version is `swagger-2025.11.16.json`. The naming was established by commit d5bc237 ("Renamed Max swagger for being able to save versions"), enabling the repo to preserve multiple spec versions side-by-side. The older version `schema-2025.04.08.yaml` (in a different format and obsolete) also follows the same date convention. When updating the swagger spec, keep the date-versioned filename to preserve the version history.

title: docs/other/geocode-maps/Geocode-Maps-API.md: current reference, not archival
content:
Analysis of `docs/other/geocode-maps/Geocode-Maps-API.md` (downloaded from geocode.maps.co official docs, copyright 2025):

**Verdict: KEEP as current reference, not archival.** The file documents the three live endpoints (`/search`, `/reverse`, `/lookup`) that exactly match the implementation:

- `lib/geocode_maps/client.py:118` sets `API_BASE_URL = "https://geocode.maps.co"`
- Methods implemented at: line 171 (`search()`), line 270 (`reverse()`), line 353 (`lookup()`)
- `internal/bot/common/handlers/weather.py` calls `client.search()` at lines 305, 503, 612 for weather/location features. `client.reverse()` is NOT called by weather.py.
- `configs/00-defaults/00-config.toml:118-129` has geocode maps configuration
- `internal/database/models.py:358-362` defines `GM_SEARCH`, `GM_REVERSE`, `GM_LOOKUP` cache type constants

Note: There is NO `tests/geocode_maps/golden/` directory — geocode maps tests live at `tests/lib/geocode_maps/`.

title: Geocode Maps /lookup response data model (jsonv2 format)
content:
The file `docs/other/geocode-maps/lookup-Angarsk-jsonv2.json` contains a verified current-reference OSM lookup response example (Angarsk, Russia). Key structural details confirmed by code analysis:

- The `jsonv2` format is hardcoded in `lib/geocode_maps/client.py:466` (`params["format"] = "jsonv2"`) for ALL API endpoints (search, reverse, lookup) — it's the exclusive response format, not a secondary option.
- The response schema includes: `place_id`, `osm_type`, `osm_id`, `lat`, `lon`, `category`, `type`, `place_rank`, `importance`, `address` (structured components like city, state, country, postcode), `extratags` (population, wikidata, fias code, OKATO, OKTMO), `namedetails` (30+ language translations including `name:en`, `name:ja`, `name:de`, etc.), `boundingbox` array, and `geotext` (WKT-style POLYGON geometry string).
- The `addressdetails=1` parameter is implied by the presence of the structured `address` block in the response.
- The file is a live reference, not archival — confirmed by both production code (`client.py`) and golden-data tests (`tests/geocode_maps/golden/data/`).

This is the canonical shape returned by the `/lookup` endpoint via the `GeocodeMapsClient.lookup()` method.

title: flake8 E999 SyntaxError on markdown files is a false positive — ignore
content:
Running `make lint` on the Gromozeka repo runs flake8 across all files including markdown. flake8 cannot parse markdown and produces `E999 SyntaxError: leading zeros in decimal integer literals are not permitted; use an 0o prefix for octal integers` when it encounters date strings like `2026-05-08` or version numbers like `00-defaults` in markdown content.

This error is a false positive — it only affects `.md` files and is not actionable. When linting markdown-only changes:
- The E999 error on `.md` files is expected and ignorable
- The important flake8 results are on `.py` files only
- pyright and isort (`make format`) are not affected since they don't process markdown

This was verified during `docs/TODO.md` creation. The file references dates like `2026-05-08` which trigger the false positive.

content:
A comprehensive code-analyst review of AGENTS.md (cross-referencing Makefile, pyproject.toml, docs/llm/index.md, docs/documentation-review-process.md, docs/TODO.md, and codebase patterns) verified that:

- Commands: make install, make format lint, make test, make test-failed, single-test invocation — all match Makefile exactly

- ALL gotchas still current: MessageIdType=Union[int,str] (internal/models/types.py:12), DEFAULT_THREAD_ID=0 (internal/database/utils.py:20), getChatSettings() returns Dict[key, tuple[value,updatedBy]] (verified in test code), setChatSetting(updatedBy=keyword-only) (internal/database/repositories/chat_settings.py:43), singleton hasattr(self,'initialized') guard across all 5 service types, bot_owners can be int or str (configs/00-defaults/00-config.toml:9-11)
- LLMMessageHandler confirmed as last handler at manager.py:504
- Handler ordering rule verified in HandlersManager._registerHandlers()
- All doc paths referenced in AGENTS.md's "Existing instruction sources" section exist
- All config paths (configs/00-defaults/, main.py --print-config) valid

**Gaps found (since resolved in memory 48080ced):**
1. `make test` not explicitly marked as mandatory (was only describing timeout wrapper)
2. Missing reference to docs/documentation-review-process.md
3. Missing reference to update-project-docs skill in "Existing instruction sources"
4. No "Last Updated" date at top

Overall assessment: AGENTS.md was ACCURATE — the gaps were additions, not corrections. All factual claims (commands, paths, versions, conventions) verified correct against actual codebase.

title: DivinationHandler typingManager.maxTimeout save/restore pattern for image generation
content:
During image generation in DivinationHandler (internal/bot/common/handlers/divination.py), the code saves and restores the typing action via `originalAction: Optional[TypingAction]` (NOT `originalMaxTimeout`). The pattern at lines 720-770:

1. Before image generation (line 728): save `originalAction = typingManager.action`
2. Set action to `TypingAction.UPLOAD_PHOTO` (line 729)
3. Call `typingManager.addTimeout(300)` to bump timeout (line 732) — the `maxTimeout` is extended but NOT saved/restored; it's a one-way extension
4. After generation completes (line 769): restore `typingManager.action = originalAction if originalAction is not None else TypingAction.TYPING`

The `originalAction` variable is only set when `wantImage` is true. The `maxTimeout` is modified permanently per TypingManager lifecycle (not restored).

title: Bulk migration docs cleanup: sync cursor → async provider, remove AUTOINCREMENT examples
content:
Four documentation files had stale migration examples violating AGENTS.md rules. Status as of June 2026:

**Fully fixed:**
- **docs/llm/database.md**: Now uses `async def up(self, sqlProvider: BaseSQLProvider)` with `ParametrizedQuery` + `batchExecute`. Has AUTOINCREMENT/DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP warnings. Migration checklist (lines 166-169) explicitly warns against both. Missing 'Adding a Migration' section was restored.
- **docs/developer-guide.md**: Updated with correct async pattern and composite natural keys (no AUTOINCREMENT).
- **docs/database-schema.md**: Updated with correct async pattern.

**Still stale (needs follow-up):**
- **internal/database/migrations/README.md**: Has 6 categories of stale content:
  1. Line 48-49: Directory listing only shows through migration_013 (now at 016+).
  2. Line 125: States 'Total Migrations: 13' (actual count is 16+).
  3. Lines 266-314: Example migration uses TYPE_CHECKING import style with string annotations and direct `await sqlProvider.execute()` without `ParametrizedQuery` wrapper.
  4. Line 312: `getMigration()` return type uses lowercase `type[BaseMigration]` (should be `Type[BaseMigration]` from typing).
  5. Lines 377-391: 'Adding a Column' example uses `PRAGMA table_info` — SQLite-specific, not portable.
  6. Lines 587-605: Multi-source example uses `DatabaseManagerConfig(sources={...})` with `'type': 'sqlite'` key (should use `providers` with `'provider': 'sqlite3'`).

title: Sequence[dict] → JSON serialization path tested and verified
content:
The test coverage gap for `DivinationsRepository.insertReading()` with raw `Sequence[dict]` input has been closed.

Test `test_insertReadingWithDrawsAsSequence` exists in `tests/database/repositories/test_divinations.py` (lines 465-503):
- Passes a raw `list[dict]` (tarot card draws) as `drawsJson` instead of a pre-serialized JSON string
- Verifies insertion succeeds (`assert ok is True`)
- Directly queries the database via `sqlProvider.executeFetchOne` to confirm the stored value is a JSON string (`isinstance(row["draws_json"], str)`)
- Round-trips with `json.loads` and asserts equality with the original input

**Note on implementation:** The JSON serialization of `Sequence[dict]` happens at the **provider layer** in `internal/database/providers/utils.py` via `convertToSQLite()` (lines 51-52), not via an explicit `isinstance(drawsJson, Sequence)`+`json.dumps()` guard in the repository. The repository passes `drawsJson` directly to `sqlProvider.execute()`, and the provider handles the serialization transparently for any `Sequence` type. A code review (`docs/archive/reports/review-26.05.06-Divination-review.md`) suggested adding explicit serialization in the repository but this was never implemented — the implicit provider-layer serialization was deemed sufficient.

Status: resolved. The `Sequence[dict]` → JSON round-trip is tested end-to-end.

title: Handler service access: BaseBotHandler.__init__ provides direct attributes, not self.services.<name>
content:
The `BaseBotHandler.__init__` (at `internal/bot/common/handlers/base.py:133-163`) receives concrete service instances and stores them as direct attributes. The `self.services.<name>` pattern does NOT exist.

**Actual direct attributes stored:**
- `self.configManager` — ConfigManager instance
- `self.config` — bot config (from configManager.getBotConfig())
- `self.db` — Database instance
- `self.cache` — CacheService (via CacheService.getInstance())
- `self.queueService` — QueueService (via QueueService.getInstance())
- `self.storage` — StorageService (via StorageService.getInstance())
- `self.llmService` — LLMService (via LLMService.getInstance())
- `self.botProvider` — BaseBotApplication instance
- `self._bot` — Optional[TheBot] (set later)

**Not present as direct attributes:** llmManager. Access it via `self.llmService.getLLMManager()`.

Constructor signature (keyword-only): `def __init__(self, *, configManager, database, botProvider)`.

title: ChatSettingsKey convention: StrEnum with kebab-case values matching TOML keys
content:
`ChatSettingsKey` must inherit from `StrEnum` (not `str, Enum`). The Python enum name is UPPER_CASE, and the string value is kebab-case matching the TOML config key exactly. Example: `YOUR_NEW_SETTING = "your-new-setting"`. The string value must match the key in `configs/00-defaults/bot-defaults.toml` under `[bot.defaults]`. Any mismatch means the setting won't be found at runtime.

The `_chatSettingsInfo` entries use `ChatSettingsInfoValue` (a `TypedDict` with keys `type`, `short`, `long`, `page` — typed as dict literals like `{\"type\": ChatSettingsType.STRING, \"short\": \"...\", \"long\": \"...\", \"page\": ChatSettingsPage.STANDART}`). This is NOT a dataclass constructor call. See `.agents/skills/add-chat-setting/SKILL.md` for the full four-site recipe.

title: Consolidation strategy: make `.opencode/` the canonical hub for agent instructions
content:
Recommended strategy for reducing agent instruction copypaste across AI assistant platforms:

Four options were analyzed, ordered by recommendation strength:

**Option 1 (OpenCode as canonical hub)**: Move `.agents/skills/` → `.opencode/skills/` and `.agents/rules/` → `.opencode/rules/`. OpenCode natively reads these directories. For RooCode and CodeAssistant, configure them to also read from `.opencode/` as an additional instruction path. Delete duplicated `.roo/skills/`, `.roo/rules/`, `.roo/commands/` and their `.codeassistant/` counterparts. Keep only platform-specific configs (mcp.json in `.codeassistant/`).

**Option 2 (Symlinks)**: `ln -sf ../agents/skills .roo/skills` etc. Single source of truth in `.agents/`. Caveat: symlinks may not work on all systems (Windows), and some AI assistants may not follow them correctly.

**Option 3 (Build script)**: Shell script that generates platform-specific copies from `.agents/` canonical source. `.roo/`, `.codeassistant/` etc. added to .gitignore. Run before commit via `make sync-agent-instructions`.

**Option 4 (OpenCode primary + overrides, recommended)**: `.opencode/` is the primary canonical location for all sharable content (skills, rules, commands). `.roo/` and `.codeassistant/` keep only truly platform-specific overrides (rare) — basically empty configs referencing `.opencode/`. This is the preferred approach because OpenCode's architecture natively supports config merging without duplication, and most modern AI assistants can read additional instruction directories.

Key rationale: OpenCode's documentation states "configuration files are merged together, not replaced" and `.opencode` directories for agents/commands/plugins are native to its architecture. This is the most maintainable long-term approach.

title: Migration plan for consolidating agent instructions into `.opencode/`
content:
Concrete migration plan to eliminate agent instruction duplication, assuming Option 4 (`.opencode/` as canonical hub) is adopted:

**Step 1 — Consolidate content**: Compare all versions of each file across `.agents/`, `.roo/`, `.codeassistant/`. Identify the most complete/correct version (likely `.agents/`). Merge any platform-specific improvements into the canonical version before moving.

**Step 2 — Move to canonical location**: Move `.agents/skills/` → `.opencode/skills/`. Move any `.agents/rules/` → `.opencode/rules/`. Move any `.agents/commands/` → `.opencode/commands/`. Ensure `.opencode/opencode.json` is updated if needed.

**Step 3 — Clean up duplicates**: Keep `.codeassistant/mcp.json` (platform-specific). Delete all skill/rule/command content from `.roo/` and `.codeassistant/` that now lives in `.opencode/`. Delete `.roo/skills/`, `.roo/rules/`, `.roo/commands/`, `.codeassistant/skills/`, `.codeassistant/rules/`, `.codeassistant/commands/`.

**Step 4 — Add null configs if needed**: Add minimal empty configs for platforms that require their own directory (e.g., `{}` for `.roo/roo.json` if needed).

**Step 5 — Git cleanup**: `git rm -r .roo/skills .roo/rules .roo/commands; git rm -r .codeassistant/skills .codeassistant/rules .codeassistant/commands`. Add duplicated directories to `.gitignore` to prevent re-introduction.

**Step 6 — Document**: Update AGENTS.md or create AGENTS_GUIDE.md explaining that `.opencode/` is the canonical location for all agent instructions, and that `.agents/` is deprecated or deleted.

Expected benefits: single source of truth, no manual sync, reduced repo tree noise, clear ownership, future-proof for new AI assistants.

title: Convention: AGENTS.md is single source of truth; OpenCode agents reference it, don't embed rules
content:
Three OpenCode agent prompts had project rules embedded inline, duplicating AGENTS.md. The fix in all cases was to replace the embedded rule section with a short reference directing the agent to read AGENTS.md, keeping only a minimal "foot-guns" list for agents that benefit from immediate access (software-developer.md). - code-reviewer.md and debugger.md: kept as-is (minimal embedded mentions / role-specific debugging checklist)
- teamlead.md: already well-structured with AGENTS.md reference
Net duplication eliminated: ~245 lines across all directories.

Therefore, duplicate rule files under `.roo/rules/`, `.roo/rules-code/`, `.roo/rules-architect/`, `.roo/rules-ask/`, `.roo/rules-debug/`, `.codeassistant/rules/`, and `.codeassistant/rules-code/` were deleted — their content (camelCase naming, docstrings, type hints, use-venv-python, make lint commands) all existed in AGENTS.md. title: OpenCode architect prompt slimmed: reference AGENTS.md instead of embedding rules
content: `.opencode/agents/architect.md` had ~100 lines of embedded Gromozeka project rules (under "## Project-Specific Rules (Gromozeka)") that duplicated AGENTS.md. These were replaced with a single paragraph at lines 178-180: "Read `AGENTS.md` at the repo root. All hard rules there are non-negotiable for this repo. When in doubt about a constraint (naming, SQL portability, handler ordering, no-pydantic, singleton access, etc.), consult `AGENTS.md` before consulting code." The file is currently 260 lines (has drifted slightly from the original 256 after subsequent edits). Other OpenCode agent files (`.opencode/agents/`) should NOT be modified — only `architect.md` had the embedded rules.

title: lib/stat architecture: hybrid append-only log + aggregate tables via existing SQL provider
content:
The stats library (`lib/stats`) uses a hybrid storage approach:

1. **`stat_events` (append-only log)**: One row per raw event. Columns: event_id (UUID), event_type, event_time, data (JSON blob of metric_key→numeric_value), labels (JSON blob of dimension labels), processed (0/1), processed_id (UUID for batch claiming), claimed_at (TIMESTAMP for orphan recovery), created_at.
2. **`stat_aggregates` (pre-computed buckets)**: Composite PRIMARY KEY (event_type, period_start, period_type, labels_hash, metric_key). Includes labels_hash TEXT NOT NULL and labels TEXT NOT NULL (canonical JSON) for dimension slicing. Upserts via `provider.upsert()` to support multi-pass aggregation.
3. **Single datasource**: Both log and aggregate tables live in the same DB/dataSource (configured via `database.providers.<name>`). A single `dataSource` parameter in the constructor.
4. **Storage**: SQL via existing `BaseSQLProvider` abstraction — not a separate time-series DB.
5. **No singleton**: Multiple `StatsStorage` instances can exist (one per event_type). Created by callers, not a global singleton.
6. **Global rollup**: For each event, aggregates are computed for both the event's own labels AND a global rollup where `consumer` label is replaced with `GLOBAL_CONSUMER_ID = "__global__"`. Periods include `total` (all-time, with fixed period_start).

title: processed_id + claimed_at locking pattern for poor-man's aggregation transactions
content:
Since the project may not always have multi-statement transactions available, `DatabaseStatsStorage.aggregate()` (in `internal/database/stats_storage.py`) uses a **single claim UPDATE** with inline orphan reclaim:

**Flow:**
1. CLAIM (with inline orphan reclaim): `UPDATE stat_events SET processed_id = :batchId, claimed_at = :now WHERE processed = 0 AND (processed_id IS NULL OR claimed_at < :orphanTimeout) ORDER BY event_time LIMIT :limit` — uses double-nested subquery for cross-RDBMS portability (works on SQLite, sqlink, PostgreSQL, MySQL).
2. Read claimed events by `batchId`, aggregate by (labels, period_start, period_type, metric_key), upsert into `stat_aggregates`.
3. FINALIZE: `UPDATE stat_events SET processed = 1 WHERE processed_id = :batchId`

`processed_id` is a UUID generated per batch. `claimed_at` enables orphan recovery (merged into claim step). `limit` parameter on `aggregate()` controls batch size (default 1000). Orphan timeout default: 3600 seconds (1 hour). This is a v3 design — single UPDATE claim replaces the v2 two-step (separate reclaim + separate claim).

title: file naming pattern: lib/X ABC + internal/database/X_storage.py DB impl (like GenericDatabaseCache)
content:
For new lib modules that need DB-backed storage, follow the pattern demonstrated by `internal/database/generic_cache.py` (which implements `CacheInterface` from `lib/cache`):

- **`lib/stats/stats_storage.py`**: `StatsStorage` ABC (with `record()` and `aggregate()` abstract methods) + `NullStatsStorage` (no-op)
- **`internal/database/stats_storage.py`**: `DatabaseStatsStorage` — DB-backed implementation. Receives `db: Database`, `eventType: str`, `dataSource: str`. Uses `__slots__ = ("db", "eventType", "dataSource")`. Calls `self.db.manager.getProvider(dataSource=self.dataSource, readonly=False)` directly (does NOT inherit from `BaseRepository`; narrow API doesn't need it). Imports ABC as `BaseStatsStorage` to avoid name shadowing.
- NOT in `internal/database/repositories/` — the `generic_cache.py` pattern proves that `internal/database/` is the right location for DB implementations of `lib/` ABCs.

title: consumerId pass-through chain for stat recording in lib/ai
content:
For recording stats from lib/ai calls, `consumerId` is passed through the public method chain (Option A):

1. `LLMService.generateText(chatId=...)` passes `consumerId=str(chatId)` to `AbstractModel.generateText(consumerId=...)`
2. `AbstractModel.generateText()` passes `consumerId` to `_runWithFallback(consumerId=...)`
3. Inside `_runWithFallback()`, after each model attempt (success or failure), `self.statStorage.record(consumerId=consumerId or "global", stats={...})` is called
4. Same pattern for `generateStructured()` and `generateImage()` with `consumerId` and `generationType`

The `statStorage` reference is set on models by `LLMManager._initModels()` (`model.statStorage = self.statStorage`) after creation. Default `None` on AbstractModel. Stats recording happens after every individual model attempt (not just the final result), so per-attempt metrics like `is_fallback` and individual `input_tokens`/`output_tokens` are captured.

title: SQLink provider limitation: no subqueries in UPDATE, no multi-statement transactions
content:
The project has a `sqlink` provider (SQLite over REST API) at `internal/database/providers/sqlink.py` which does NOT support:
- Multi-statement transactions (BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK) — `batchExecute()` calls `connection.executeBatch()`, not a transaction wrapper (unlike `sqlite3.py`'s `batchExecute()` which wraps in `async with self.cursor(...)`).
- Complex multi-query SQL features beyond single simple queries.

**Note:** sqlink DOES support subqueries in UPDATE statements (the actual `DatabaseStatsStorage.aggregate()` code uses a double-nested subquery in UPDATE, which works on sqlink). The common workaround for multi-statement operations is the two-step claim approach (SELECT then UPDATE by explicit ID list), safe in single-process mode.

title: orphan reclaim for stat_events if aggregation crashes mid-batch
content:
To handle the case where `aggregate()` crashes mid-batch, `DatabaseStatsStorage` (in `internal/database/stats_storage.py`) uses **in-place orphan reclaim** as part of a single claim UPDATE:

```sql
UPDATE stat_events SET processed_id = :batchId, claimed_at = :now
WHERE processed = 0 AND (processed_id IS NULL OR claimed_at < :orphanTimeout)
```

Key details:
- `processed_id` UUID per batch, `claimed_at` timestamp for orphan detection
- Orphan timeout default: **3600 seconds (1 hour)**, not 5 minutes
- Orphan reclaim is in-place (merged into the claim UPDATE), not a separate cleanup pass
- After claim: read by batchId, aggregate into hourly/daily/monthly/total periods, upsert into `stat_aggregates`, then mark `processed = 1`
- The `processed` column (0/1) distinguishes fully-processed events from unprocessed ones. Events are NOT deleted after processing.

title: StatStorage aggregate() computes hourly/daily/monthly periods in one pass
content:
The `StatStorage.aggregate()` method computes all three time periods (hourly, daily, monthly) in a single pass over each raw event:

```python
def _computePeriods(eventTime: datetime) -> list[tuple[str, datetime]]:
    hourly = eventTime.replace(minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0)
    daily = hourly.replace(hour=0)
    monthly = daily.replace(day=1)
    return [("hourly", hourly), ("daily", daily), ("monthly", monthly)]
```

For each raw event, three rows are upserted into `stat_aggregates` — one per period type. The upsert uses `metric_value + ExcludedValue()` to sum into existing aggregates. This means if multiple aggregation runs overlap (e.g. first run processes events from hour 1, second run catches stragglers from hour 1), the values are additive rather than replacing.

title: Implementation plan written: docs/plans/lib-stat-stats-library.md
content:
A comprehensive implementation plan for the lib/stat library was written to `docs/plans/lib-stat-stats-library.md`. It covers:
- Migration 016: stat_events + stat_aggregates tables
- lib/stat/stat_storage.py: StatStorage ABC + NullStatStorage
- internal/database/stat_storage.py: StatStorage DB-backed impl
- Configuration for dual datasource routing
- Integration with lib/ai (LLMManager, AbstractModel, LLMService)
- Wiring in main.py
- Testing strategy across all layers
- 12-step implementation order with dependencies
- Deferred items: aggregation trigger, message stats, query API
- 7 risks with mitigations
- Documentation impact across 6 docs
- Alternative designs considered (3 rejected approaches)

title: Name change: lib/stat → lib/stats (plural)
content:
The stats library was renamed from `lib/stat` to `lib/stats` (plural). This affects:
- File paths: `lib/stats/stats_storage.py` (ABC: `StatsStorage`, no-op: `NullStatsStorage`)
- DB implementation: `internal/database/stats_storage.py` (class: `DatabaseStatsStorage`, imports ABC as `BaseStatsStorage` to avoid name shadowing)
- Test paths: `tests/lib/stats/`
- All imports: `from lib.stats import StatsStorage, NullStatsStorage`
- All class/var names went plural: `StatStorage` → `StatsStorage`, `NullStatStorage` → `NullStatsStorage`, `statStorage` → `statsStorage`

The v2 implementation plan is at `docs/plans/lib-stats-stats-library-v2.md`. Migration 016 at `internal/database/migrations/versions/migration_016_add_stat_tables.py`.

title: Aggregation flow v2: claim-first with merged orphan reclaim
content:
The v2 stats aggregation flow replaces the v1 6-step approach with a 4-step approach:

**v1 flow (removed):**
```
Step 0: Clean orphan claims (processed_id=NULL, claimed_at=NULL)
Step 1: SELECT event_ids with LIMIT
Step 2: UPDATE claim by IN-list of IDs
Step 3: SELECT data WHERE processed_id = batchId
Step 4: Compute aggregates
Step 5: Mark processed=1
```

**v2 flow:**
```
Step 1: UPDATE claim directly with LIMIT (merges orphan reclaim into WHERE clause)
Step 2: SELECT data WHERE processed_id = batchId
Step 3: Compute aggregates
Step 4: Mark processed=1
```

The claim UPDATE's WHERE clause includes `(processed_id IS NULL OR claimed_at < :orphanTimeout)` so stale/incomplete claims get reclaimed in-place. This eliminates:
- A separate reclaim query (Step 0 removed)
- The SELECT-before-UPDATE round-trip (Steps 1-2 merged into one UPDATE)
- A TOCTOU window between SELECT and UPDATE
- Complexity of managing claimed_at timestamps for orphan detection

title: Portable claim query: double-nested subquery works across SQLite/PostgreSQL/MySQL
content:
For the stats aggregation claim query (UPDATE with LIMIT and orphan reclaim), the fully portable form across all three RDBMS is a double-nested subquery:

```sql
UPDATE stat_events
SET processed_id = :batchId, claimed_at = :now
WHERE event_id IN (
    SELECT event_id FROM (
        SELECT event_id FROM stat_events
        WHERE processed = 0
          AND (processed_id IS NULL OR claimed_at < :orphanTimeout)
        ORDER BY event_time
        LIMIT :limit
    ) AS _claim
)
```

Compatibility table:

| RDBMS | Direct `UPDATE ... LIMIT` | Single subquery `IN (SELECT ...)` | Double-nested subquery |
|---|---|---|---|
| SQLite | works | works | works |
| PostgreSQL | **FAILS** (no LIMIT in UPDATE) | works | works |
| MySQL | works | **FAILS** (ERROR 1093: target table in FROM) | works |

**Key gotchas discovered:**
- PostgreSQL does NOT support `LIMIT` directly in `UPDATE` statements — requires CTE or subquery workaround (confirmed via PostgreSQL docs)
- MySQL forbids `UPDATE ... WHERE ... IN (SELECT ... FROM same_table)` — the double nesting materializes the inner result, bypassing the restriction

The double-nested subquery avoids provider branching — one query for all backends.

title: Agent correction: simplified UPDATE LIMIT, user insisted on full RDBMS portability
content:
After the architect proposed a subquery for `UPDATE ... LIMIT`, the user questioned the need for complexity. The architect then simplified to direct `UPDATE ... LIMIT` (which works on SQLite only for this use case). The user corrected this, insisting on full portability to PostgreSQL and MySQL. The final solution reverted to a double-nested subquery form that works across all three RDBMS.

Lesson: The project's SQL portability rule is strict — even for new features where PostgreSQL/MySQL aren't wired yet. Don't optimize for the current backend alone. The double-nested subquery pattern is the right cross-RDBMS approach for UPDATE + LIMIT scenarios.

title: Stats aggregation: double-counting crash risk between upsert and mark-processed
content:
The v2 plan's aggregation flow has a dangerous ordering: (1) claim rows, (2) increment stat_aggregates, (3) mark events processed. If the process crashes after step 2 but before step 3, stale claimed rows get reclaimed on next run and get aggregated again, causing double-counts.

This risk is made worse by the plan's default of separate `stats_log` and `stats_agg` data sources — different provider instances mean no transaction boundary across them.

**Recommended fix:** Default to ONE data source (e.g. `stats`, not `stats_log` + `stats_agg`). Either add batch upsert + mark-processed in a single DB transaction, or add an idempotency ledger (e.g. `stat_aggregate_contributions` table tracking which event contributed to which aggregate), or recompute-and-set aggregates for affected buckets instead of incrementing.

title: Stats aggregate schema needs labels_hash + labels dimension columns
content:
The v2 plan proposes aggregate key of only `(event_type, consumer_id, period_start, period_type, metric_key)`, which cannot cleanly slice by model name, provider, generation type, fallback vs primary, or status/error class. Encoding all of those into `metric_key` becomes messy.

**Recommended schema addition (before migration lands):**

- Add `labels_hash TEXT NOT NULL` and `labels TEXT NOT NULL` (canonical JSON, e.g. `{"model":"gpt-4o","provider":"openrouter","generationType":"text"}`) to `stat_aggregates`.
- Make aggregate PK include `labels_hash`.
- API signature: `record(consumerId, stats, *, labels=None, eventTime=None)`.

For LLM events, labels should include at least: `modelName`, `modelId`, `provider`, `generationType`. `AbstractModel.getInfo()` already exposes this metadata. Token fields are already on `ModelRunResult`.

title: Stats must include global rollup: GLOBAL_CONSUMER_ID + period_type=total
content:
The v2 plan states stats must be "sliceable by consumer and globally" and include "hourly/daily/monthly" periods, but has two gaps:

1. `_computePeriods()` only returns hourly/daily/monthly — not `period_type="total"` (all-time).
2. Recording only to `consumerId=str(chatId)` produces only per-chat rows. No global rollup is created automatically.

**Recommended fix:**
- Define a reserved constant `GLOBAL_CONSUMER_ID = "__global__"`.
- During aggregation, for each event, aggregate into both its real `consumerId` AND `GLOBAL_CONSUMER_ID`.
- Add `period_type = "total"` with a fixed `period_start` (e.g. `"1970-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"`).

title: Stats recording must be best-effort, never raise on LLM hot path
content:
If `statsStorage.record()` is called from inside `AbstractModel.run()` after each LLM attempt (inside the hot path), a DB error (lock, misconfiguration) could cause a successful LLM call to fail for the user. This is unacceptable for observability that is supposed to be non-critical.

**Recommended fix:**
- `record()` must catch all exceptions, log them, and never raise.
- Consider returning `bool` from `record()` for tests/callers that care.
- Validate metric values at `record()` time: numeric only, finite values only. Reject/ignore bad metrics rather than crashing.

title: Stats plan uses dynamic SQL in upsert expression — replace with parameterized approach
content:
The v2 plan proposes `"metric_value": f"metric_value + {total}"` in the aggregate upsert updateExpressions. This is dynamic numeric SQL concatenation — unnecessary risk that breaks on `nan`/`inf` and could enable injection if the value comes from untrusted input.

**Recommended fix:** Use a provider-supported additive expression mechanism if available, or add one. Never interpolate raw numeric values into SQL strings. Validate that metric values are finite numbers before they enter any SQL path.

title: Stats: one data source default, not split log/agg, until idempotency solved
content:
The v2 plan defaults to separate `stats_log` and `stats_agg` data sources (separate DB files or connections). This creates two problems:

1. No transaction boundary: aggregate upsert and mark-events-processed cannot be committed atomically, creating the double-counting crash risk.
2. `DatabaseManager` caches providers by provider name (`internal/database/manager.py:166-170`), so `stats_log` and `stats_agg` are separate provider instances even when pointing to the same file.

**Recommended fix:** Default to ONE data source named `stats`. Do not offer split log/aggregate databases until idempotent aggregation (with a contribution ledger or transactional batch API) is implemented.

title: Stats: class naming convention — avoid shadowing by renaming DB impl
content:
The v2 plan defines `internal/database/stat_storage.py` with:

```python
from lib.stats.stats_storage import StatsStorage

class StatsStorage(StatsStorage):  # confusing: same name as parent
```

This is confusing and may upset lint/type tooling.

**Recommended fix:** Use distinct names:
```python
from lib.stats.stats_storage import StatsStorage as BaseStatsStorage

class DatabaseStatsStorage(BaseStatsStorage):
```

Import `DatabaseStatsStorage` in `main.py`, not the bare `StatsStorage`.

title: Stats plan uses hardcoded LIMIT instead of provider.applyPagination
content:
The v2 plan hardcodes `LIMIT :limit` inside the claim subquery:

```sql
WHERE processed = 0
  AND (processed_id IS NULL OR claimed_at < :orphanTimeout)
ORDER BY event_time
LIMIT :limit
```

The project rule states "never append LIMIT … OFFSET … yourself" (AGENTS.md §SQL portability). `BaseSQLProvider.applyPagination()` exists for this (`internal/database/providers/base.py:342-357`).

**Recommended fix:** Use `logProvider.applyPagination(innerQuery, limit=limit)` on the inner SELECT, then embed the provider-generated paginated SQL into the subquery.

title: Stats v3: TEXT timestamp columns everywhere (not TIMESTAMP)
content: All timestamp-like columns in stats schemas (`period_start`, `event_time`, `claimed_at`, `created_at`, `updated_at`) are declared as `TEXT NOT NULL` storing ISO 8601 strings. This matches the actual behavior of the provider layer — `convertToSQLite()` in `internal/database/providers/utils.py` already serializes `datetime.datetime` objects to `.isoformat()` strings. Using `TIMESTAMP` in the schema was misleading since the bytes stored are ISO strings, not native datetime types. A `_normalizeDatetime()` helper is used to handle both string and datetime input when reading from DB.

title: Stats v3: best-effort record() with try-except — observability must never break primary flow
content: The `record()` method in the DatabaseStatsStorage wraps the entire insert in try-except, logs errors with `logger.exception()`, and never raises. This is a deliberate design decision for observability infrastructure: stats recording sits on the LLM hot path and must never cause request failures. The ABC documents that implementations _may_ be best-effort. Callers do not need to wrap record() calls themselves. Exceptions that break through (e.g. schema mismatch) are caught and logged at ERROR level.

title: Stats v3: metric_value validation before additive upsert expression
content: The aggregate upsert uses an additive expression: `updateExpressions={"metric_value": "metric_value + :delta"}` with `:delta` as a named parameter. Input values are validated to be finite (not NaN/Inf) before constructing the upsert — done via `_validateMetricValue(val)` which raises `ValueError` on non-finite values. This prevents SQL injection or corruption from NaN/Inf float literals. The v2 plan's `f"metric_value + {total}"` approach is replaced with a parameterized `metric_value + :delta` plus a separate parameters dict.

title: Stats v3: DatabaseStatsStorage chosen class name (resolves shadowing)
content: The v3 plan names the DB-backed implementation `DatabaseStatsStorage` (in `internal/database/stats_storage.py`), resolving the shadowing problem from v2 where `StatsStorage` was subclassed from `StatsStorage`. The ABC in `lib/stats/stats_storage.py` stays named `StatsStorage`. Imports use: `from internal.database.stats_storage import DatabaseStatsStorage`.

title: Stats v3: total period uses 1970-01-01T00:00:00 epoch sentinel
content: The all-time/total aggregate period uses `1970-01-01T00:00:00+00:00` as its fixed `period_start` value and `"total"` as the `period_type`. This is computed alongside hourly/daily/monthly in `_computePeriods()`. The aggregation pass produces two rows per event: one for the real labels (per-consumer) and one with consumer set to `"__global__"` — so global stats are computed in the same single pass as per-consumer stats. `period_start` for hourly/daily/monthly are ISO strings, not naive datetimes.

title: Stats v3: disabled by default (stats.enabled=false) until query API exists
content: The stats feature is disabled by default (`stats.enabled = false` in config). This is intentional: without a query API (getAggregates), a trigger for periodic aggregation, and idempotent aggregation (requires provider transaction API), the feature would collect unbounded raw data with no consumer. The plumbing ships now but requires explicit opt-in via config to activate. Follow-up work tracks: query API, aggregation trigger, and idempotent batch processing.

title: Stats recording architecture: each model records its own stats
content:
The stats recording in `lib/ai/abstract.py` follows the pattern where **each model records its own stats** rather than the orchestrator (`_runWithFallback`) doing it. In each `generateText`/`generateImage`/`generateStructured`:
- In the no-fallback path (fallbackModels=None): the model calls `_generate*()` then `_recordAttemptStats(consumerId, result, generationType)` — the final result.
- In the fallback path: the lambda calls `model.generateText(..., fallbackModels=None)` which enters the no-fallback path of the *next* model, and that model records its own stats. `_runWithFallback` is a pure orchestrator with NO stats recording — no double-counting.

`_recordAttemptStats` no longer has an `isFallback` parameter. It was removed when the user refactored the integration. Error stats are recorded even on exceptions (try/except around `_generate*`). The `statsStorage` is always present on `AbstractModel` (never None — defaults to `NullStatsStorage()` via `LLMManager`), so no None-safe guards are needed in the hot path.

title: LLMService uses injectLLMManager pattern, no parameter threading
content: LLMService no longer receives `llmManager` as a parameter on every `generateText`/`generateStructured`/`generateImage` call. Instead, `main.py` calls `LLMService.getInstance().injectLLMManager(self.llmManager)` once after LLMManager initialization. The service stores it as `self.llmManager` and uses `self.resolveModel()` internally. This eliminates boilerplate parameter threading through the entire call chain.

title: Stats config: no separate stats.db, uses default database
content: The stats storage no longer uses a separate `[database.providers.stats]` / `stats.db` provider. Both `stat_events` and `stat_aggregates` tables live in the default database. Controlled by `llm-stats-data-source = "default"` in `configs/00-defaults/stats.toml` (falls back to `self.database.manager.default` in main.py). This is simpler than maintaining a second SQLite file just for stats tables.

title: statsStorage is required (not Optional) on AbstractModel and AbstractLLMProvider
content: `AbstractModel.__init__` and `AbstractLLMProvider.addModel` both require `statsStorage: StatsStorage` as a mandatory keyword argument — never `Optional[StatsStorage] = None`. `LLMManager` always passes a real `StatsStorage` instance, defaulting to `NullStatsStorage()` if none was provided in config. This means all model/provider subclasses must accept and forward the `statsStorage` parameter, and no None-checking is needed in the stats recording hot path.

title: Bug fix: double-recording in stats fallback path
content:
The original implementation had a double-recording bug in the fallback path. When `_runWithFallback` was called, it recorded stats for each model attempt via `_recordAttemptStats`. But each model's `generate*` method also called `_recordAttemptStats` when invoked via the fallback lambda (because the lambda passed fallbackModels=None, entering the no-fallback stats-recording path). For a 2-model fallback chain, this produced 4 stats records instead of 2.

The fix (done by user after code review): Each model's `generate*` method handles its own stats recording. `_runWithFallback` is a pure orchestrator with NO stats recording. The fallback lambda passes `consumerId` through to each model's `generate*` method, so stats are attributed to the correct consumer. Error exceptions are also captured for stats via try/except around `_generate*` calls.

title: Agent correction: inline imports in main.py violate AGENTS.md
content:
The agent placed conditional imports inside a function body in main.py:
```python
if enabled:
    from internal.database.stats_storage import DatabaseStatsStorage  # WRONG
```

AGENTS.md explicitly forbids this: "Do not add imports inside of methods or functions. All imports must be at the top of the file."

The user corrected this by moving all imports to module level and making the conditional logic use a typed variable (`llmStatsStorage: Optional[StatsStorage] = None`) that gets assigned inside the conditional blocks. The `StatsStorage` type is always imported regardless of the enabled flag.

title: DatabaseStatsStorage uses getProvider() directly, not repository methods (unlike GenericDatabaseCache)
content:
`DatabaseStatsStorage` (`internal/database/stats_storage.py`) does NOT use the repository abstraction layer (no `self.db.repository.*` calls). Instead, every operation obtains a provider directly via `self.db.manager.getProvider(dataSource=self.dataSource, readonly=False)`.

This is a deliberate architectural distinction from `GenericDatabaseCache` (which uses `self.db.repository.*` methods). The stats implementation needs explicit provider control because its operations span multiple queries (claim via UPDATE, select claimed, upsert aggregates, mark processed) that don't cleanly map to any existing repository. The claim query in particular requires raw SQL with `applyPagination()` and controlled subquery nesting.

Constructor: `__init__(self, db: Database, eventType: str, *, dataSource: str)` — follows the same `db: Database` + `__slots__` pattern as `GenericDatabaseCache` but adds `dataSource` as a keyword-only parameter specifying which named data source both `stat_events` and `stat_aggregates` tables live under.

title: consumerId parameter passed to model calls: str(chatId) if not None else None
content: In LLMService (internal/services/llm/service.py), all three model call sites (generateText, generateStructured, generateImage) now pass `consumerId=str(chatId) if chatId is not None else None` to the underlying AbstractModel methods (generateText, generateStructured, generateImage). The chatId was already available as a parameter at all three call sites but was only used for rate limiting and logging. The defensive `if chatId is not None else None` ensures that when chatId is None, consumerId is passed as None rather than the literal string "None". The consumerId is passed through for stats recording in the underlying model.

title: Stats storage wiring in GromozekBot.__init__: order of initialization
content:
Stats storage initialization was inserted into `GromozekBot.__init__` in `main.py` between database init and LLMManager init, with this exact order:

1. `self.database = Database(...)`
2. Stats storage creation (new):
   - If `configManager.getStatsConfig().get("enabled", False)` → `DatabaseStatsStorage(db=self.database, eventType="llm_request", dataSource="stats")`
   - Otherwise → `NullStatsStorage()`
3. `self.llmManager = LLMManager(modelsConfig, statsStorage=llmStatsStorage)` — now receives the stats storage instance

The LLMManager constructor signature was updated to accept a keyword-only `statsStorage` parameter. Conditional imports are used inline to avoid loading `DatabaseStatsStorage` when stats are disabled:

title: Testing technique: circular reference class to trigger JSON serialization errors in stats record()
content:
When testing `DatabaseStatsStorage.record()` error handling (the best-effort never-raises pattern), a self-referencing object can be used to trigger `json.dumps()` failures for labels. The technique:

```python
class UnserializableObject:
    """Object whose repr() creates a self-referencing dict to trigger JSON errors."""
    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        d: dict[str, Any] = {}
        d["self"] = d  # circular reference
        return str(d)
```

Usage in test:
```python
await statsStorage.record(
    {"normal": 1, **dict.fromkeys(["bad1", "bad2"], UnserializableObject())},
    consumerId="chat_error",
)
```

This triggers a `ValueError` inside `json.dumps()` when serializing labels, which `DatabaseStatsStorage.record()` catches with `logger.exception()` and swallows. The test then verifies that non-finite metrics like `float("inf")` and `float("nan")` are also filtered out during aggregation.

Discovered while implementing `testRecordFailureIsNonFatal` in `lib/stats/test/test_sql_storage.py`. Previous attempts to simulate DB errors (e.g., calling a non-existent `provider.close()`) failed because the SQLite provider has no such method. This approach tests the JSON serialization failure path within `record()` itself, which is more realistic.

title: Regression test mandate: every bug fix must include a regression test
content: A hard rule was added to both `AGENTS.md` (under Hard Rules) and `docs/llm/tasks.md` §1.5: every bug fix — whether in production code, test code, config, or docs — must include a regression test that FAILS before the fix and PASSES after. Include edge-case tests that the bug touched (Optional/Union conversions, None handling, schema mismatches, etc.). Never rely on existing test coverage alone to catch regressions. This extends the previous rule that was handler-bug-only to cover ALL bug fixes.

title: sqlToCustomType handles Optional/Union with None passthrough and type unwrapping
content:
`internal/database/utils.py` was updated so `sqlToCustomType()` properly handles Optional and Union types:

1. **None passthrough for Optional**: When `data is None` and the expected type is `Optional[T]` (i.e., `Union[T, None]`), returns `(True, None)` instead of failing.
2. **Union type unwrapping**: When the expected type is a Union (e.g., `Union[int, str]`) and the initial type check fails, the function recursively tries `sqlToCustomType` against each Union member type and returns the first successful conversion.
3. **Non-Optional None rejection**: `None` with a non-Optional type (e.g., bare `int`) still returns `(False, None)` as expected.

Regression tests were added in `tests/database/test_utils.py` in the `TestSqlToCustomTypeOptionalUnion` class (line 391) covering: Optional+None (str/int/datetime), Optional+non-None values (datetime strings, bytes to int), Union conversion (numeric/alpha strings, int, bool bytes), and edge cases (incompatible data, non-Optional None rejection).

title: Test files missing required statsStorage parameter — audit
content:
Concrete audit of test files that do NOT pass the required `statsStorage` parameter to `AbstractModel.__init__()` or `AbstractLLMProvider.addModel()`. These all need `NullStatsStorage()` (from `lib.stats`) added as the parameter value.

1. **`lib/ai/test_stat_integration.py:127`** — `_makeModel()` helper calls `_TestModel(...)` without `statsStorage`. The `_TestModel` itself (line 54) uses `*args, **kwargs` forwarding so the constructor is flexible, but the helper omits the required param.

2. **`tests/lib_ai/model_wrappers.py:57`** — shared golden-test helper `createModel()` calls `provider.addModel(...)` without `statsStorage`.

3. **`tests/lib_ai/golden/test_golden.py`** — multiple calls to `provider.addModel()` at lines 62, 117, 191, 252, all missing `statsStorage`.

4. **`tests/divination/golden/scenario_runner.py:270`** — `self.provider.addModel(...)` missing `statsStorage`.

The fix pattern: pass `statsStorage=NullStatsStorage()` in all these calls. This matches the production pattern where `LLMManager` defaults to `NullStatsStorage()` when no stats storage is configured.

title: Test suite organization for AI library — three distinct suites
content:
The AI library tests are organized into three distinct suites with different purposes:

1. **`lib/ai/providers/test_*.py`** (e.g., `test_basic_openai_provider.py`, `test_openrouter_provider.py`, `test_yc_openai_provider.py`) — **Unit tests** for provider implementation classes. Test provider logic directly.

2. **`lib/ai/test_stat_integration.py`** — **Integration tests** specifically for stats recording functionality on `AbstractModel`.

3. **`tests/lib_ai/golden/`** (with `test_golden.py`, `collect.py`, subdirectories for each provider) — **Golden/master tests** that replay recorded HTTP responses using the `lib/aurumentation` framework (httpx transport patching). Test actual API integration without hitting real APIs.

4. **`tests/divination/golden/`** — **Application-specific** golden tests for the divination feature.

These are different test suites and are found at different paths. Both test suites at #3 and #4 use the same golden-data pattern (recorded HTTP fixtures) but test different layers. All of them were affected by the `statsStorage` requirement change.

title: Provider unit tests (lib/ai/providers/test_*.py) also miss statsStorage on direct model construction
content:
The existing audit (memory 1a91cf4e) covers tests that call `provider.addModel(...)` without `statsStorage`. There's an additional category: the collocated provider unit tests in `lib/ai/providers/test_yc_openai_provider.py`, `test_openrouter_provider.py`, and `test_basic_openai_provider.py` instantiate **model constructors directly** (bypassing `addModel()`) and also omit the required `statsStorage` parameter. These need `statsStorage=NullStatsStorage()` added to their model constructor calls.

Unlike the golden/divination tests which use `provider.addModel()`, these provider unit tests call model `__init__` directly with keyword arguments like `provider=..., modelId=..., modelVersion=..., temperature=..., contextSize=..., openAiClient=...`. The `statsStorage` parameter is required in the model `__init__` signature but is not being passed.

title: `_createModelInstance`/`addModel` now use `*` keyword-only separator — all params after `name` must be keyword
content:
The provider method signatures `_createModelInstance` and `addModel` were changed to include a `*` keyword-only separator right after the first positional `name` parameter (basic_openai_provider.py lines 732-742 and 764-774). This means ALL parameters after `name` must be passed as keyword arguments — `modelId`, `modelVersion`, `temperature`, `contextSize`, `statsStorage`, `extraConfig`. This was a side-effect of the statsStorage refactoring that also changed `_createModelInstance` from positional to keyword-only style.

Consequences for subclasses and tests:
1. **Subclass `_createModelInstance` overrides must also have the `*` marker** in their signature to match the base class, or pyright flags them.
2. **Mock implementations** (e.g. `MockOpenAIProvider._createModelInstance` in `test_basic_openai_provider.py`) must match the base signature exactly — including the `*` marker and all keyword params — or pyright reports `reportIncompatibleMethodOverride`.
3. **All callers** must use keyword syntax: `provider.addModel("name", modelId="...", modelVersion="...", temperature=0.7, contextSize=4096, statsStorage=...)` — not positional calls like `provider.addModel("name", "gpt-4", "1.0", 0.7, 4096)`.

Affected test files that still use positional calls (as of 2026-06-10, pending fix):
- `lib/ai/providers/test_basic_openai_provider.py` — lines 301, 350, 369, 1210
- `lib/ai/providers/test_openrouter_provider.py` — lines 265, 785-786
- `lib/ai/providers/test_yc_openai_provider.py` — lines 354, 905-906
- `MockOpenAIProvider._createModelInstance` at line 73-81 in test_basic_openai_provider.py is missing the `*` marker and `statsStorage` param

title: Mock override must replicate `*` keyword-only marker from base class or pyright errors
content:
When overriding a method in a mock subclass where the base class has a `*` keyword-only separator (e.g., `def _createModelInstance(self, name: str, *, modelId: str, ...)`), the mock's override MUST include the identical `*` keyword-only marker in its own signature. Omitting it — even if Python's runtime duck-typing would allow it — causes pyright to fail with "Expected 1 positional argument" errors on call sites.

Discovered in `test_basic_openai_provider.py` where `MockOpenAIProvider._createModelInstance` omitted the `*` marker and pyright flagged 10+ call sites. Fix: add `*` after `name: str` in the mock override signature to match the base `BasicOpenAIProvider._createModelInstance` exactly.

Same rule applies to any mock subclass overriding a method with keyword-only parameters in the base class — pyright checks the override signature against the base and requires structural parity including keyword-only markers, not just runtime compatibility.

title: # type: ignore[assignment] for deliberate None assignment in tests
content:
When a test deliberately assigns `None` to a non-Optional attribute to verify graceful handling (e.g., `model.statsStorage = None` to test that the code doesn't crash), the approved pattern is to suppress pyright locally with `# type: ignore[assignment]` rather than changing the production type hint to `Optional`. This is more conservative — it keeps the production API strict (no callers should pass None) while still allowing the test to exercise the edge case.

This was applied to `lib/ai/test_stat_integration.py` line 407 where `model.statsStorage = None  # type: ignore[assignment]` tests graceful None handling for `StatsStorage` (a non-Optional type).

title: Test fix pattern: LLMManager DI refactoring breaks calls AND constructor invocations
content:
When refactoring LLMManager from method parameter (passed to `generateTextViaLLM(llmManager=...)`) to dependency injection (`service.injectLLMManager(mockLlmManager)`), tests break in two distinct ways that must both be fixed:

**Fix 1 — Fixture injection**: Add `service.injectLLMManager(mockLlmManager)` right after creating the LLMService singleton, and add `mockLlmManager` as a fixture dependency:
```python
# OLD:
def llmService():
    LLMService._instance = None
    service = LLMService()
    return service

# NEW:
def llmService(mockLlmManager):
    LLMService._instance = None
    service = LLMService()
    service.injectLLMManager(mockLlmManager)
    return service
```

**Fix 2 — Remove parameter from calls**: Remove `llmManager=mockLlmManager` from ALL `generateTextViaLLM()`, `generateStructured()`, and `generateImage()` calls.

**Fix 3 — Check constructor calls too**: This pattern also revealed a pre-existing bug in `tests/bot/test_divination_discovery.py` where a test (`testCacheKeyGenerationConsistency`) was creating `DivinationHandler` without the required `llmManager` parameter — the test was silently broken before. Always scan test constructor calls when doing this refactoring, not just the generate* method calls.

The three files affected were: `tests/test_llm_service.py` (29 occurrences), `tests/integration/test_llm_integration.py` (12 occurrences), `tests/bot/test_divination_discovery.py` (1 occurrence removed + 1 occurrence added to fix broken constructor call).

title: sqlToCustomType Optional[datetime] Union unwrapping gotcha
content: `internal/database/utils.py:sqlToCustomType()` was fixed to correctly handle `Optional[datetime.datetime]`. The function receives the expected type via `Union[datetime, None]` (as produced by type hints). The fix unwraps `Optional[T]` properly: when data is None and the expected type is `Optional[X]`, it returns `(True, None)` instead of attempting to call `X(None)`. For non-None data with an Optional type, it recurses on the inner (non-None) type. This matters because SQLite returns None for nullable timestamp columns, and the old code would crash trying to instantiate `datetime.datetime(None)`.

title: labels_hash column belongs to stat_aggregates, not stat_events
content: The `labels_hash TEXT NOT NULL` column exists only in the `stat_aggregates` table (migration_016 line 125), not in `stat_events`. The `stat_events` table columns are: `event_id`, `event_type`, `event_time`, `data`, `labels`, `processed`, `processed_id`, `claimed_at`, `created_at` (migration_016 lines 99-111). The `DatabaseStatsStorage.record()` method inserts into `stat_events` without `labels_hash`. The `aggregate()` method computes `labels_hash` via `_hashLabels()` and upserts it into `stat_aggregates` for the composite primary key. Manual INSERTs in tests targeting `stat_events` must NOT include `labels_hash`. Confirmed by fix to `testTimestampNormalization` which removed `labels_hash` from the INSERT.

title: statsStorage is required (not Optional) on AbstractModel — tests must not set it to None
content: `AbstractModel.__init__` at `lib/ai/abstract.py:85` requires `statsStorage: StatsStorage` as a mandatory parameter — never `Optional[StatsStorage]`. The field `self.statsStorage` is typed as `StatsStorage`, not `Optional[StatsStorage]`. Setting `model.statsStorage = None` in tests tests an invalid program state that can never occur in production. The `_recordAttemptStats` method (line 702) directly calls `self.statsStorage.record()` without a None guard, so None would crash. The `testStatsStorageNoneDoesNotCrash` test was removed for this reason. Use `NullStatsStorage()` instead when a no-op storage is needed.

title: Test discipline: manual INSERTs must match exact table schemas
content: When writing tests that manually INSERT rows into database tables, the column list and VALUES must exactly match the production schema. A mismatch (e.g., referencing `labels_hash` in a `stat_events` INSERT when that column only exists in `stat_aggregates`) will cause crashes that are hard to debug because the error message mentions the column but the test author may assume the schema includes it. Always verify the actual table DDL in the migration file before writing manual INSERTs in tests.

title: Truncated tool output: agent must re-run the truncated portion, not summarize
content:
When a tool's output is truncated (shown with `...`) and the user explicitly asked for the "COMPLETE output" (e.g. full `make format lint test` results with pass/fail counts), the agent must NOT respond with just a summary of what was cut off. Instead:

1. **Acknowledge the truncation explicitly** to the user.
2. **Re-run the specific command that was cut off** (e.g., re-run just the test portion separately) to get the full, non-truncated output.
3. **Return the complete output** as the user requested.

Failing to do this causes the agent to provide an incomplete response, defeating the user's explicit instruction to see the full output. In this instance, the user ran `make format lint test 2>&1` and asked for the complete output; the tool output was truncated after "🧪 Running all Gromozeka tests, do...", but the agent replied with only "All 1811 tests passed (slowest: 6.01s)" — a summary, not the complete output as requested.

title: sqlToTypedDict has no test coverage in internal/database/test_utils.py
content:
The public function `sqlToTypedDict(data, typedDictClass, *, keepOriginal=False)` from `internal/database/utils.py` (lines 319-374) has **no direct unit tests** — no test file imports or calls it by name. A grep for `sqlToTypedDict` across `tests/**/*.py` returns zero results.

However, `sqlToTypedDict` IS exercised **indirectly** through `sqlToCustomType` in `TestSqlToCustomTypeMetadataDict` (20 test methods, starting at line 1184 of `tests/database/test_utils.py`). When `sqlToCustomType` is called with a dict payload and a TypedDict class, it internally dispatches to `sqlToTypedDict` at lines 220 and 290 of `utils.py`. Every code path in `sqlToTypedDict` (required/optional key validation, type conversion, error raising) is covered by these tests.

The production usage is extensive: `sqlToTypedDict` is called at 24 call sites across repository files (chat_messages, chat_users, spam, media_attachments, chat_info, cache, divinations, chat_summarization, delayed_tasks, stats_storage, ensured_message).

True gap: no standalone targeted test for `sqlToTypedDict` edge cases (e.g. keepOriginal param, Mapping input, TypedDict with total=False). Adding direct tests is low-priority given the existing indirect coverage.

title: sqlToCustomType isinstance short-circuits Union before member iteration
content:
`sqlToCustomType` has an early `isinstance(data, expectedType)` check that runs BEFORE the Union member iteration loop. For Union types, Python evaluates `isinstance(data, Union[T1, T2])` as `isinstance(data, (T1, T2))`, which returns True if data matches ANY member. This causes the function to return the data AS-IS without attempting type conversion via the member iteration loop.

Consequences:
- `sqlToCustomType("123", Union[int, str])` returns `(True, "123")` (string preserved) — the isinstance check catches "123" as a str match before int conversion is attempted
- `sqlToCustomType(42, Union[str, int])` returns `(True, 42)` (int preserved) — isinstance catches 42 as int match

The Union member iteration (recursive calls per member) only runs when data does NOT directly match any Union member, e.g., with bytes data:
- `sqlToCustomType(b"42", Union[str, int])` — b"42" is neither str nor int, so the loop runs: str(b"42")="b'42'" succeeds first and returns (True, "b'42'")

This means the ORDER of Union members matters for conversion: if no member directly matches by isinstance, the first member that can convert the data (via its type constructor) wins. Tests added for this behavior in `TestSqlToCustomTypeOptionalUnion` had to match actual isinstance-preferred semantics, not assumed conversion order.

title: pyright ignore comment must be on same line as the error expression
content:
When using `# pyright: ignore[reportArgumentType]` (or any pyright suppression), the comment MUST be on the same line as the specific expression that triggers the error. If black reformats the code into a multi-line function call, a trailing `# pyright: ignore` comment becomes separated from the error and pyright does not recognize it, still reporting the error.

This was encountered in `internal/database/test_utils.py` when calling `sqlToCustomType(data, Optional[datetime.datetime])`. The original code had:
```python
success, value = sqlToCustomType(
    "2024-06-15T14:30:00", Optional[datetime.datetime]
)  # pyright: ignore[reportArgumentType]
```

After black formatting, the `# pyright: ignore` comment was on a separate line from the `Optional[datetime.datetime]` argument that triggered the error. pyright still flagged `reportArgumentType` because the ignore comment was not on the same line as the error.

The fix was to restructure the call so the type-annotated argument and the ignore comment are on the same line:
```python
success, value = sqlToCustomType(
    "2024-06-15T14:30:00", Optional[datetime.datetime])  # pyright: ignore[reportArgumentType]
```

This is a general pyright behavior: `# pyright: ignore[code]` suppresses on a per-line basis, not per-expression. The comment must be on the literal line that contains the violating expression. Unlike `# type: ignore` (mypy), pyright does not apply the suppression to the entire statement if the comment is on a different line.

title: Provider override hooks: _getBaseUrl, _getModelId, _getExtraParams pattern
content: All OpenAI-compatible providers inherit from BasicOpenAIProvider and override three hooks: 1. **_getBaseUrl()**: CustomOpenAIProvider reads from config["base_url"]; OpenrouterProvider returns "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"; YcOpenaiProvider returns "https://ai.api.cloud.yandex.net/v1" (not llm.api.cloud). 2. **_getModelId()**: YcOpenaiProvider constructs gpt://{folderId}/{modelId}/{modelVersion}; others pass as-is. 3. **_getExtraParams()**: OpenrouterProvider adds HTTP-Referer/X-Title headers; CustomOpenAIProvider and YcOpenaiProvider return empty dicts (YC's max_tokens/stream are commented out). Hooks in basic_openai_provider.py (lines 220, 263, 909).

title: BasicOpenAIModel._generateImage returns only first image, logs warning on multiple
content: In `lib/ai/providers/basic_openai_provider.py`, `BasicOpenAIModel._generateImage()` extracts the base64 image data from `retMessage.images[0]["image_url"]["url"]` and decodes it to bytes. It only returns the **first** image from the response. If the API returns multiple images, a `logger.warning` is emitted but only the first is returned. The returned result carries `mediaMimeType` based on the base64 data URI prefix.

title: CustomOpenAIProvider registered in manager.py but not exported in providers/__init__.py
content: `CustomOpenAIProvider` is registered as a provider class in `lib/ai/manager.py` (mapped to the `"custom_openai"` config type), but it is **not** exported in `lib/ai/providers/__init__.py`. The `__init__.py` exports `YcAIProvider`, `YcOpenaiProvider`, `OpenrouterProvider`, `BasicOpenAIProvider`, and `BasicOpenAIModel` — but not `CustomOpenAIProvider`. This means the class is only importable via the direct path `lib.ai.providers.custom_openai_provider.CustomOpenAIProvider` and is not part of the public provider API surface.

title: LLMManager two-phase init: _initProviders before _initModels with error isolation
content:
`LLMManager.__init__()` (in `lib/ai/manager.py`) performs a two-phase initialization:

1. **`_initProviders()`** — iterates `config["providers"]`, maps each provider `type` string to its class (via a hardcoded dict in `manager.py`), and instantiates the provider. If a provider's `__init__` raises (e.g. bad config), the exception is caught and logged — one bad provider does **not** block initialization of others.

2. **`_initModels()`** — reads `config["models"]`, filters out entries with `enabled: False`, looks up the named provider (from phase 1), and calls `provider.addModel(...)` with the model config. Optionally wires JSON logging if `enableJSONLog` is set in config.

This decoupling ensures provider registration failures don't prevent model setup for working providers.

title: Token estimation in AbstractModel: char-count heuristic, not real tiktoken
content: In `lib/ai/abstract.py`, `AbstractModel` (line ~516) uses a character-count heuristic for token estimation: `len(text) / 3.5 * 1.1`. There is a `tiktokenEncoding` field set to `"o200k_base"` but it is never actually used for real tokenization — no tiktoken encoding/decoding happens anywhere. The heuristic is used for context-size guarding in `generateText` and `generateStructured`. This is a deliberate simplification, not a missing feature.

title: Stats recording in AbstractModel: NullStatsStorage default, lib/stats dependency
content: Every `generate*` call in `AbstractModel` (`lib/ai/abstract.py`, line ~666) records stats via `self.statsStorage.record(...)`. Labels include `modelName`, `provider`, `generationType`, `status`. The `statsStorage` parameter accepts an instance from `lib/stats` (which provides `StatsStorage` interface), and if `None` (not injected), defaults to `NullStatsStorage` — a no-op implementation that never raises. This makes stats recording optional and safe.

title: JSON log files use date-suffix, append mode per session
content: In `AbstractModel.printJSONLog` (`lib/ai/abstract.py`, line ~625), JSON request/response logging appends one JSON line per LLM call to a dated logfile with a `.YYYY-MM-DD` suffix (e.g., `file.2026-06-10`). Multiple sessions share one file per day via append mode. Controlled by `jsonLogFilePath` config on the model. This is separate from the standard Python logging system.

title: "dood!" interjection is a project-wide convention in lib/ai
content: Throughout `lib/ai/`, the interjection "dood!" is consistently used in docstrings, error messages, and comments (e.g., `"Subclasses must implement _get_base_url, dood!"` in `basic_openai_provider.py:667`, `"Structured output isn't implemented by {self.modelId}, dood!"` in `abstract.py`). This is a deliberate stylistic convention of the codebase, not a typo or bug. It appears in error messages, NotImplementedError raises, and some docstrings across `abstract.py`, `basic_openai_provider.py`, and `yc_openai_provider.py`.

title: ModelRunResult.__str__ omits raw result via _OMIT sentinel
content: `ModelRunResult.__str__` (`lib/ai/models.py`, line ~945) uses `__slots__` + a custom `_STR_RENDERERS` dict to produce compact, readable string output. The raw API response object stored in `.result` is deliberately omitted from `__str__` output via the `_OMIT` sentinel — a design choice to keep logs readable by not printing large API response JSON blobs by default.

title: support_tools defaults to False in BasicOpenAIModel
content: In `BasicOpenAIModel.__init__` (`lib/ai/providers/basic_openai_provider.py`, line ~174), `support_tools` defaults to `False`. Tool/function calling is only enabled when explicitly set to `true` in the model's `extraConfig`. This means models must opt-in to tool calling — it is not automatically enabled even for OpenAI-compatible providers that support it.

title: LLMManager graceful init: failed provider/model logs and continues
content: In `LLMManager._initProviders` (`lib/ai/manager.py`), if a provider or model fails to initialize (e.g., bad config, missing dependency, API error), the error is logged and initialization continues rather than raising an exception. This means a broken provider config does not block the rest of the system — other providers and models still load and function normally.

title: Conversation review: scripts/convert_llm_log_to_readable.py switched JSON → YAML for readable multiline strings
content: The `scripts/convert_llm_log_to_readable.py` was originally outputting JSON, but JSON keeps `\n` escaped in strings, making long prompts and responses unreadable. Fixed by switching to YAML output with literal block style (`|-`), which renders multiline content as actual multiline text. Added `PyYAML==6.0.2` to requirements.txt. The script also now accepts both JSON and YAML input files (auto-detected by extension).

title: Bug: scripts/run_llm_debug_query.py couldn't read files after ConfigManager changed CWD
content: The `scripts/run_llm_debug_query.py` initially initialized ConfigManager BEFORE reading the input file. ConfigManager.__init__ calls os.chdir(rootDir) (where rootDir = "storage" from config), changing the working directory. This meant relative file paths passed as CLI arguments couldn't be found. Fixed by reading and parsing the input file BEFORE ConfigManager initialization. This is now a documented pattern for any script that needs to accept a file path from CLI while also using ConfigManager.

title: Bug: scripts/run_llm_debug_query.py only read JSON input, but convert script outputs YAML
content: After `convert_llm_log_to_readable.py` was changed to output YAML (`.readable.yaml`), the `run_llm_debug_query.py` script couldn't read the output — it only parsed JSON. Fixed by adding YAML support with file extension detection: `.json` → JSON parser, `.yaml`/`.yml` → YAML parser. Added `_loadYamlFile()` and refactored to `loadReadableFile()` with auto-detection. Both scripts now work together end-to-end: convert → edit → rerun.

title: Gotcha: flake8 F541 error on f-strings without placeholders
content: The project's flake8 linting enforces F541: `f-string without any placeholders`. Using `f"..."` when no `{...}` interpolation is needed causes a lint failure. Always use plain string literals (`"..."`) instead of f-strings when the string contains no curly-brace expressions. This came up during `scripts/run_llm_debug_query.py` development where `print(f"\\n")` was flagged by flake8 and had to be changed to `print("\\n")`.

title: AbstractModel uses modelId attribute, not getName() method
content: When working with AbstractModel instances (from `lib/ai/abstract.py`), the model identifier is stored as the `modelId` attribute (set in `__init__`), not a `getName()` method. The correct way to reference a model's name is `model.modelId`, NOT `model.getName()`. This was discovered during `scripts/run_llm_debug_query.py` development when pyright flagged `Cannot access attribute "getName" for class "AbstractModel"`.

title: Script pattern: LLM debug query script uses ConfigManager + LLMManager with modelId
content: `scripts/run_llm_debug_query.py` establishes a pattern for LLM debug scripts: parse CLI args first (input file, optional model override, config-dir, dotenv-file), THEN load the input file reading (before ConfigManager initialization since ConfigManager changes CWD), load config via ConfigManager, initialize LLMManager, get the model via `llmManager.getModel(modelName)` where modelName comes from the input file or CLI override, reconstruct ModelMessage objects from JSON (handling tool_calls and tool_call_id), and run `model.generateText(messages)`. The config init uses `configManager.getModelsConfig()` passed to `LLMManager()`. Model override via `--model` CLI flag takes priority over the model field in the input JSON. Uses `--config-dir` and `--dotenv-file` CLI args like other debug scripts.

title: Custom YAML representer for literal block style (`|-`) in LLM log scripts
content:
In `scripts/convert_llm_log_to_readable.py`, a custom YAML string representer is registered to control how multiline strings render. Without this, PyYAML uses `double-quoted` style with escaped `\n`, which defeats readability — the entire point of switching to YAML. The fix overrides `yaml.representer.SafeRepresenter.represent_str` to check `'\n' in data`: if multiline, emit literal block style (`|-`, which strips the trailing newline); otherwise, use the default inline style. This is registered as `yaml.add_representer(str, ...)` on the `SafeDumper` before calling `yaml.dump`.

Key details:
- Uses `yaml.representer.SafeRepresenter.represent_str` as the base (preserves default behavior for non-multiline strings)
- `|-` (strip literal block) is used instead of `|` (clip) because LLM response text typically shouldn't have a trailing newline
- `default_flow_style=False` is passed to `yaml.dump()` for overall block format
- `sort_keys=False` preserves field order (date, status, request, response, model)
- `indent=2` and `width=100` control formatting

This pattern should be used in any script that outputs YAML with user-facing text content where readability matters.

title: YAML-first-then-JSON-fallback parsing pattern for dual-format log files
content:
In `scripts/convert_llm_log_to_readable.py`, the `readLogFile()` function implements a pragmatic dual-format parsing strategy: it attempts YAML parsing first (which handles both single-list YAML and multi-document YAML), then falls back to JSON line-by-line parsing only if YAML fails. This is necessary because the same script must handle both legacy JSON log files and newly-generated YAML files, and it's also useful because YAML is a superset of JSON for many structures.

The specific approach:
1. Read the entire file content
2. Try `yaml.safe_load_all(content)` to handle both single-list (`- item1\n- item2`) and multi-document (`---\nitem1\n---\nitem2`) YAML formats
3. If YAML parsing raises `yaml.YAMLError`, fall back to the original JSON line-by-line parsing (`json.loads(line)` for each non-blank line)
4. This keeps backward compatibility with existing JSON log files while supporting the new YAML format

This pattern is useful for any script that processes LLM logs or similar data that may exist in either JSON or YAML format over a transition period.

title: Correction: scripts/run_llm_debug_query.py shebang changed to system python3 instead of venv python3
content:
In the session implementing YAML support for `scripts/run_llm_debug_query.py`, the shebang was changed from `#!../venv/bin/python3` to `#!/usr/bin/env python3` as an unsolicited change. This is inconsistent with the project convention. The dominant shebang pattern for executable scripts in this repo is `#!/usr/bin/env ./venv/bin/python3` (used by 6+ scripts: `check_image_parsing.py`, `check_tool_calling.py`, `list_models.py`, `check_structured_output.py`, `reproduce_llm_dialog.py`, `sandbox_bootstrap.py`, `aiodocker_demo.py`, `reproduce_layout_extraction.py`), or at minimum `#!../venv/bin/python3` (used by `convert_llm_log_to_readable.py`, `convert_readable_to_llm_log.py`). `#!/usr/bin/env python3` uses the system default Python, bypassing the project venv, which may have different package versions. The shebang should be corrected to `#!/usr/bin/env ./venv/bin/python3` to match the project convention.

Note: This doesn't affect normal usage via `./venv/bin/python3 scripts/run_llm_debug_query.py` (which overrides the shebang), but matters if the script is run directly as an executable.

title: _recordAttemptStats signature: isFallback param removed, status label added
content:
The `_recordAttemptStats` method in `lib/ai/abstract.py` has had its signature simplified:
- **Removed**: The `isFallback: bool` keyword-only parameter was removed from the signature entirely.
- **Added**: A `status` label is now included in the stats recording labels dict.
- **Added**: A `status_{name}` metric key (e.g. `status_success`, `status_failure`, `status_timeout`) is recorded alongside other metrics.
