The first pattern (`[TOOL_CALL_START]`) is a custom markup used by Gromozeka's prompt templates.

title: None content normalization in reconstructMessages — msgDict.get("content") or ""
content:
During code review of the newly created `internal/services/llm/utils.py`, a critical bug was found: `msgDict.get("content", "")` returns `None` when the `"content"` key exists but its value is `null` (this is valid for OpenAI-format assistant messages that only carry `tool_calls`). The default `""` only fires when the key is absent entirely. A `ModelMessage` constructed with `content=None` violates its own `content: str = ""` annotation and crashes downstream code.

Fix: use `msgDict.get("content") or ""` instead, which normalizes both missing and None content to `""`.

Regression test: `testNoneContentDefaultsToEmpty` in `tests/services/llm/test_reconstruct_messages.py` — verifies `{"role": "assistant", "content": None, "tool_calls": [...]}` produces `content == ""`.

title: JSONL silent data loss removed from llmReplayCommand — fail fast on malformed attachments
content:
The initial `/llm_replay` implementation in `DevCommandsHandler.llmReplayCommand` had a JSONL (one-JSON-per-line) fallback parsing loop that silently skipped lines failing `json.loads()`, discarding data without warning to the user. For a debugging/replay tool, silent data corruption is especially harmful.

Fix: removed the JSONL fallback entirely. The code now only handles two valid shapes: `json.loads()` returns a `dict` (used directly) or a `list` (extracts `data[0]`, expects it to be a dict). If the file isn't valid JSON or the shape is unexpected, the error is reported to the user immediately with a clear message. This is a deliberate design decision: fail fast rather than silently corrupt.

Note: the normalization at `dev_commands.py:953-964` does NOT wrap a dict in a list; it passes the dict through directly. For a list, it extracts `data[0]` (first element) rather than using the whole list.

title: Empty toolCalls list not set when all entries are non-function type
content:
In `reconstructMessages` in `internal/services/llm/utils.py`, when a message dict has `tool_calls` entries but all are of non-`function` type (e.g., `"type": "retrieval"`, `"type": "code_interpreter"`), the original code unconditionally set `kwargs["toolCalls"] = toolCalls`, resulting in an empty list being set on the message. This silently discards tool call data and differs semantically from leaving `toolCalls` absent (which defaults to `[]` anyway via `ModelMessage`).

Fix: changed from `kwargs["toolCalls"] = toolCalls` to `if toolCalls: kwargs["toolCalls"] = toolCalls` — only sets the key when at least one function-type tool call was found.

title: Mock test pyright pragma for DevCommandsHandler tests
content:
When writing integration tests for `DevCommandsHandler`, pyright in `basic` mode flags false positives on:
- AsyncMock attributes like `.assert_awaited_once`, `.call_args`, `.await_count`
- Optional member access on `Optional[TheBot]` like `handler._bot.downloadAttachment`
- Decorator-altered call signatures on command methods called directly

Fix: add the following pragma comment at the top of any mock-heavy test file (after the module docstring):
```python
# pyright: reportAttributeAccessIssue=false, reportOptionalMemberAccess=false, reportCallIssue=false
```
This suppresses all three categories for the entire file, which is standard practice for mock-heavy test modules in this project.

title: YAML round-trip trailing newline limitation in convert_readable_to_llm_log.py
content:
The reverse conversion script `scripts/convert_readable_to_llm_log.py` has a known round-trip fidelity limitation: content originally ending with `\\n` (escape sequence) in the JSON log format will lose the trailing newline after a full JSON→YAML→JSON round trip.

Root cause: YAML's `|` (literal block style) uses "clip" chomping by default, which adds exactly one trailing newline. After the forward conversion (in `convert_llm_log_to_readable.py`), the `convertContentBack` in the reverse script strips one trailing newline to undo this YAML artifact. However, it cannot distinguish between "the YAML added a trailing newline" and "the original content actually ended with `\\n`".

This is documented as a known limitation. In practice, LLM log content rarely ends with `\n`, so the risk is low. If complete fidelity is ever needed, one could use `|+` (keep) or `|-` (strip) chomping indicators, or store a metadata flag.

title: DevCommandsHandler: no custom __init__, no tests, inherits all services from BaseBotHandler
content:
DevCommandsHandler (internal/bot/common/handlers/dev_commands.py) does NOT define its own __init__ method. It relies entirely on BaseBotHandler.__init__ (base.py L133-163), which sets up: self.configManager, self.config, self.db, self.botProvider, self.cache (CacheService.getInstance()), self.queueService (QueueService.getInstance()), self.storage (StorageService.getInstance()), self.llmService (LLMService.getInstance()), and self._bot (injected later via injectBot()).

This differs from handlers like WeatherHandler or YandexSearchHandler which have custom __init__ for proxy resolution or additional client initialization.

Tests exist at: tests/bot/common/handlers/test_dev_commands.py (moved from the old collocated internal/bot/common/handlers/ location to the canonical tests/ directory on June 12, 2026). Any new command added here must include tests.

The handler registers with HandlerParallelism.PARALLEL in HandlersManager (handler chain position #6, after SummarizationHandler).

title: Command visibility/availableFor pairing convention: BotOwner vs Public
content:
Bot commands in Gromozeka use two distinct visibility/availableFor pairing conventions, depending on who can execute them:

**BotOwner-restricted commands** (for developer/debug/admin commands):
```python
visibility={CommandPermission.BOT_OWNER},
availableFor={CommandPermission.BOT_OWNER},
```
This makes the command invisible in /help for non-owners and non-executable by non-owners. Used for: `models`, `settings`, `set`/`unset`, `test`, `clear_cache`, `get_admins`, `shutdown`.

**Public commands** (for user-facing features):
```python
visibility={CommandPermission.PRIVATE, CommandPermission.HIDDEN},
availableFor={CommandPermission.DEFAULT},
```
`PRIVATE` means the command appears in private chat /help; `HIDDEN` means it shows in group /help too but is hidden from the auto-generated command list. `DEFAULT` permits all users. Used for: `echo`.

Key: `visibility` controls who sees it in /help listings; `availableFor` controls who can execute it. They are always set together with the same value for BotOwner commands.

title: sendMessage full signature from BaseBotHandler
content:
BaseBotHandler.sendMessage() (base.py L505-527) is the universal message-sending method. Its full signature:

```python
async def sendMessage(
    self,
    replyToMessage: Optional[EnsuredMessage],
    messageText: Optional[str] = None,
    *,
    addMessagePrefix: str = "",
    photoData: Optional[bytes] = None,
    sendMessageKWargs: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None,
    tryMarkdownV2: bool = True,
    tryParseInputJSON: Optional[bool] = None,
    sendErrorIfAny: bool = True,
    skipLogs: bool = False,
    mediaPrompt: Optional[str] = None,
    messageCategory: MessageCategory = MessageCategory.BOT,
    inlineKeyboard: Optional[Sequence[Sequence[CallbackButton]]] = None,
    typingManager: Optional[TypingManager] = None,
    splitIfTooLong: bool = True,
    chatId: Optional[int] = None,
    threadId: Optional[int] = None,
    notify: Optional[bool] = None,
    attachmentList: Optional[Sequence[Tuple[bytes, MessageType, Optional[str]]]] = None,
    toolsHistory: Optional[Sequence[ModelMessage]] = None,
) -> List[EnsuredMessage]:
```

Important conventions:
- `messageCategory=MessageCategory.BOT_COMMAND_REPLY` for command responses
- `messageCategory=MessageCategory.BOT_ERROR` for error messages (see separate memory)
- `skipLogs=True` for noisy test loops (e.g. iterating many times in /test)
- Pass `typingManager=typingManager` from the command method signature to show typing indicator

title: Error reporting pattern: sendMessage with MessageCategory.BOT_ERROR
content:
All bot commands report errors back to the chat using a consistent pattern:

```python
await self.sendMessage(
    ensuredMessage,
    messageText="Error description here",
    messageCategory=MessageCategory.BOT_ERROR,
)
```

This pattern is used uniformly across DevCommandsHandler for:
- Validation failures (missing args, invalid input)
- Semantic errors (e.g., "Unknown key: `foo`")
- Missing dependencies (e.g., "No replied message found")
- Parsing errors (e.g., "Invalid iterations count: ...")

The decorator `replyErrorOnException=True` (default on commandHandlerV2) acts as a safety net for unhandled exceptions, but explicit error messages via BOT_ERROR are the standard pattern for expected failure modes.

Don't use BOT_ERROR for success responses — use BOT_COMMAND_REPLY for normal command output.

title: Reply-to message and attachment handling patterns for bot commands
content:
When a command needs to process a message that the user replied to (e.g., analyze a photo, inspect entities), use this two-step check pattern:

**Step 1: Check if the command message is a reply**
```python
if ensuredMessage.replyId is None:
    await self.sendMessage(ensuredMessage, messageText="Must reply to a message", messageCategory=MessageCategory.BOT_ERROR)
    return
```

**Step 2: Get the replied-to message**
```python
repliedMessage = ensuredMessage.getEnsuredRepliedToMessage()
if repliedMessage is None:
    await self.sendMessage(ensuredMessage, messageText="No replied message found", messageCategory=MessageCategory.BOT_ERROR)
    return
```

**For accessing raw platform message attributes** (Telegram entities, Max markup):
```python
message = ensuredMessage.getBaseMessage()
if isinstance(message, telegram.Message) and message.reply_to_message:
    # Access telegram.Message.entities, .text, etc.
elif isinstance(message, maxModels.Message) and message.link:
    # Access max models via message.link.message
```

**For downloading file attachments** (from media.py analyze_command):
Branch on self.botProvider:

**Telegram path:**
```python
if self.botProvider == BotProvider.TELEGRAM and isinstance(parentMessage, telegram.Message) and self._bot is not None:
    fileId = parentMessage.photo[-1].file_id  # or .sticker.file_id, etc.
    mediaData = await self._bot.downloadAttachment(mediaId="", fileId=fileId)
```

**Max path:**
```python
elif self.botProvider == BotProvider.MAX and isinstance(parentMessage, maxModels.Message) and self._bot is not None:
    for attachment in parentMessage.body.attachments:
        if isinstance(attachment, maxModels.PhotoAttachment):
            mediaData = await self._bot.downloadAttachment(mediaId=attachment.payload.token, fileId=attachment.payload.url)
```

downloadAttachment signature: `async def downloadAttachment(self, mediaId: str, fileId: str) -> Optional[bytes]` — returns None on failure.

title: LLM imports needed when adding LLM calls to DevCommandsHandler
content:
DevCommandsHandler currently does NOT import or call any LLM types. Its imports (at the top of dev_commands.py) include: asyncio, logging, sys, time, typing.Optional, telegram, lib.max_bot.models, lib.utils, UpdateObjectType, TypingManager, and from internal.bot.models: BotProvider, ChatSettingsKey, ChatSettingsValue, ChatType, CommandCategory, CommandHandlerOrder, CommandPermission, EnsuredMessage, OutputFormat, commandHandlerV2, MessageRecipient, MessageCategory, CacheNamespace, BaseBotHandler.

If adding an LLM generation call to a DevCommandsHandler command, the required additional imports are:
```python
from lib.ai import ModelMessage, ModelResultStatus
# Optional: from lib.ai import ModelImageMessage  # if processing images
```

The simpler method `self.llmService.generateText()` is preferred for dev commands (as opposed to `self.llmService.generateTextViaLLM()` which is for tool-enabled calls with callbacks and condensing). Use pattern from media.py:
```python
reqMessages = [ModelMessage(role="system", content=prompt)]
llmRet = await self.llmService.generateText(
    reqMessages,
    chatId=ensuredMessage.recipient.id,
    chatSettings=chatSettings,
    modelKey=<ChatSettingsKey or AbstractModel>,
    fallbackKey=<ChatSettingsKey or AbstractModel>,
)
if llmRet.status != ModelResultStatus.FINAL:
    await self.sendMessage(ensuredMessage, messageText=f"Error: {llmRet.error}", messageCategory=MessageCategory.BOT_ERROR)
    return
resultText = llmRet.resultText
```

title: convertContentBack algorithm for YAML→JSON reverse conversion
content:
In `scripts/convert_readable_to_llm_log.py`, the `convertContentBack()` function reverses the forward transformation by:

1. **Strip one trailing `\n`** — YAML literal block style (`|`) with default "clip" chomping always appends a trailing newline that was NOT in the original data. Strip exactly one if `content.endswith("\n")`. This is the inverse of the forward script's YAML serialization behavior.

2. **Replace remaining actual newlines with `\\n` escape sequences** — `content.replace("\n", "\\n")` converts all remaining actual newlines back to the literal `\n` escape sequences used in the JSON log format.

This reverses the forward `convertMessageContent()` which does `content.replace("\\n", "\n")` (literal backslash-n → actual newline).

The algorithm correctly handles these cases:
- **No newlines in content**: YAML doesn't use block style, no trailing newline added, nothing to strip, nothing to escape → passes through unchanged.
- **Multiline content without trailing `\n`**: YAML block style adds one trailing `\n`, strip it, then escape remaining newlines → correct round-trip.
- **Multiline content with trailing `\\n` (original)**: Forward converts to actual `\n`, YAML adds another `\n` → content ends with `\n\n`. Strip one `\n` → leaves the intentional one → escape it → correct round-trip.

Applied to both `content` (inside request messages) and `response` fields. Other fields (`raw`, `date`, `status`, `model`, `provider`, `tool_calls`, `tool_call_id`) are preserved unchanged since the forward script doesn't process them either.

title: LLM log conversion scripts: mirroring convention and input format asymmetry
content:
The two LLM log conversion scripts are designed as mirror images:

- `scripts/convert_llm_log_to_readable.py` (JSON/YAML → readable YAML) — forward direction
- `scripts/convert_readable_to_llm_log.py` (readable YAML → JSON) — reverse direction

**Mirroring convention:**
- `convertContent()` / `convertContentBack()` — paired string transformers
- `processMessage()` / `processMessageBack()` — per-message processing
- `processLogEntry()` / `processLogEntryBack()` — per-entry processing
- Identical argparse pattern: `buildParser()` → `main()` → `sys.exit(main())`
- Same error handling: `FileNotFoundError`, `yaml.YAMLError`, generic `Exception` → print message, return 1
- Output path derivation: forward replaces `.json` with `.readable.yaml`; reverse replaces `.readable.yaml` with `.json`

**Input format asymmetry:**
- Forward script (`convert_llm_log_to_readable.py`) accepts both JSON (one-JSON-per-line) and YAML input — uses YAML-first-then-JSON-fallback with file extension detection.
- Reverse script (`convert_readable_to_llm_log.py`) reads **YAML only** — this is intentional because the readable format is always YAML. It uses `yaml.safe_load_all()` for multi-document YAML support.

**Output formats:**
- Default: one JSON object per line (`json.dumps(entry)` + `\n` for each entry) — matches the original append-only log format.
- `--pretty`: formatted JSON array (`json.dumps(entries, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)`).

**Round-trip verification method:** A full JSON → YAML → JSON round trip (pipe output of forward script into reverse script) should produce byte-identical JSON. This is the standard verification approach for these scripts.

title: Gotcha: self._bot Optional type triggers pyright error on downloadAttachment — guard with if self._bot is not None
content:
When calling `self._bot.downloadAttachment(...)` in a bot handler, pyright reports `"downloadAttachment" is not a known attribute of "None"` because `self._bot` has type `Optional[TheBot]` (the `TheBot.__init__` sets `self._bot: Optional[TheBot] = None` and it's populated later).

The fix is to add a guard before the call:

```python
if self._bot is not None:
    jsonBytes = await self._bot.downloadAttachment(mediaId="", fileId=fileId)
else:
    await self.sendMessage(ensuredMessage, messageText="Bot not initialized", messageCategory=MessageCategory.BOT_ERROR)
    return
```

This pattern is used in `internal/bot/common/handlers/media.py` (around line 452-475) and was needed for the `/llm_replay` command in `dev_commands.py`. A bare `self._bot.downloadAttachment(...)` without the guard causes a `make lint` pyright failure even though at runtime `self._bot` is always set (the bot initializes it before handlers are called).

title: Gotcha: ExtraDataDict total=False causes pyright error on direct bracket access — use .get() with isinstance check
content:
When accessing fields from `ExtraDataDict` (defined in `internal/services/llm/models.py` as a `TypedDict(total=False)`), using bracket notation like `extraData["typingManager"]` causes pyright error: `"Could not access item in TypedDict "typingManager" is not a required key in "ExtraDataDict", so access may result in runtime exception`.

The fix is to use `.get()` with an `isinstance` check:

```python
# Instead of:
extraData["typingManager"].addTimeout(120)

# Use:
typingMgr = extraData.get("typingManager")
if isinstance(typingMgr, TypingManager):
    typingMgr.addTimeout(120)
    await typingMgr.sendTypingAction()
```

This pattern was needed in the `/llm_replay` command's `processIntermediateMessages` callback in `dev_commands.py`. The `.get()`/`isinstance` approach satisfies pyright because `isinstance` narrows the type from `Any` to the concrete `TypingManager` type.

title: internal/services/llm/__init__.py heavy import chain prevents collocated tests
content:
File `internal/services/llm/__init__.py` imports `from .service import LLMService, LLMToolHandler`, which triggers a deep import chain: `service.py` → `internal.bot.models.chat_settings` → more heavy dependencies. When pytest discovers any test file under `internal/services/llm/` (collocated), it tries to import the package's `__init__.py`, which triggers this chain and fails with `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'internal'` because the import chain can't resolve at test collection time.

This means collocated tests under `internal/services/llm/` are not viable. Tests for `internal/services/llm/utils.py` (or any module under this package) MUST be placed under `tests/services/llm/` instead — matching the project convention of stripping `internal/` from the source path.

The fix applied was to create `tests/services/llm/test_reconstruct_messages.py` (mirroring `internal/services/llm/utils.py`) rather than placing `test_utils.py` in the same directory as the source.

title: Mocking pattern: EnsuredMessage with MessageType.DOCUMENT for attachment tests
content:
When testing bot commands that process document attachments (e.g., `/llm_replay` which needs a JSON file), the `EnsuredMessage` must be constructed or mocked with specific setup:

1. **Set `messageType=MessageType.DOCUMENT`** on the `EnsuredMessage` so the handler recognizes it as a document.
2. **Mock `getBaseMessage()`** to return a mock object with a `.document.file_id` attribute for the media ID path. For Telegram, this is a `Mock(spec=telegram.Message)` with `.document = Mock(file_id="mock_file_id")`.
3. **For reply-to-document resolution**: Set `isReply=True` (or `replyId` is not None) on the `EnsuredMessage`, and mock `getEnsuredRepliedToMessage()` to return another `EnsuredMessage` with `messageType=MessageType.DOCUMENT`.
4. **Mock `self._bot.downloadAttachment(fileUniqueId, fileId)`** on the bot to return `Optional[bytes]` (the JSON content for success cases, `None` for error cases).

Example from `internal/bot/common/handlers/test_dev_commands.py`:
```python
baseMsg = Mock()
baseMsg.document = Mock(file_id="file123", file_unique_id="unique123")
ensuredMessage = Mock(spec=EnsuredMessage)
ensuredMessage.messageType = MessageType.DOCUMENT
ensuredMessage.getBaseMessage.return_value = baseMsg
ensuredMessage.isReply = False
ensuredMessage.replyId = None
```

For reply-to-document case:
```python
repliedMsg = Mock(spec=EnsuredMessage)
repliedMsg.messageType = MessageType.DOCUMENT
ensuredMessage.isReply = True
ensuredMessage.replyId = 42
ensuredMessage.getEnsuredRepliedToMessage.return_value = repliedMsg
```

title: mockLlmService fixture pattern for handler tests: LLMManager chain + AsyncMock
content:
When writing tests for bot handlers that call `self.llmService`, the `mockLlmService` fixture must configure several collaborating objects in a specific chain:

```python
service = Mock(spec=LLMService)
manager = Mock()
mockModel = Mock(spec=AbstractModel)
manager.getModel = Mock(return_value=mockModel)
manager.listModels = Mock(return_value=["model-a", "model-b"])
service.getLLMManager = Mock(return_value=manager)
service.generateTextViaLLM = AsyncMock(return_value=modelRunResult)
```

Key details:
- `Mock(spec=LLMService)` ensures pyright type-checking works on the mock.
- `getLLMManager()` returns a plain `Mock` (no spec needed) since `LLMManager` has many methods.
- `getModel()` returns `Mock(spec=AbstractModel)` so the handler can call `.modelKey` on the result.
- `generateTextViaLLM` must be `AsyncMock` since it's an async method.
- The returned `ModelRunResult` must be constructed with the expected fields: `status`, `resultText`, `toolCalls`, `inputTokens`, `outputTokens`, `totalTokens`, `elapsedTime`.

To inject the mock into the handler, use `mock.patch.object(LLMService, 'getInstance', return_value=mockLlmService)` before constructing the handler, since `BaseBotHandler.__init__` calls `LLMService.getInstance()`.

title: Test fixture singleton bypass: direct assignment after `with patch` block leaves getInstance unpatched
content:
When writing handler tests that mock `LLMService` (or any singleton), a fragile pattern is to patch `getInstance` only during `__init__` and then assign the mock directly to the handler:

```python
with patch.object(LLMService, "getInstance", return_value=mockLlmService):
    h = DevCommandsHandler(bot, config, userId, chatSettingsManager=MagicMock())
h.llmService = mockLlmService  # workaround after patch is released
```

This is fragile because:
1. The `with` block ends before the fixture returns, restoring `getInstance` to its original behavior.
2. The direct `h.llmService = mockLlmService` reassignment works because `BaseBotHandler.__init__` stores `self.llmService = LLMService.getInstance()` (so the instance is cached on the handler).
3. But any code path that calls `LLMService.getInstance()` after the `with` block (e.g., inside a helper method called by the handler) would get a REAL (unpatched) instance, causing test failures that are hard to debug.

**Correct pattern**: Keep the `patch.object` active for the entire test scope. Either:
- Wrap the entire test body (not just `__init__`) in the `with` block, or
- Use a `@patch` decorator on the test function, or  
- Ensure the mock is set up as an autouse fixture that resets `_instance = None` so the patched `getInstance` is always called.

Alternatively, rely on the existing autouse fixture pattern from `tests/conftest.py` that resets singleton `_instance = None`, but the patched `getInstance` must remain active when the handler or its dependencies call it.

title: Tool-call matchers use shared helpers: _tryApplyToolCallMatch + _tryParseJson
content:
In `LLMService.generateTextViaLLM()` (`internal/services/llm/service.py`), three private methods (`_matchTextForJSONToolCall`, `_matchTextForToolCallStart`, `_matchTextForToolCallSquareBracketsAndJson`) detect tool calls embedded in LLM response text. They tried in sequence at lines 487-492, in order: JSON-fence → TOOL_CALL_START → square-bracket.

All three delegate to two shared helpers:
1. **`_tryApplyToolCallMatch(mlRunResult, *, toolName, parameters, toolCallId, prefixStr, suffixStr) -> bool`** (line 152): Centralized validation and in-place mutation. Only accepts when (a) prefix or suffix is empty (tool call at start or end of response), (b) toolName is registered in `self.toolsHandlers`, (c) parameters is a dict. On success, sets `status=TOOL_CALLS` and populates `toolCalls`.
2. **`_tryParseJson(data) -> Tuple[Any, int]`** (line 201): Wraps `json.JSONDecoder().raw_decode()` with a fallback that fixes escaped single quotes (`\\'` → `'`) before retrying. Returns both parsed object and end-position index.

All three matchers use greedy `{.*}` in their regex patterns, relying on `_tryParseJson`'s `raw_decode` to handle nested JSON (unlike the old non-greedy `{.*?}` which broke on nested objects). The greedy `{.*}` combined with `raw_decode` correctly extracts only the first JSON object, with trailing text preserved as suffix.

Key invariants:
- Tool call must appear at BEGINNING or END of response (prefix or suffix must be empty)
- Tool name must be in `self.toolsHandlers` to accept the match
- Invalid JSON causes a log warning and returns `False` (silent skip, not an error)

Downstream execution (line 510-518) also defensively checks `toolCall.name in self.toolsHandlers`, returning an error tool result if a tool call somehow passes the matchers with an unregistered name.

title: No unit tests for text-to-tool-call conversion paths in LLMService
content:
Of the five private methods that convert LLM response text to tool calls in `internal/services/llm/service.py`, only `_tryParseJson` has unit test coverage: 19 tests in `tests/services/llm/test_try_parse_json.py` covering valid JSON, invalid JSON, escaped single-quote fallback, end-position correctness, etc.

The other four methods (`_matchTextForJSONToolCall`, `_matchTextForToolCallStart`, `_matchTextForToolCallSquareBracketsAndJson`, `_tryApplyToolCallMatch`) have NO unit test coverage. Existing tests in `test_llm_service.py` and `test_llm_integration.py` only exercise tool calls that arrive with `status=TOOL_CALLS` from mocked models; they never test the text-to-tool-call regex conversion paths.

Test gaps include:
- Nested JSON parameters (the old `{.*?}` regex bug)
- `_tryApplyToolCallMatch` with unregistered tool name (should return False)
- Non-empty prefix AND non-empty suffix (should return False)
- `raw_decode` suffix computation (trailing text preserved in resultText)
- All three matchers with edge cases (empty tool name, non-dict parameters, invalid JSON)

title: Inconsistent .strip() in suffix computation across tool-call matchers
content:
In `internal/services/llm/service.py`, the three tool-call matchers compute the suffix differently after `_tryParseJson`:

1. `_matchTextForJSONToolCall` (line 261): `suffixStr = jsonStr[endPos:] + match.group(3)` — no `.strip()`
2. `_matchTextForToolCallStart` (line 307): `suffixStr = toolArgsStr[endPos:].strip() + match.group(4)` — with `.strip()`
3. `_matchTextForToolCallSquareBracketsAndJson` (line 351): `suffixStr = toolArgsStr[endPos:].strip() + match.group(4)` — with `.strip()`

The inconsistency between #1 and #2/#3 means the JSON-fence method may retain trailing whitespace/newlines from the raw_decode remainder in its suffix. In practice this is harmless because `_tryApplyToolCallMatch` applies a final `.strip()` to the combined `(prefixStr + suffixStr)`, but the inconsistency should be fixed for correctness and consistency.

title: Tool-call matcher cascade no longer short-circuits on JSON-in-backticks alone — hasMatch semantic change
content:
During the extraction of inline tool-call detection into three separate methods in `LLMService.generateTextViaLLM()` (`internal/services/llm/service.py`), the behavior of `hasMatch` changed subtly but intentionally.

**Old behavior (inline code):** When the JSON-backtick regex matched, `hasMatch` was set to `True` immediately — before trying to parse the JSON or validating it against `self.toolsHandlers`. This meant any backtick-wrapped JSON in LLM output prevented the `[TOOL_CALL_START]` pattern from being checked, even if the JSON wasn't a valid tool call.

**New behavior:** Each matcher returns `True` only when the full validation pipeline succeeds (greedy `{.*}` regex AND `_tryParseJson` AND `_tryApplyToolCallMatch` which checks `toolName in self.toolsHandlers`). If a pattern matches but fails validation, subsequent matchers are tried. Cascade order (lines 487-492): JSON-fence → TOOL_CALL_START → square-bracket.

**Impact:** This is strictly more correct — backtick-wrapped JSON that isn't a valid tool call no longer blocks `[TOOL_CALL_START]` detection. However, it could surface new tool-call matches in edge cases where an LLM outputs both unrelated JSON in code fences AND a `[TOOL_CALL_START]` markup in the same message. The old code would have missed the tool call silently; the new code detects it.

title: Behavioral change: TOOL_CALL_START matcher now gates on toolName in self.toolsHandlers
content:
In the refactoring of tool-call detection in `LLMService.generateTextViaLLM()` (`internal/services/llm/service.py`), the extracted `_matchTextForToolCallStart` method (and ALL three matchers) delegate to the shared helper `_tryApplyToolCallMatch` which checks `toolName in self.toolsHandlers` (line 190) — a guard that was NOT present in the old inline code.

Old behavior: Any `[TOOL_CALL_START]toolname{...}` block was converted to a tool call regardless of whether `toolname` was registered. If unregistered, execution would later fail with "Tool X not found".

New behavior: If `toolname` is not in `self.toolsHandlers`, the matcher returns False and the text is left as-is in the LLM response (shown to the user as raw text). This guard applies to ALL THREE matchers (JSON-fence, TOOL_CALL_START, square-bracket) via the shared `_tryApplyToolCallMatch` at `service.py:186-190`, not just TOOL_CALL_START specifically. This is an intentional improvement that brings consistency with the JSON-fence pattern (which already had this guard), but changes error handling behavior for unregistered tool names — previously an error message, now silent passthrough.

title: Removed TODO about parameters/arguments requirement — design intent lost in refactoring
content: During the refactoring of tool-call text matching in `LLMService.generateTextViaLLM()` (`internal/services/llm/service.py`), the old inline code in `_matchTextForJSONToolCall` contained `# TODO: is "parameters"|"arguments" required?`. This comment was removed when the code was extracted into `_tryApplyToolCallMatch`. The resolution is encoded in the logic: `isinstance(parameters, dict)` on the first line of the shared helper effectively makes dict-typed parameters required (non-dict and None are rejected, returning False). Future readers lose this design rationale. Consider adding a comment near the `isinstance` check to preserve the design intent.

title: Type annotation gotcha: _tryApplyToolCallMatch parameters param is over-narrow as Optional[Dict]
content:
In `LLMService._tryApplyToolCallMatch()` (`internal/services/llm/service.py`), the `parameters` parameter is annotated as `Optional[Dict[Any, Any]]`, but this is over-narrow. Two of the three callers pass the result of `json.JSONDecoder().raw_decode()`, which can return any JSON value (list, string, number, bool, None) — not just `Dict | None`. The first caller (`_matchTextForJSONToolCall`) can also pass a non-dict value from `jsonData.get("arguments")` or `jsonData.get("parameters")` if the LLM returns a non-dict value for that key.

The runtime `isinstance(parameters, dict)` guard correctly handles non-dict values, so there is no runtime bug. However, the annotation misleads readers and future type-checking passes. The correct annotation should be `Any` since the method validates the type itself.

Suggested fix:
```python
def _tryApplyToolCallMatch(
    self,
    mlRunResult: ModelRunResult,
    *,
    toolName: str,
    parameters: Any,  # validated by isinstance() inside
    toolCallId: Optional[str],
    prefixStr: str,
    suffixStr: str,
) -> bool:
```

title: README scope: keep compact (~100-120 lines), link to detailed docs
content: The README.md was compacted from 847 lines to 103 lines by deliberate decision. The README should be a lightweight overview that can be read in 90 seconds. Essential content only: project one-liner, requirements (4 lines), feature bullets (~14 one-liners), 4-step quick start, minimal config snippet (~10 lines), key run commands (4 examples), key commands table (6 entries max with "see /help for the rest"), dev commands, troubleshooting, contributing, and license. Everything else — architecture diagrams, full project tree (~260 lines), full handler list, full command list (~50 lines), detailed config sections (~100 lines), migration guide, testing structure — belongs in `docs/developer-guide.md` or `docs/llm/`. Rationale: 847-line README was unreadable, forced users to scroll past content they didn't need, and duplicated information better kept in detailed docs.

title: README_BOT.md deleted: obsolete prototype docs should be removed, not rewritten
content: `README_BOT.md` was a "minimal Telegram bot" prototype description referencing files and methods that no longer exist (`database.py`, `test_bot.py`, `setup_handlers()`, `handle_message()`). The decision was to delete it entirely rather than rewrite it, because the comprehensive README.md already covers everything a rewrite would contain. A condensed duplicate would create a permanent maintenance/sync burden with no reader benefit. When a documentation file describes an old architecture that no longer exists and provides no unique value over current docs, deletion is the right approach.

title: Config env var for bot token is ${BOT_TOKEN}, not ${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}
content: The default config at `configs/00-defaults/00-config.toml` uses `token = "${BOT_TOKEN}"` as the bot token placeholder. When documenting config examples (e.g., in README.md), the env var name must be `${BOT_TOKEN}` — using `${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}` is wrong and will cause the bot to fail to start with an empty token. This applies regardless of platform (Telegram or Max Messenger use the same env var).

title: keepConnection in database config defaults to true, not false
content: The `database.providers.default.parameters.keepConnection` setting in `configs/00-defaults/00-config.toml` defaults to `true` (connect on creation and keep connection open). When documenting default config values (e.g., in README examples), using `keepConnection = false` is incorrect and misleads about connection behavior. The `keepConnection = true` default has meaningful consequences for connection timing and in-memory DB data retention.

title: Emojis persist in several non-archive docs violating AGENTS.md no-emoji policy
content:
Despite the AGENTS.md policy ("No emojis unless explicitly requested"), emojis persist in several non-archive documentation files. Known locations (confirmed June 2026):

1. `docs/developer-guide.md:2473` — 🐧 (penguin emoji)
2. `docs/database-README.md` — 11+ emoji-laden section headers (📚🗂️🔍🔌🚀📖🔗🛠️📊🤝📝)
3. `docs/templates/pr-template.md` — Emojis in section headers (🐛🚀🔧📝🧪🔄)
4. `docs/templates/task-report-template.md` — 🔄 emoji
5. `docs/design/golden-data-testing-system-v2.md:1837` — 🎉
6. `docs/design/lib-cache-design-v0.md:558` — 🐧
7. `docs/design/geocode-maps-client-design-v1.md:1184` — 🎉
8. `docs/suggestions/refactoring.md:1401` — 🔄
9. `docs/suggestions/improvements.md:1037,1042` — 👁🚀
10. `docs/custom-modules-design.md:437` — 👋 in example string
11. Archive files also have emojis but are intentionally left as historical records.

These are lower-priority cleanup items — the no-emoji rule applies primarily to AGENTS.md, README.md, and llm/ agent docs (already clean). Fixing emojis in design docs, suggestions, and templates is optional.

title: Documentation drift detection workflow: explore → compare → fix → review → test
content:
The effective workflow used for the large-scale README overhaul (June 2026) was:

1. **Read baseline** — Read current README.md and key project docs (AGENTS.md, developer-guide.md, architecture docs, main.py entry point)
2. **Deep project exploration** — Explore full directory structure, read key source files (main.py, config system, handlers, services, database, libraries). Document everything found.
3. **Compare against documentation claims** — Catalog each README claim vs reality: class names, architecture, commands, features, test counts, config paths, licenses.
4. **Delegate targeted fixes** — Dispatch parallel tasks: one for the README rewrite (with detailed drift catalog as context), one for ancillary file cleanup (e.g., deleting README_BOT.md, fixing AGENTS.md references, fixing docs with stale class names).
5. **Code review** — Review all diffs for accuracy before committing. Critical issues found at this stage (e.g., wrong env var name, wrong config default).
6. **Quality gates** — Run `make format lint test` after every change batch.
7. **Narrow scope if needed** — If the result is still too large (847-line README), further compact based on user feedback.

This workflow is reusable for any large-scale documentation sync effort.

title: License is BSD 3-Clause, not MIT — README was wrong
content: The project's LICENSE file at repo root is BSD 3-Clause (Copyright (c) 2025, NotA Company). The README.md historically stated "MIT" — this was incorrect. When documenting the project, always refer to the license as BSD 3-Clause. Verified by reading `LICENSE` file directly.

title: README formatting specifics: no emojis, bold, link-to-docs pattern
content:
Compressing README.md from 847 to 103 lines confirmed these specific formatting rules:

1. **No emojis anywhere** in README — strictly banned.
2. **Bold** for feature name prefixes (e.g. `**Multi-provider LLM**: description`), not overused otherwise.
3. **Every verbose section gets a link** to `docs/developer-guide.md` or `docs/llm/index.md` — architecture diagrams, project tree (~260 lines), full handler list, migration guide, testing structure, detailed config sections.
4. **Feature list format**: exactly one line per feature with key name bolded, no sub-headers or paragraphs. ~10-14 items.
5. **Quick Start**: exactly 4 steps — `git clone`, `make install`, configure `.env` (link to config docs), `./run.sh`.
6. **Key commands table**: exactly 5-6 entries (`/start`, `/help`, `/configure`, `/taro`, `/weather`, `/search`) with "See `/help` in-chat for the full command list." note.
7. **Minimal config snippet**: ~6 lines showing only `bot.token`, `bot.mode`, `database.path`, `logging.level` — link to `docs/llm/configuration.md` for full docs.
8. **Troubleshooting**: one-liner only — "Check logs in `logs/`, verify your `.env` file, and see `docs/developer-guide.md`."
9. **Contributing**: one-liner only — "PRs welcome. Run `make format lint test` before submitting."
10. **Acknowledgments**: one line, no bullets.
11. **Requirements section**: 4 lines (Python 3.12+, SQLite3, libmagic, bot token).
12. **Commits/lint/test**: `make format lint` before committing, `make test` after any change.

title: Sandbox v1 architecture: three-axis split (Backend/Runtime/Manager Protocols)
content:
The sandbox system is designed with three orthogonal protocols:

1. **Backend protocol** (`Backend`) — containerization/execution backend. Only Docker in v0 (`BackendName.DOCKER`), but the protocol is designed upfront so Firecracker or other backends can be added later without changing callers.
2. **Runtime protocol** (`Runtime`) — language-specific execution environment. Only Python in v0 (`RuntimeName.PYTHON`), but TypeScript/Bash/Ruby can be added later via the same interface.
3. **Manager** (`SandboxManager`) — singleton that orchestrates Backend + Runtime. Owns the session lifecycle, FIFO queue, MetadataStore, and library pool.

Key conventions from this design: RuntimeName and BackendName are StrEnum types (from `lib/sandbox/enums.py`), used everywhere instead of raw strings. `RuntimeName.PYTHON = "python"`, `BackendName.DOCKER = "docker"`. Container and install image names are derived from these enums.

title: Sandbox v1: Libraries are runtime-scoped, admin-managed, RO-mounted
content:
Libraries in the sandbox system are NOT per-session or per-run. They are per-runtime (e.g., all Python sessions share the same library pool). The library pool lives at `${rootDir}/runtimes/<runtimeName>/libs/` on the host and is mounted RO into every session container.

Key design points:
- The install container is the only process that writes to the lib pool (protected by `fcntl.flock`)
- `runCode()` never installs packages — if `requiredPackages` are absent, it raises `MissingDependenciesError` without starting anything
- Admin API: `installRuntimeLibraries()`, `removeRuntimeLibraries()`, `listRuntimeLibraries()`
- After every install/remove, `pip freeze` output is captured as a snapshot in the metadata store for migration/audit purposes
- No package deny-list — admin responsibility to not install dangerous packages
- Bootstrap via `scripts/sandbox-bootstrap.py`, driven from `sandbox.bootstrap.starter_packages` TOML config key
- Alpine base image for both run and install containers. Only `build-base` is included from the heavy compile toolchain in the install image (Dockerfile.install: `python3 py3-pip py3-wheel py3-setuptools build-base git libmagic sqlite tzdata`). Packages like `gfortran`, `openblas-dev`, `openssl-dev`, `libffi-dev`, and `cargo` are NOT present — source builds requiring these will fail on musllinux. The run image stays minimal with no build toolchain.

title: Sandbox v1: stdout/stderr redirected to files, timeout wrapper, container naming
content:
Three concrete execution decisions in the sandbox v1 design:

1. **stdout/stderr → files** (not docker logs): Output is redirected to `stdout.log` and `stderr.log` via shell redirection inside the container. Portable across non-Docker backends.

2. **timeout wrapper on every run**: Commands wrap with `timeout -s TERM -k <graceSecs> <runTimeoutSecs> sh -c '...'`. Exit code 124 detection sets `timedOut=True` on RunResult. Belt-and-braces: Docker's own `asyncio.wait_for` with `watchdogTimeout` (timeoutSeconds + timeoutGraceSeconds + 1) still applies.

3. **Container naming**: NOT uniform. Three patterns used:
   - Run containers: `sandbox-<runId>` (uses run ID, not UUID)
   - Install containers: `sandbox-install-<uuid.uuid4().hex>` (full UUID hex string)
   - List containers: `sandbox-list-<uuid.uuid4().hex>`

4. **No tmpfs anywhere**: Only the bind-mounted workspace and RO lib mount are writable/readable. No tmpfs filesystem in containers.

title: Sandbox v1: MetadataStore Protocol with pluggable backends
Sandbox v1: Session/Run lifecycle and concurrency model
content:
Session and run lifecycle in the sandbox v1 design:

**Sessions**: Opaque string IDs, auto-created by `runCode()` with config defaults (default runtime, default TTL). Ephemeral containers but persistent workspace volume. `dropSession(force=False)` waits for in-flight run on FIFO queue then drops; `force=True` SIGKILLs running container, drains queue (SessionDropped on all waiters), then drops. `resetSession` was removed — identical to dropSession + createSession from caller's view.

**Runs**: Never auto-install packages — `requiredPackages` is a precondition check against the lib pool. If missing → `MissingDependenciesError`. SessionHash derived from full SHA-256 (64 hex chars) of sessionId.

**Concurrency model**: Per-session FIFO asyncio.Lock with bounded queue (overflow → `SandboxBusy`). Global concurrency semaphore on top. NetworkPolicy dataclass with just `enabled: bool` today, passed per-run (not stored in SessionInfo).

**Run retention**: Everything deleted as one unit (directory + contents). Workspace directory listing available via `listFiles()` method (returns list[FileInfo], supports recursive walk via rglob with per-file OSError guard). `getSessionUsage()` was planned but not implemented — no SessionUsage type exists. Prometheus metrics not implemented in current version.

title: Sandbox v1: Gromozeka integration split into separate design document
content:
The sandbox v1 design intentionally separates the core library from Gromozeka integration into two documents:

1. `docs/plans/python-sandboxing-v1.md` — the standalone sandbox library design (lib/sandbox/). No Gromozeka dependencies.
2. `docs/plans/python-sandboxing-v1-integration.md` — Gromozeka-specific glue: SandboxService singleton in `internal/services/sandbox/`, TOML config mapping `[sandbox.*]`, `RunPythonHandler` and `SandboxAdminHandler` (registered BEFORE LLMMessageHandler), `chatId+threadId` → `sessionId` mapping, bot_owners admin gating, and doc-sync checklist.

Clean layering: `lib/sandbox/` has no bot dependencies. `internal/services/sandbox/` adapts it to Gromozeka's conventions (singleton via getInstance(), service pattern).

title: Sandbox v1 design docs and implementation plan now in docs/plans/
content:
Three files produced from the June 10 2026 sandbox architecture session:

1. `docs/plans/python-sandboxing-v1.md` — Core sandbox library design (lib/sandbox/): Backend/Runtime/Manager protocols, Alpine base, runtime-scoped library pools, MetadataStore abstraction, full lifecycle, API surface, security model, GC, M1→M6 milestones.
2. `docs/plans/python-sandboxing-v1-integration.md` — Gromozeka glue: SandboxService singleton, TOML config, handlers, admin commands, doc-sync checklist, M5.1→M5.4 milestones.
3. `docs/plans/python-sandboxing-v1-implementation.md` — Teamlead-facing implementation plan with 24 work packages across 6 phases, dependency graph, specialist routing, risk register, verification gates per phase.

The implementation plan is structured for delegation: each WP has specialist assignment, dependencies, files to create/modify, acceptance criteria, test plan, and verify step. Cross-cutting rules block saves the teamlead from re-typing constraints. Reviewer passes inserted between phases.

title: Sandbox singleton: injectConfig() + getInstance() no-args pattern
content: The SandboxManager singleton uses a two-phase initialization: `SandboxManager.injectConfig(config)` stores the config in a class variable, then `SandboxManager.getInstance()` creates/returns the instance. `getInstance()` takes NO arguments. `__init__()` reads config from `cls._configInstance` directly, no config parameter. This avoids the anti-pattern of passing config to `getInstance()` and makes the interface cleaner. The singleton uses `__new__` + `hasattr(self, 'initialized')` guard + `RLock` for thread safety. `injectConfig()` raises `RuntimeError` if called after the instance already exists. Raised via `cls._instance is not None` check.

title: Kebab-case TOML keys with fromDict() camelCase mapping
content: All sandbox config dataclasses map kebab-case TOML keys to camelCase Python fields via a `@classmethod fromDict()` method. Every config class (StorageConfig, DockerBackendConfig, SessionDefaults, SecurityConfig, ConcurrencyConfig, GcConfig, InstallContainerConfig, BasicRuntimeConfig, SandboxConfig) has its own fromDict(). ResourceLimits in types.py also has fromDict() with kebab-case keys. Never use snake_case keys in TOML config files. Never use **kwargs unpacking from config dicts directly — always go through fromDict() for validation. fromDict() docstrings must say "kebab-case keys" not "snake_case keys". Example: `dir-mode` → `dirMode`, `base-url` → `baseUrl`.

title: No dict-or-dataclass union types — always use fromDict()
content: Never have a field that can be either a dict or a dataclass instance. When receiving configuration from dicts (e.g., from TOML files), always convert immediately to the typed dataclass via fromDict(). For example, `SandboxConfig.runtimes` values are converted from dicts to `BasicRuntimeConfig` instances in `SandboxConfig.fromDict()`. Downstream code (e.g., `prepareRuntime()`) should always use direct attribute access (`runtimeConfig.runImageTag`), never `hasattr(runtimeConfig, "runImageTag")` or `getattr(runtimeConfig, ...)`. The `isinstance(rtData, dict)` check in fromDict() with a clear error message prevents silent failures.

title: Async context managers for lock acquisition
content: Use `@asynccontextmanager` for lock/semaphore acquisition instead of manual `acquire()` + `try/finally: release()` patterns. SessionLockRegistry has `sessionLock(sessionId)` context manager. GlobalRunLimiter has `runSlot()` context manager. DropSession is the exception — it uses manual acquire/release because it needs special exception handling (catching SessionBusy/SessionDropped when force=True). The context manager pattern ensures locks are always released even on exceptions, reducing boilerplate. Example: `async with self._lockRegistry.sessionLock(sessionId):` instead of `await self._lockRegistry.acquire(sessionId); try: ... finally: self._lockRegistry.release(sessionId)`.

title: Full UUIDs, no truncation; kwargs-only for struct params
content: Two hard rules: (1) Always use full `uuid.uuid4().hex` (32 hex chars) — never truncate with `[:12]` or similar. Truncation breaks uniqueness guarantees. (2) Methods that accept complex structured parameters (like `ContainerSpec`) must use kwargs-only syntax: `def runOneshot(self, *, spec: ContainerSpec)` not `def runOneshot(self, spec: ContainerSpec)`. Callers must use `self._backend.runOneshot(spec=spec)`. This makes call sites explicit about which struct is being passed and prevents positional argument confusion when signatures change.

title: close() on base Protocol, never hasattr checks
content: All backends must have a `close()` method defined on the base Protocol/ABC. Never use `if hasattr(self._backend, "close")` or similar hasattr checks for protocol methods — if a method is part of the protocol contract, it must exist. The `close()` method was added to `SandboxBackend` Protocol in `backends/base.py`. This gives static type checking and eliminates defensive hasattr patterns. The same applies to any method that should be optional — either make it required on the Protocol or provide a default no-op implementation.

title: Docker connector close order: session.connector before client.close()
content: In DockerBackend.close(), the aiohttp connector must be closed BEFORE calling aiodocker's client.close(). In aiodocker 0.26.0, the `Docker` class stores the aiohttp ClientSession as a PUBLIC attribute `self.session` (not `self._session`). After `client.close()` is called, the session is set to None, making the connector inaccessible. The fix: `if hasattr(self._client, "session") and self._client.session is not None:` then `await self._client.session.connector.close()` before `await self._client.close()`. Use `# type: ignore[reportAttributeAccessIssue]` for accessing private-esque attributes. Log warnings on connector close failure instead of bare `except: pass`.

title: Docker Binds format for Colima/macOS compatibility
content: Docker volume mounts must use the `"Binds"` format (string list: `"hostPath:containerPath:mode"`) in the host config dict, not the `"Mounts"` dict format. The `"Mounts"` format (with Type/Source/Target keys) doesn't work properly with Docker for Mac and Colima — containers can see the mounted directory but not the files inside it. The `"Binds"` format uses simple colon-separated strings and works correctly across all Docker platforms. Example: `hostConfig = {"Binds": [f"{hostPath}:{containerPath}:{mode}"]}`.

title: Install containers need readOnlyRoot=False; run containers readOnlyRoot=True
content: Run containers use `readOnlyRoot=True` (read-only root filesystem) for security. But install containers must use `readOnlyRoot=False` because pip needs to write to temporary directories (`/tmp`, `/var/tmp`) during package installation. Setting readOnlyRoot=True on install containers causes pip to fail with `FileNotFoundError: No usable temporary directory found`. Additionally, the install image must include `setuptools` and `wheel` (via `pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel`) since many source-distribution packages require them for building on Alpine/musl.

title: Octal permission parsing: int(val, 0) not int(val, 0o770)
content: When parsing file/directory permission modes from TOML config (e.g., `"0700"` or `"0o700"`), use `int(value, 0)` with base=0, which auto-detects octal format from the leading `0` or `0o` prefix. Never use `int(value, 0o770)` (which interprets the second argument as a numeric base value, not as a mask — 0o770 = decimal 504, which would try to parse octal strings in base 504 and fail). The correct call is `int(data.get("dir-mode", "0700"), 0)`. Add validation: `if not (0 <= dirMode <= 0o7777): raise ValueError(...)`.

title: Runtime ABC with BasicRuntimeConfig, not Protocol
content: The `Runtime` class is an `ABC` (abstract base class), not a Protocol. It takes `BasicRuntimeConfig` in its `__init__`, stores it as `self._config`, and provides methods like `getScriptName()`, `runCommand()`, `installCommand()`, `listCommand()`, `parseListCommandOutput()`, and `validatePackageSpec()`. It also has `markPrepared()` and `isPrepared()` methods for tracking runtime readiness state. Subclasses must call `super().__init__(config)` and set `name: RuntimeName`. This diverges from earlier design docs which had Runtime as a Protocol — the ABC pattern is what's actually implemented.

title: Sandbox enums: RuntimeName and BackendName defined in lib/sandbox/enums.py
content:
The sandbox library has two initial StrEnum classes in `lib/sandbox/enums.py`:

- `RuntimeName(StrEnum)`: Only `PYTHON = "python"` for v0. More runtimes (Node.js, Go, Ruby) can be added later.
- `BackendName(StrEnum)`: Only `DOCKER = "docker"` for v0. More backends (Firecracker, gVisor) can be added later.

Both are re-exported from `lib/sandbox/__init__.py` via `from .enums import BackendName, RuntimeName` with `__all__ = ["BackendName", "RuntimeName"]`.

These enums implement the three-axis split (Backend/Runtime/Manager protocols) documented in existing sandbox memories and design docs. The enum-based pattern means backends and runtimes are added by adding enum values, not by changing string constants throughout the codebase.

title: Collocated tests in lib/ are permitted despite AGENTS.md "no collocated tests" rule
content:
AGENTS.md states: "No new collocated tests in lib/ or internal/. All new test files MUST go under tests/." Current state:

1. `pyproject.toml` includes `lib` in `testpaths = ["tests", "lib", "internal"]` — so collocated tests in `lib/` WOULD be discovered by pytest if they existed.
2. **However, as of June 2026, zero collocated test files exist under `lib/`.** All tests were moved to `tests/` during the Phase 4 test reorganization.
3. No collocated tests exist under `internal/` either.

The "no collocated tests" rule has been fully enforced. All test files live under `tests/` with directory structure mirroring source (strip `internal/` prefix for source under `internal/`, use `tests/lib/X/Y/test_Z.py` for `lib/X/Y.py`). When creating new tests, always use the `tests/` convention — do not place collocated tests under `lib/` or `internal/`.

title: dataclasses.FrozenInstanceError inherits from AttributeError in Python 3.12+ — not TypeError
content:
When testing frozen dataclasses (``@dataclass(frozen=True)``) in Python 3.12+, attempting to set an attribute raises ``dataclasses.FrozenInstanceError``, which inherits from ``AttributeError`` — **not** ``TypeError`` as in Python 3.11 and earlier.

This was discovered in ``lib/sandbox/tests/test_types.py`` (WP-1.2). The test expected ``pytest.raises(TypeError)`` on frozen dataclass mutation, but Python 3.13.13 raises ``FrozenInstanceError`` (a subclass of ``AttributeError``).

**Fix**: Use ``pytest.raises(dataclasses.FrozenInstanceError)`` instead of ``pytest.raises(TypeError)`` when testing frozen dataclass immutability. This is both more precise and works across all Python 3.12+ versions Gromozeka targets.

title: Testing slots=True on dataclasses: avoid cls.__new__(cls) for classes with required fields
content:
When verifying that a ``@dataclass(slots=True)`` class actually uses ``__slots__``, you cannot call ``cls.__new__(cls)`` on a frozen dataclass with required fields — the dataclass ``__init__`` must be called with valid arguments, and ``__new__`` alone triggers a pyright type checking error ("No overloads for __new__ match the provided arguments").

Two safe patterns for testing ``slots=True``:

1. **Construct an instance and check absence of ``__dict__``** (preferred when args are easy to provide):
   ```python
   obj = MyDataclass(arg1=..., arg2=...)
   assert not hasattr(obj, '__dict__')
   ```

2. **Check ``__slots__`` on the class directly** (works when construction is inconvenient):
   ```python
   assert hasattr(MyDataclass, '__slots__')
   ```

This pattern was established in ``lib/sandbox/tests/test_types.py`` for all 18 dataclasses.

title: pytest.mark.parametrize ids callable with multiple argnames receives individual values, not tuples
content: When using `@pytest.mark.parametrize("parent,child", [(SandboxError, ConfigError), ...])` with an `ids` callable, the callable receives individual parameter values (one at a time), NOT the whole tuple. For example, `ids=lambda p: p[0].__name__` crashes because `p` is `SandboxError` (a class), not a tuple. The correct approach is to either (a) use a list of string IDs matching the length of the parameter set, or (b) use `ids=lambda p: p.__name__` and rely on it being called once per argname element (but this produces confusing output with 2× IDs). The cleanest fix is to drop the callable and pass explicit string IDs: `ids=["SandboxError→ConfigError", ...]`. Discovered during sandbox error hierarchy test implementation.

title: Literal type for constrained string values alongside StrEnum in dataclasses
content:
Config dataclasses use `typing.Literal` for fields that accept a constrained set of string values but don't merit a full `StrEnum`. Example from `DockerBackendConfig.imagePullPolicy`:

```python
imagePullPolicy: Literal["never", "if-not-present", "always"] = "if-not-present"
```

This is an intentional complement to the project's StrEnum convention: use StrEnum for named constants referenced across multiple modules, use Literal for one-off string constraints that are only relevant within a single dataclass field.

title: SandboxConfig.runtimes: dict[RuntimeName, Any] polymorphic runtime config pattern
content:
`SandboxConfig.runtimes` is typed as `dict[RuntimeName, BasicRuntimeConfig]` with `field(default_factory=dict)` (line 359). All runtime entries use the same `BasicRuntimeConfig` class — there is no polymorphic `Any` type and no subclass like `PythonRuntimeConfig` in the actual codebase (PythonRuntimeConfig only appears in design docs under `docs/plans/`).

When adding a new runtime, register a new `RuntimeName` enum value (currently only `PYTHON = 'python'` in `lib/sandbox/enums.py:17`) and provide a `BasicRuntimeConfig` with that runtime's image tags, Dockerfile paths, env vars, and install container limits. The `fromDict()` method at lines 371-377 constructs `BasicRuntimeConfig` instances from raw dicts and enforces that input values must be dicts via an `isinstance(rtData, dict)` check (line 375).

title: StorageConfig.rootDir as required field — no default for infrastructure paths
content:
`StorageConfig.rootDir` is the only required field in `StorageConfig` (line 42, no default value). `fromDict()` enforces this at lines 56-57 with `raise ConfigError` if `root-dir` is missing. This is deliberate: rootDir is an environment-specific path without a sensible default.

However, it is NOT the only required field in the sandbox config hierarchy. `BasicRuntimeConfig` (lines 302-308) has seven required fields with no defaults: `runImageTag`, `installImageTag`, `runDockerfile`, `installDockerfile`, `libMountPath`, `env`, and `installContainer`.

title: Sandbox config: Literal and StrEnum coexist in same module
content: Demonstrated in lib/sandbox/config.py: `DockerBackendConfig.imagePullPolicy` uses `Literal["always", "missing", "never"]` while enum fields (e.g., `BackendConfig.backend` of type `BackendName`) use `StrEnum`. This confirms the project tolerates both — Literal for closed string sets that are never compared/enumerated programmatically, StrEnum for named constants that appear in isinstance() checks, switch/match, or config registries.

title: len(cls.__slots__) triggers pyright Iterable[str] vs Sized error
content:
When verifying `@dataclass(slots=True)` by calling `len(SomeClass.__slots__)`, pyright (even in basic mode) reports `reportArgumentType`: "Argument of type 'Iterable[str]' cannot be assigned to parameter 'obj' of type 'Sized' in function 'len'".

This happens because `cls.__slots__` is typed as `Iterable[str]` (not `tuple[str, ...]`) in the type stubs, and `Iterable` does not inherit from `Sized`. The error occurs whether accessing `__slots__` via a specific class (e.g. `ContainerSpec.__slots__`) or via a `cls: type` parameter.

**Workaround**: Avoid `len()` on `__slots__`. Instead use:
```python
assert hasattr(cls, "__slots__")
assert bool(cls.__slots__)  # truthiness check works for non-empty tuples
```

Note: the existing `test_types.py` at `lib/sandbox/tests/test_types.py` uses `len(cls.__slots__)` with `cls: type` and passes pyright. This was observed to pass, but the same pattern failed in `test_protocols.py`. The root cause is inconsistent — the reliable fix is to avoid `len()` on `__slots__` entirely.

title: Runtime Protocol is synchronous (not async) unlike SandboxBackend and MetadataStore
content:
The sandbox library has three orthogonal abstractions with different async/sync profiles and inheritance patterns:

1. **`SandboxBackend` (ABC)** — all methods are `async`. Container/execution backend (Docker API, healthchecks, image management).
2. **`MetadataStore` (ABC)** — all methods are `async`. Persistence backend for session/run metadata (filesystem or database I/O).
3. **`Runtime` (ABC)** — MOSTLY synchronous but `validatePackageSpec()` is `async`. Concrete implementations (e.g., `PythonRuntime`) explicitly inherit from `Runtime` (not structural subtyping). `Runtime` is an ABC, not a Protocol.

Method signatures:
- **`Runtime`**: `runCommand(runId, *, hasStdin, limits) -> list[str]` (sync), `installCommand(packages, *, upgrade) -> list[str]` (sync), `listCommand(stdoutPath, stderrPath) -> list[str]` (sync), `parseListCommandOutput(outcome, stdout, stderr) -> List[PackageInfo]` (sync), `validatePackageSpec(spec) -> None` (async)

Note: `detectArtifacts` does NOT exist as a Runtime method. The fourth abstract method is `parseListCommandOutput`, not `detectArtifacts`.

title: RuntimeRecord uses `runtime` field name (not `name`) for RuntimeName identifier
content: In `lib/sandbox/types.py`, the `RunInfo` dataclass uses `runtime: RuntimeName` as the field name (not `name`). This is the run metadata record that stores the runtime associated with a code execution. The MetadataStore protocol methods (loadPackagesInfo, savePackagesInfo) also use parameter name `runtime` for RuntimeName. No separate RuntimeRecord class exists — RunInfo serves as the persisted run metadata type.

title: Sandbox: ContainerSpec.mounts loosely typed as list[dict[str, str]]
content: In `lib/sandbox/backends/base.py:43`, the `ContainerSpec.mounts` field is typed as `list[dict[str, str]]`, which doesn't enforce required keys (`hostPath`, `containerPath`, `mode`). The design doc §8.2 and tests use structured mount specs with these three keys. Consider defining a `MountSpec` TypedDict or dataclass (e.g., `TypedDict('MountSpec', {'hostPath': str, 'containerPath': str, 'mode': str})`) for type safety at the API boundary. This was flagged as a non-blocking recommendation.

title: atomicWriteJson: tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile defaults to binary mode — must pass mode='w'
content:
In `lib/sandbox/storage.py`, the `atomicWriteJson` function uses `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile` which defaults to binary mode (`mode='w+b'`). When passing the file handle to `json.dump()`, pyright reports a type error because `json.dump` expects a `TextIO` but gets a `BinaryIO`.

Fix: always pass `mode='w'` (or `mode='w+'`) to `NamedTemporaryFile` when writing text content like JSON:

```python
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
    delete=False,
    dir=str(tmpDir),
    mode='w',          # <-- required for text output
) as tmpFile:
    json.dump(payload, tmpFile, indent=2, default=str)
```

Without this, pyright correctly flags the type mismatch and `json.dump` could behave unexpectedly (writing repr strings for bytes content).

title: Testing atomicWriteJson cleanup: default=str makes json.dump hard to fail — use circular reference
content:
When testing the temp-file cleanup logic in `atomicWriteJson` (`lib/sandbox/storage.py`), a custom `Unserialisable` class with `__str__` / `__repr__` is **not** sufficient to trigger a `TypeError` from `json.dump()` because the function passes `default=str`, which converts any object via `str()` before raising.

The correct technique to trigger a serialization failure is a **circular reference** (a list/dict containing itself). Python's `json.dump` detects circular references and raises `ValueError: Circular reference detected` before ever calling `default`:

```python
circularPayload = {"key": []}
circularPayload["key"].append(circularPayload["key"])
# json.dump(circularPayload, ..., default=str)  # -> ValueError
```

This reliably triggers the cleanup path in the atomic write function, verifying that temporary files are cleaned up when the write fails.

Existing related memory (#5013de97) covers the same technique for `DatabaseStatsStorage.record()` testing — this is the same pattern applied to sandbox testing.

title: ensureDirectoryLayout skips chown because StorageConfig lacks uid/gid fields
content:
The `ensureDirectoryLayout` function in `lib/sandbox/storage.py` creates the sandbox storage directory tree (`runtimes/`, `sessions/`, `meta/{sessions,runs,runtimes}/`, `tmp/`). The original design spec mentioned best-effort chown, but this was **not implemented** because `StorageConfig` (defined in `lib/sandbox/config.py`) only has `rootDir`, `dirMode`, and `fileMode` fields — it has no `uid`/`gid` fields.

Decision: skip chown entirely. The function creates directories with `mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` and applies chmod with `config.dirMode`. If chown is needed later, `StorageConfig` would need to be extended with uid/gid fields first, and `ensureDirectoryLayout` would attempt `os.chown()` with best-effort warning on failure (when not running as root).

This is consistent with the existing design where `StorageConfig.rootDir` is the only required field — uid/gid ownership is considered an environment concern, not a storage configuration concern.

title: pytest tmp_path fixture must use snake_case parameter name, cannot be camelCase
content:
pytest's built-in `tmp_path` fixture is resolved by parameter name. Even though the Gromozeka project convention requires camelCase for all variable/parameter names, the pytest fixture must be used as `tmp_path` (snake_case). Using `tmpPath` as the fixture parameter name causes pytest to not recognize it, resulting in a `fixture 'tmpPath' not found` error.

This is a conflict between project convention and pytest's fixture resolution mechanism. The built-in fixture name must be used as-is.

Affected test files: any test using `tmp_path` (e.g. `lib/sandbox/tests/test_locks.py` for pool lock tests).

title: acquirePoolLock return type must be IO[str], not object, for pyright compatibility
content:
`acquirePoolLock()` in `lib/sandbox/locks.py` opens a file and returns a file handle for `fcntl.flock`. The initial spec used `-> object` as the return type (intentionally opaque), but pyright rejects `.close()` calls on `object` type. 

The correct annotation is `-> IO[str]` for the return type and `lockHandle: IO[str]` for `releasePoolLock`'s parameter. This satisfies pyright while still keeping the handle opaque to most callers (they just pass it to `releasePoolLock`). The inner helper function `_acquire()` also needs its return type annotated as `IO[str]` (not `object`) to avoid a cascade of type errors.

title: GlobalRunLimiter waitSeconds accepts float for sub-second timeouts
content:
`GlobalRunLimiter.__init__` in `lib/sandbox/locks.py` accepts `waitSeconds: float` (not `int`). The original spec typed it as `int`, but `asyncio.wait_for` accepts float timeout values, and sub-second timeouts are essential for testing (e.g. `waitSeconds=0.1`). 

The `ConcurrencyConfig.globalQueueWaitSeconds` remains `int` (a config file default, typically 60 seconds), but the `GlobalRunLimiter` constructor widens the type to `float` for flexibility. This is a deliberate widening: callers can pass config values (int) directly without conversion, and tests can pass sub-second values (float) without issues.

title: SessionLockRegistry: wait count pre-increment and post-decrement pattern for queue enforcement
content:
SessionLockRegistry.acquire() in lib/sandbox/locks.py uses a specific pattern for queue limit enforcement based on the _SessionState dataclass:

1. **State retrieval**: `state = self._states.setdefault(sessionId, _SessionState())` — atomically creates or retrieves the _SessionState for the session.
2. **Cancelled check**: If `state.cancelled` is True, raises SessionDropped immediately — no waiters increment needed.
3. **Capacity check**: `if state.waiters >= self._config.maxQueuedRunsPerSession` — rejects before blocking, raises SessionBusy.
4. **Pre-increment**: `state.waiters += 1` (line 106) — incremented BEFORE blocking on `await state.lock.acquire()` so the capacity check reflects all in-flight tasks (holder + waiters).
5. **Post-decrement on failure**: In the `except BaseException` block (line 112-114), decrements waiters if acquisition fails (CancelledError, SessionDropped, etc.). This ensures waiters remains accurate even when a task is cancelled while waiting on the lock.
6. **Normal release**: release() (line 132) decrements `state.waiters` on successful release.

The key distinction from the old _waitCounts dict implementation: decrement on failure happens in an `except BaseException` block (not `finally`), and there is no `max(0, ...)` guard — clearCancelled() removes the entire state entry rather than leaving a zeroed counter.

title: SessionLockRegistry forceCancel: dual cancellation check + lock release to wake waiters
content:
SessionLockRegistry.forceCancel() in lib/sandbox/locks.py uses the cascade mechanism to ensure all waiters discover cancellation:

1. **Mark cancelled**: In forceCancel() (line 152-153): retrieves or creates _SessionState via setdefault, sets `state.cancelled = True`.
2. **Release the lock**: Calls `state.lock.release()` once (line 155) to wake the current holder or one waiter. Catches RuntimeError if the lock is not held (line 156-157).

Cancellation is checked at two points in acquire():
- **Before acquiring** (line 99-100): If `state.cancelled` is True, raises SessionDropped immediately (short-circuit for new callers after forceCancel).
- **After acquiring** (lines 109-111): If `state.cancelled` was set while the coroutine was blocked on the lock, releases the lock (waking the next waiter — the cascade mechanism) and raises SessionDropped.

The cascade works as follows: each waiter that wakes up checks the cancelled flag, releases the lock to wake the next waiter, and raises SessionDropped. This continues until all waiters have been processed. No asyncio.Condition or multi-release loop is needed.

release() guards against RuntimeError on lock.release() (lines 133-136) since after forceCancel, the lock may already be free after the cascade completes.

title: @dataclass(slots=True) has no __dict__ — use dataclasses.fields() + getattr() for serialization
content:
Sandbox record dataclasses (RunInfo, SessionInfo, PackageInfo, etc. in lib/sandbox/types.py) all use @dataclass(slots=True). As a result, they have no __dict__ attribute — slots classes don't create one. Using record.__dict__.items() would raise AttributeError.

The actual serialization approaches used:
1. Hand-written toDict() methods (dominant pattern): RunInfo.toDict() manually enumerates fields with type-specific conversions (datetime.isoformat(), enum.value), SessionInfo.toDict() does the same plus delegates to ResourceLimits.toDict() for nested objects.
2. slottedObjectToDict() utility: PackageInfo.toDict() delegates to libUtils.slottedObjectToDict(self, recursive=True), which introspects __slots__ via __class__.__mro__ (NOT dataclasses.fields()).

dataclasses.fields() is never used anywhere in lib/sandbox/ for serialization. The hand-written approach was chosen over dataclasses.fields() + getattr() because each toDict() needs custom per-field type conversions (datetime → ISO string, enum → .value, nested dataclass → .toDict()) that a generic reflection-based approach cannot handle without complex dispatch.

title: Sentinel pattern for optional None dataclass fields in test helpers
content:
When a dataclass field is `X | None` (e.g., `finishedAt: datetime | None` on `RunRecord`) and you have a test helper factory function, you cannot use `None` as the default parameter value because it's ambiguous: you can't distinguish between "caller didn't provide this" (use a sensible default) and "caller explicitly wants None" (pass None through).

Use a module-level sentinel object as the default:

```python
_UNSET = object()

def _makeRunRecord(
    finishedAt: datetime | None | object = _UNSET,  # type: ignore[assignment]
) -> RunRecord:
    if finishedAt is _UNSET:
        finishedAt = datetime(2025, 1, 15, 12, 0, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
    # finishedAt is now either the caller's value (including None) or the default
    ...
```

The `type: ignore[assignment]` comment suppresses pyright's complaint about `object` not being a valid `datetime | None`. This pattern works regardless of whether the dataclass uses `slots=True`.

title: SandboxManager createSession: workspace path convention and chmod
content:
In `SandboxManager.createSession()` in lib/sandbox/manager.py, the workspace directory path is computed as:

```python
sHash = sessionHash(sessionId)
workspacePath = Path(self._config.storage.rootDir) / "sessions" / sHash / "workspace"
```

The directory is created with `workspacePath.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)`, then permissions are set via `os.chmod(workspacePath, self._config.storage.dirMode)`. The `exist_ok=True` is deliberately used even outside forceRecreate to handle any stale directory remnants; the idempotency guarantee comes from the metadata store check (if the session record exists, return it without touching the filesystem).

This path convention (`rootDir/sessions/<hash>/workspace/`) is the canonical workspace layout and is assumed by other methods like `getSessionUsage()` which walks `record.workspacePath`.

title: Yandex Cloud MCP proxy: manual invocation via stdin/stdout JSON-RPC
content:
The `@yandex-cloud/mcp` package (`npx -y @yandex-cloud/mcp`) is a proxy server for authenticated connections to remote Yandex Cloud MCP servers. It runs as a server over stdin/stdout speaking the MCP (Model Context Protocol) — NOT a CLI tool you pass arguments to directly.

To call tools manually, pipe JSON-RPC messages to stdin:

```bash
# List available tools
echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list"}' | npx -y @yandex-cloud/mcp -s <server> -p <profile> -H "Folder-Id: <folder_id>"

# Call a tool (search example)
echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"search","arguments":{"query":{"queryText":"your search query"}}}}' | npx -y @yandex-cloud/mcp -s search -p <profile> -H "Folder-Id: <folder_id>"
```

Flags: `-s/--server` (required), `-p/--profile` (YC CLI profile), `-H/--header` (custom headers, e.g. Folder-Id), `--username` (OAuth email), `--subject-id` (service account ID), `--no-auth`, `--region` (ru/kz).

This is useful for debugging or testing MCP tools without an MCP client application.

title: Yandex Search MCP returns base64-encoded raw_data, decoded by infrastructure
content:
The Yandex Search MCP (`@yandex-cloud/mcp` with the `search` server) returns search results in a `raw_data` field that is base64-encoded XML. The agent does NOT decode this — the base64 decoding happens in the infrastructure/system layer between the MCP tool and the agent. The agent only ever sees the already-decoded results.

This means:
1. The MCP's `raw_data` output is NOT plain text — it's base64-encoded.
2. If inspecting MCP tool outputs manually (via `tools/call` JSON-RPC), the `raw_data` will appear as an opaque base64 string.
3. The decoded XML contains search results with URLs, titles, descriptions, and snippets.
4. The decoding is transparent to the agent — it happens in the tool execution pipeline before the result reaches the agent.

title: Yandex Cloud Search MCP: available tools (search and search_generative) and their schemas
content:
The Yandex Cloud Search MCP server exposes two tools:

1. **`search`** — Standard Yandex Search API. Input schema:
   - `query` (required, object): `{ queryText: string, page?: integer }`
   - `folderId` (optional, string): Yandex Cloud folder ID (overrides Folder-Id header)
   - `period` (optional, string): Time period — `"0"` (unspecified), `"1"` (all time), `"2"` (last 24h), `"3"` (2 weeks), `"4"` (month)

2. **`search_generative`** — Generative/chat-completion-style search. Input schema:
   - `messages` (required, array): Chat-style messages with `role` (`ROLE_USER`/`ROLE_ASSISTANT`) and `content`
   - `folderId` (optional, string)
   - `site` (optional, object): `{ site: string[] }` — restrict to specific websites
   - `enableRichStructuredAnswer` (optional, boolean)

Both tools return base64-encoded XML in `raw_data` (decoded transparently by the infrastructure layer before reaching the agent).

title: SandboxManager listFiles handles path="/" as workspace root outside resolveWorkspacePath
content:
`SandboxManager.listFiles()` defaults `path="/"` to mean "workspace root". However, `resolveWorkspacePath()` (from `lib.sandbox.storage`) **rejects absolute paths** (paths starting with `/`) — it treats them as path traversal attempts. This means `listFiles` cannot call `resolveWorkspacePath` with `path="/"`.

The implementation handles this by checking for `path == "/"` before calling `resolveWorkspacePath`:

```python
if path == "/":
    resolved = workspacePath
else:
    resolved = resolveWorkspacePath(workspacePath, path)
```

When `path == "/"`, it resolves directly to `workspacePath` (the session workspace root). All other paths go through `resolveWorkspacePath` for security. This is a deliberate design: `"/"` is a shorthand for "list the workspace root", not an actual filesystem path traversal.

The same pattern does NOT apply to `readFile`, `writeFile`, or `deleteFile` — they take paths to specific files/directories and always go through `resolveWorkspacePath`, rejecting absolute paths.

title: SandboxManager _touchSessionInternal — private TTL bump helper for file operations
content:
