content:
Docstring gap audit completed on `lib/sandbox/locks.py`. The file already had comprehensive docstrings (module, class `SessionLockRegistry`, class `GlobalRunLimiter`, all methods, all functions) with camelCase naming, top-level imports, and thorough Args: sections. The only gap was missing explicit `Returns:` sections on methods that return `None`.

The following 9 functions/methods had `Returns: None` added:
- `SessionLockRegistry.__init__()`, `acquire()`, `release()`, `forceCancel()`, `clearCancelled()`
- `GlobalRunLimiter.__init__()`, `acquire()`, `release()`
- `releasePoolLock()`

Verification passed: `import lib.sandbox.locks` succeeds, `make format` passes (no changes needed), pyright on `locks.py` reports 0 errors/warnings/informations.

Note: There are pre-existing, unrelated pyright errors in `lib/sandbox/tests/test_errors.py` (not in `locks.py` itself).

title: Correction: instance data fields are camelCase, not UPPER_CASE — UPPER_CASE is for module-level constants only
content:
During a docstring compliance pass on `lib/sandbox/errors.py`, an agent mistakenly applied the AGENTS.md rule "UPPER_CASE for constants" to instance data fields — changing `MissingDependenciesError.missing` to `MISSING`, and `InvalidPackageSpec.spec`/`reason` to `SPEC`/`REASON`. This broke the tests in `lib/sandbox/tests/test_errors.py` because they referenced the original camelCase attribute names (`.missing`, `.spec`, `.reason`).

The correction: AGENTS.md's `UPPER_CASE for constants` rule applies strictly to **module-level constants** (e.g., `MAX_RETRIES = 3`), NOT to **instance attributes** that hold per-instance data. Instance data fields follow camelCase like all other variables/args/fields/functions/methods.  

The fix was reverting the field names back to camelCase in `lib/sandbox/errors.py`, which resolved the pyright errors and restored test compatibility.

Lesson: When applying naming conventions from AGENTS.md, carefully distinguish between:
- Instance attributes (camelCase) — data that varies per instance
- Module-level constants (UPPER_CASE) — immutable values defined at module scope

title: lib/sandbox/backends/base.py is already fully docstring-compliant with AGENTS.md
content:
During a docstring compliance audit of `lib/sandbox/backends/base.py` (June 10, 2026), the file was found to be already fully compliant with AGENTS.md requirements. No changes were needed.

Confirmed present:
- Complete module docstring with description and Classes section
- Complete docstrings for all 4 classes: `ContainerSpec`, `ContainerOutcome`, `ManagedContainerInfo`, `SandboxBackend` (abstract base)
- All dataclass fields documented in Attributes sections
- All 7 abstract methods have complete docstrings with Args: and Returns: sections
- All imports at top of file
- All naming follows conventions (camelCase, PascalCase)
- `make format lint` passes and `import lib.sandbox.backends.base` succeeds

This file is a clean baseline — no docstring work needed in future sessions.

title: Dataclass field docstrings go in class-level Attributes: section, not inline string literals
content:
In `lib/sandbox/`, dataclass fields are documented in the class docstring's `Attributes:` section, NOT with inline triple-quoted string literals placed before each field.

The wrong approach (dangling string literals — syntactically valid but semantically useless; Python discards them):
```python
@dataclass(slots=True)
class SessionInfo:
    """Class docstring."""
    """str: Unique identifier for the session."""
    sessionId: str
```

The correct approach (following patterns in `lib/sandbox/types.py`, `lib/sandbox/config.py`, and all other `@dataclass` uses in the project):
```python
@dataclass(slots=True)
class SessionInfo:
    """Persisted metadata for a sandbox session.

    Attributes:
        sessionId: Unique identifier for the session.
        sessionHash: Hash of the session ID for stable filesystem naming.
    """
    sessionId: str
    sessionHash: str
```

This was confirmed during a docstring improvement pass on `lib/sandbox/metadata/base.py`. The agent initially attempted the first (wrong) pattern and self-corrected after examining the project's existing `@dataclass` conventions. Files checked for the canonical pattern: `lib/sandbox/types.py` (NetworkPolicy, ResourceLimits, etc.), `lib/sandbox/config.py` (StorageConfig, etc.).

This differs from `__slots__` classes in `internal/bot/models/` (e.g., `EnsuredMessage`), which DO use inline triple-quoted comments after each slot assignment in `__init__`. The convention is context-dependent.

title: lib/sandbox/ Returns section convention: plain descriptions, no explicit type prefixes
content:
The `lib/sandbox/` package follows a Returns section format convention where descriptions use plain text without explicit type prefixes. This was confirmed by examining existing docstrings across storage.py, docker.py, manager.py, types.py, runtimes/base.py, locks.py, and errors.py — all use plain descriptions like:

```python
Returns:
    The full 64-character lowercase hex digest.
```

NOT:
```python
Returns:
    int: The full 64-character lowercase hex digest.  # WRONG for this package
```

This convention was established in a code review of docstring changes to gc.py (June 10, 2026) where the reviewer flagged that the changes introduced a new format that deviates from the rest of the package. The gc.py changes used `"int: Number of sessions removed."` instead of `"Number of sessions removed."`.

The AGENTS.md docstring rules (Google-style with Args:/Returns:) don't specify whether types should be included in Returns descriptions. The `lib/sandbox/` sub-project has its own convention on this point.

title: Investigation: reported sessionHash import error in lib/sandbox/metadata/filesystem.py — not reproducible
content:
A bug report claimed `from lib.sandbox.storage import sessionHash` fails in `lib/sandbox/metadata/filesystem.py` with `ImportError: cannot import name 'sessionHash' from 'lib.sandbox.storage'`.

Thorough investigation (import graph tracing, circular dependency analysis, stale cache elimination, multiple import-order scenarios) confirmed the issue **does not exist** in the current codebase:

1. `sessionHash` is correctly defined as a standalone function in `lib/sandbox/storage.py` (line 41-50).
2. Import in `lib/sandbox/metadata/filesystem.py` line 27 is correct: `from ..storage import atomicWriteJson, sessionHash`.
3. No circular import chain: `filesystem → storage → (config, errors)` is acyclic — neither `config.py` nor `errors.py` import from `metadata/filesystem.py`.
4. All import orders work: storage first then filesystem, filesystem first, sessionHash then filesystem, etc.
5. All 2195 project tests pass (including 172 sandbox tests).
6. Clearing all `__pycache__` directories and retrying confirmed no stale cache issue.

The reported error was likely a transient problem (corrupted `.pyc` cache already cleared), a misdiagnosis, or an environment-specific issue. No code fix was needed or applied.

title: Timeout in runOneshot: re-inspect container after SIGKILL to capture signal/exitCode
content:
In `DockerBackend.runOneshot()` (`lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py`), when `asyncio.wait_for(container.wait(), timeout=spec.limits.timeoutSeconds)` raises `asyncio.TimeoutError`, the original code killed the container but did not capture the exit signal. This left callers unable to distinguish timeouts from other failures.

The fix adds a re-inspection step after killing on timeout:

```python
except asyncio.TimeoutError:
    await self.killContainer(container.id, signal="SIGKILL")
    inspectData = await container.show()
    exitCode = inspectData.get("State", {}).get("ExitCode")
    oomKilled = inspectData.get("State", {}).get("OOMKilled", False)
    signal = "SIGKILL" if exitCode != 0 else None
    return ContainerOutcome(
        containerId=container.id,
        exitCode=exitCode,
        signal=signal,
        oomKilled=oomKilled,
        inspects=inspectData,
    )
```

Key points:
- `signal` is set to `"SIGKILL"` only if `exitCode != 0` (non-zero exit means the signal killed it; zero exit means the container exited normally before the inspect).
- This replaces the previous behavior where the exception would propagate without a `ContainerOutcome` return, making it impossible to distinguish timeout vs. other container failures.
- The `container.show()` call fetches fresh state from the Docker daemon after the kill.

title: Stale client cleanup: track connectorClosed/clientClosed separately to avoid resource leak
DockerBackend._buildImage tar includes all context files — no filtering
content:
In `lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py:176-178`, `DockerBackend._buildImage()` creates a tar archive of the Docker build context by iterating `contextDir.iterdir()` and adding ALL files to the tar. This means:

1. Large unnecessary files in the context directory bloat the tar → slow builds, more bandwidth
2. Hidden files (`.dockerignore`, `.gitkeep`, etc.) are included
3. There's no `.dockerignore` support — the Docker daemon would normally respect it, but since the tar is constructed locally and sent as `fileobj`, the daemon never sees the original directory

The fix is to filter files more deliberately:
```python
for filePath in contextDir.iterdir():
    if filePath.name == ".dockerignore":
        continue  # Skip dockerignore file itself
    if filePath.is_file():
        tar.add(str(filePath), arcname=filePath.name)
```

This is a recommendation (not critical) because the context dirs are expected to be small runtime Dockerfiles.

title: runCode workspace directory must apply config.storage.dirMode via chmod
content: In `SandboxManager.runCode()` (lib/sandbox/manager.py), when a workspace directory is created for code execution, it was created with default umask permissions. The fix: after `os.makedirs(workspacePath, exist_ok=True)`, call `os.chmod(workspacePath, self._config.storage.dirMode)` to respect the configured directory permissions from `StorageConfig`. This is a different code path from `createSession()` which already applies chmod on its workspace path — `runCode()` creates per-run workspace subdirectories that also need explicit permission control.

title: prepareRuntime must track each ensureImage() independently — both images must succeed before marking prepared
content:
In `SandboxManager.prepareRuntime()` (lib/sandbox/manager.py lines 257-283), two images are built: the run image (`ensureImage(imageType="run")`) and the install image (`ensureImage(imageType="install")`). Each `ensureImage()` call is wrapped in its own `try/except ImageBuildFailed` block. If the run image build fails, the method early-returns `self._runtimes[runtime].isPrepared()` without attempting the install image. If the install image build fails (run succeeded), it similarly early-returns. Only when BOTH images succeed does the method call `runtimeImpl.markPrepared()` and return `True`.

Key implementation detail: there are NO `runImageBuilt` / `installImageBuilt` boolean flags. The tracking is done via early-return from each try/except block — the `markPrepared()` call and `return True` at lines 282-284 are only reachable if neither early-return fired.

title: Bare except Exception in collectOrphanContainers lacks exc_info — fixed with proper logging
content:
Found during code review of `lib/sandbox/gc.py`. The `collectOrphanContainers()` method at the `listManagedContainers()` call site used a bare `except Exception` that swallowed the exception without details. This made debugging container listing failures difficult.

Fix: Changed from:
```python
except Exception:
    return 0
```
to:
```python
except Exception as exc:
    logger.warning("Failed to list managed containers: %s", exc, exc_info=True)
    return 0
```

The key improvement is `exc_info=True` which logs the full traceback, enabling debugging of transient Docker daemon failures, permission errors, or API timeouts during container enumeration. This pattern (log with exc_info=True) should be used for all exception handling in GC code where the exception indicates an operational issue.

title: After SIGKILL timeout, container state may not be final — need async sleep before inspect
content: In `DockerBackend.runOneshot()` (lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py), when `asyncio.TimeoutError` is caught and the container is killed with SIGKILL, the code immediately calls `await container.show()` to inspect the container's exit code and state. However, SIGKILL is asynchronous — the container may still be terminating when `container.show()` is called, leading to stale or intermediate state being read (e.g., exit code 0 instead of -1, OOMKilled=None instead of False). The fix is to add `await asyncio.sleep(0.1)` after the kill and before inspect, giving the container time to reach a terminal state. This is in addition to the already-documented timeout fix that captures the SIGKILL signal in the ContainerOutcome.

title: Path validation: use pathlib.relative_to() instead of str.startswith() for security
content: In `lib/sandbox/storage.py`, path containment validation was changed from `str.startswith()` to `pathlib.Path.relative_to()`. The `startswith()` check was vulnerable to symlink traversal and path-normalization edge cases (e.g., `/safe/../../etc/passwd` would pass a `/safe/` prefix check). Using `relative_to()` from pathlib properly validates that one path is a true descendant of another across platforms, raising `ValueError` if not. This is the correct pattern for sandboxed storage path validation and should be used whenever validating path containment in the sandbox subsystem.

title: ResourceLimits.fromDict must guard against zero/minimum values
content: In `lib/sandbox/types.py`, `ResourceLimits.fromDict()` was updated with minimum value guards for `memoryMb`, `cpuCount`, `timeoutSeconds`, and similar numeric fields. Without these guards, values like `memoryMb=0` or `cpuCount=0` would propagate through the system as-is, leading to Docker container creation failure or unbounded resource allocation. The pattern is: validate that numeric resource limits have sensible minimums (typically >= 1 for counts, >= config-defined minimum for memory) at the deserialization boundary, before they reach Docker API calls. This is the correct place for such validation — at the type/deserialization boundary, not scattered across callers.

title: FilesystemMetadata must clean up locks in deleteSession/deleteRun to prevent dict leak
content:
In lib/sandbox/metadata/filesystem.py, deleteSession() (lines 168-179) and deleteRun() (lines 236-247) do not explicitly clean up lock entries from self._locks after deleting metadata files. The lock entries linger until TTLDict auto-expiry (300s TTL + up to 600s GC interval = up to ~900s).

This is not an unbounded memory leak because _locks is a TTLDict (TTL=300s, GC=600s) that auto-expires entries. However, explicit lock cleanup on delete would be cleaner: entries for deleted sessions/runs serve no purpose and consume memory for up to 15 minutes. The risk is low — stale lock entries don't block anything, they just waste a small amount of memory temporarily.

title: Lock dictionary init: use setdefault() instead of check-and-create to avoid race
content:
The canonical pattern for lazily initialising per-key state in asynchronous lock registries in this codebase is dict.setdefault(). In lib/sandbox/locks.py line 98, SessionLockRegistry.acquire() uses `state = self._states.setdefault(sessionId, _SessionState())` to atomically create or retrieve the _SessionState for a session in a single call. This replaces the earlier non-atomic check-then-create pattern (`if key not in self._locks: self._locks[key] = Lock()`) which had a TOCTOU race where two concurrent coroutines could both pass the check and each create a separate lock, losing the first one. dict.setdefault() is atomic in CPython (GIL-protected) and guarantees a single shared state per key.

The _SessionState dataclass (defined at line 34) has three fields: lock (asyncio.Lock, mutex for serial execution), waiters (int, count of in-flight tasks), and cancelled (bool, force-cancel flag). The setdefault pattern is used both in acquire() and forceCancel().

This applies to any dictionary used as a per-resource state holder in async contexts.

title: GC: collectOrphanContainers builds active-run set from scratch on every cycle
content: In `lib/sandbox/gc.py`, `collectOrphanContainers()` rebuilds the set of active run IDs from metadata on every GC cycle by iterating through all sessions and their runs. For large deployments with thousands of active runs, this adds O(n) overhead to every GC cycle where n = total active runs across all sessions. While correct for correctness (ensuring no active container is mistakenly GC'd), it's a performance concern as the number of concurrent sessions grows. A future optimization could cache the active run set with incremental invalidation rather than rebuilding from scratch each cycle.

title: Stale client cleanup: always clear self._client unconditionally, warn on partial failure
content:
In `DockerBackend._getClient()` (`lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py`), the stale client cleanup pattern was further refined. The previous fix (memory `232772f9`) tracked connectorClosed/clientClosed separately and only set `self._client = None` if at least one close succeeded, preserving the reference when both failed. 

This session changed that to always clear `self._client = None` unconditionally, with a warning if cleanup encountered errors:

```python
if not connectorClosed or not clientClosed:
    logger.warning("Stale client cleanup encountered errors; clearing client reference regardless")
self._client = None
```

Rationale: If both close operations fail, the client object is in an indeterminate state and keeping the reference is more dangerous than losing it — subsequent operations would try to use a broken client. Clearing it forces a fresh client creation on the next call. The warning preserves observability of the failure.

title: Timeout inspection try/except safety net after SIGKILL
content:
In `DockerBackend.runOneshot()` (`lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py`), the SIGKILL → container.show() path now includes a try/except safety net. Even with `await asyncio.sleep(0.1)` before inspection (memory `bb122ebd`), `container.show()` can still fail if the container exits/dies between the sleep and the API call.

The fix wraps the inspection in a try/except:

```python
try:
    inspectData = await container.show()
except Exception as exc:
    logger.warning("Failed to inspect container after timeout: %s", exc)
    return ContainerOutcome(
        containerId=container.id,
        exitCode=-1,
        signal="SIGKILL",
        oomKilled=False,
        inspects={},
    )
```

This ensures a graceful fallback `ContainerOutcome` is returned with exitCode=-1 and signal="SIGKILL" instead of an unhandled exception propagating up the call stack.

title: Docker timeout: None exitCode evaluates as != 0, incorrectly assigning SIGKILL signal
content:
In DockerBackend.runOneshot() (lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py, line 285), the timeout inspection path originally used `signal = "SIGKILL" if exitCode != 0 else None`, which incorrectly assigned signal="SIGKILL" when exitCode was None (since `None != 0` is True in Python).

**Fix applied**: Line 285 now uses `signal="SIGKILL" if exitCode is not None and exitCode != 0 else None`. This correctly handles three cases:
- exitCode is None → signal is None (no meaningful exit info, don't assume SIGKILL)
- exitCode is 0 → signal is None (container exited normally before inspect)
- exitCode is non-zero → signal is "SIGKILL" (signal killed the container)

The fix is at lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py line 285. This is distinct from the asyncio.sleep(0.1) mitigation (memory bb122ebd) and the try/except safety net (memory 42e007a5) — those address state stability; this addresses the logical comparison semantics.

title: FilesystemMetadataStore runtime value locks never cleaned — only session/run locks were fixed
content:
In lib/sandbox/metadata/filesystem.py, _getLock() is called with runtime.value (e.g., "python", "nodejs") in savePackagesInfo() (line 311) but not in loadPackagesInfo() (no lock there — read-only). The runtime-value lock is never explicitly cleaned up after the async with block in savePackagesInfo() (lines 304-316). There is no corresponding "delete packages info" method that would pop it.

However, _locks is a TTLDict (300s TTL, 600s GC timeout), so runtime locks auto-expire after ~15 minutes. The severity is lower than for session/run locks because package operations are infrequent. The actual remaining concern is the opposite risk (memory #3): if a package write holds the lock longer than 300s, TTLDict may GC the lock while it's still in use.

title: Docker stale client cleanup: connectorAttempted flag prevents false warnings
content:
In `DockerBackend._getClient()` (`lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py`), the stale client cleanup during connection re-establishment had a false-warning problem. The connector (`self._client.session.connector`) may not exist (be `None`), in which case no cleanup is attempted — but the `connectorClosed` flag remained `False`, triggering a spurious warning.

The fix added a `connectorAttempted` flag (initialized `False`, set to `True` only when connector actually exists and close is attempted), and split the single combined warning into two independent warnings:

```python
if connectorAttempted and not connectorClosed:
    logger.warning("Stale client cleanup: connector close failed")
if not clientClosed:
    logger.warning("Stale client cleanup: client close failed")
```

Previously the code used a single warning `"Stale client cleanup encountered errors; clearing client reference regardless"` gated on `if not connectorClosed or not clientClosed`, which fired even when no connector cleanup was needed.

This was a refinement of the existing cleanup pattern — the original logic (memory 37375b9f) tracked the right variables but didn't distinguish "cleanup not needed" from "cleanup attempted and failed."

title: TTLDict: extends Dict[K, V], not UserDict or MutableMapping
content:
The TTLDict class at lib/utils/ttl_dict.py extends Dict[K, V] (generic typing variant of dict), not UserDict or MutableMapping. This choice provides:

1. Drop-in dict replacement — all standard dict methods work without wrapping
2. Full generic type support: TTLDict[str, int] for type-safe usage
3. items(), keys(), values() are inherited from Dict[K, V] and already return correctly typed ItemsView[K, V], KeysView[K], ValuesView[V] — no explicit overrides needed, and none exist in the code. The generic parent class carries the correct type annotations through its TypeVars K and V.

Thread safety via RLock on all mutating operations. Sentinel _TTLDictSentinel / _UNSPECIFIED pattern to distinguish 'not provided' from ttl=None. Lazy expiration model — expired entries remain accessible until gc() runs (not auto-removed on read).

title: TTLDict: `d[key] = value, ttl=N` is invalid Python — use `d.set(key, value, ttl=N)`
content:
Python does not support `d["key"] = "value", ttl=120` as a way to pass keyword arguments. This evaluates as tuple assignment: `d["key"] = ("value", ttl=120)`, silently storing a tuple instead of applying a TTL.

In TTLDict, use `d.set(key, value, ttl=120)` instead. The `set()` method accepts an optional `ttl` keyword argument:
- `ttl=int` → entry expires in N seconds
- `ttl=None` → never expires (unless defaultTTL overrides)
- `ttl` omitted → uses `defaultTTL` (if set) or never expires

Standard `__setitem__` (`d[key] = value`) always uses `defaultTTL`.

title: TTLDict setdefault() bug: must respect defaultTTL, not set expiration=None
content:
When implementing TTLDict.setdefault(), there was a functional bug: setting `self._expirations[key] = None` (meaning "never expires"), but the method should respect `self.defaultTTL` just like `__setitem__` does.

The correct behavior:
- If `defaultTTL` is set (int), calculate `expiration = time.time() + self.defaultTTL` for new entries
- Only set `expiration = None` if `defaultTTL` is None (never expires by default)

This matches the principle that `setdefault()` for a non-existent key should behave identically to `__setitem__()`.

title: TTLDict update() must distinguish new vs modified keys for expiration
content:
When implementing TTLDict.update(), the initial code incorrectly handled expiration for all keys in the update set uniformly. The fix was to:

1. Capture `beforeKeys = set(self.keys())` before calling `super().update()`
2. Capture `afterKeys = set(self.keys())` after
3. Compute `newKeys = afterKeys - beforeKeys` and `modifiedKeys = beforeKeys & afterKeys`
4. For `newKeys`: set `expiration = None` (use defaultTTL latency — applied lazily)
5. For `modifiedKeys`: preserve their existing expiration state (don't reset it)

This ensures update() matches __setitem__ semantics — modifying an existing key preserves its original TTL, while adding a new key uses the default.

title: TTLDict sentinel pattern: _TTLDictSentinel and _UNSPECIFIED to distinguish absent from None
TTLDict code review findings: docstring syntax, update() semantics, coverage gaps
content:
Code review of TTLDict (lib/utils/ttl_dict.py, tests/lib/utils/ttl_dict_test.py) — findings status as of 2026-06-12:

**FIXED — Docstring syntax:** All docstrings now use correct `d.set(key, value, ttl=N)` syntax. The invalid `d["key"] = "value", ttl=N` pattern no longer appears anywhere in the file.

**FIXED — update() expiration semantics:** `update()` at lines 283-314 correctly distinguishes new vs existing keys using `beforeKeys = set(self.keys())` / `afterKeys = set(self.keys())` / `newKeys = afterKeys - beforeKeys`. Only new keys get expiration set; existing keys preserve their original expiration. Docstring at line 286 explicitly notes: 'NOTE: This method WILL NOT update TTL for existing keys'.

**STILL UNRESOLVED — Missing test coverage for update() expiration preservation:** No test verifies that updating an existing key via update() preserves its original TTL expiration. The closest test (`test_update_preserves_expiration_state` at line 458) tests that expired+re-added keys get a fresh expiration, but doesn't verify that a still-valid key's expiration is untouched after update().

**STILL UNRESOLVED — Performance documentation:** `__setitem__` (line 197) calls `self.set()` which triggers `gc(force=False)` on every write. In write-heavy workloads with many entries, this bypasses the GC timeout throttle since it runs per-write, not per-interval. Consider documenting this trade-off or adding a `gc=False` parameter to `set()` for batch operations.

**DONE WELL (still accurate):**
- Correct sentinel pattern (`_TTLDictSentinel` / `_UNSPECIFIED`)
- Thread safety with RLock on all mutators
- 56+ comprehensive tests
- Proper integration export from `__init__.py`
- Good edge case handling (ttl=0, ttl=None, negative TTL, GC timeout)
- Adherence to project conventions (camelCase, docstrings, type hints)

title: TTLDict update() must preserve expiration for existing keys
content: Before the fix, TTLDict.update() tracked all updated keys together (new + modified keys in one set), causing existing keys to lose their expiration state when updated. Fixed by capturing `set(self.keys())` before and after `super().update()`, computing `newKeys = afterKeys - beforeKeys`. New keys get `expiration=None` (uses `defaultTTL`). Modified keys preserve their existing expiration (matching `__setitem__` behavior which also preserves expiration for existing keys).

title: TTLDict docstrings: d.set(key, value, ttl=N), not tuple assignment syntax
content: All docstring examples in TTLDict must use `d.set(key, value, ttl=N)` syntax, not `d[key] = value, ttl=N`. The latter looks like Python tuple assignment but is invalid — TTLDict stores TTL via a separate `_expirations` dict, not through the key-value pair itself. Docstring examples must match the actual API.

title: Package spec parsing gaps: URL requirements, name validation, shell glob chars
content:
The sandbox package spec validation chain (`SandboxManager._validatePackageSpec()` at `lib/sandbox/manager.py:1150` → `PythonRuntime.validatePackageSpec()` at `lib/sandbox/runtimes/python/runtime.py:163`) has several gaps:

**Validation chain:**
1. Manager layer: checks for shell metacharacters (`& | ; \` $(\n\r`) and flag-like specs (`-prefix`)
2. Runtime layer: calls `Requirement(spec)` from `packaging.requirements` (PEP 508 parser)

**Known gaps:**
- **URL-based requirements** (`numpy @ https://example.com/numpy.tar.gz`) — parsed and accepted by `Requirement()`, but the URL is not validated. A malicious URL could serve arbitrary code during `pip install`.
- **Package name characters not enforced** — names like `n?mpy` or `n*m.py` (with shell glob chars) parse OK with `Requirement()` but may cause unexpected pip behavior.
- **No allowed-spec-type allowlist** — there's no explicit check that the spec is a plain package name with version constraints only (no URLs, no environment markers, no extras).
- **Editable installs** (`-e ./local_package`) — `Requirement()` does NOT accept these, so they're caught, but there's no explicit rejection with a clear error message.

**Hardening needed:**
1. Reject URL-based requirements (check for `@` followed by a URL scheme)
2. Validate package name with `re.match(r'^[a-zA-Z0-9._-]+$', name)` after parsing
3. Consider an allowlist of valid spec forms (name-only, name+comparison, name+version-range)
4. Add regression tests for all edge cases (URLs, glob chars, markers, extras, editable flags)

**Rough effort: ~3-5 hours, medium priority.** The `Requirement()` parser catches malformed specs well, but URL-based and dangerous-name cases are real security surfaces.

title: TTLDict items/keys/values absent — relies on Dict defaults (no explicit overrides)
content:
Per code review verification (session ses_14d1257d1ffeNdD5VliJSCElwR), TTLDict at lib/utils/ttl_dict.py does NOT have explicit items(), keys(), or values() method overrides. The class relies entirely on Dict's default implementations, which return correctly typed ItemsView[K, V], KeysView[K], and ValuesView[V] because TTLDict extends `Dict[K, V]` (generic dict).

This means:
- No `ItemsView`, `KeysView`, `ValuesView` imports needed — not present in the file
- No explicit method overrides — Dict's inherited methods are used
- `# type: ignore[override]` comments remain on `pop()` (line 218) and `setdefault()` (line 257) only — no such comments needed for items/keys/values since they aren't overridden

This is a valid design choice: explicitly overriding items/keys/values would be redundant since the generic parent already provides correct return types. But it differs from what some earlier session memories document (which describe explicit overrides being added).

title: ResourceLimits.memorySwapMb fix applied: docstring corrected, default changed to None, comments updated across 3 files
content:
The memorySwapMb inconsistency was fixed across three files. The fix aligns documentation and defaults with actual Docker MemorySwap semantics.

**Fix applied (2026-06-10):**

1. **lib/sandbox/types.py** — Fixed `ResourceLimits.memorySwapMb` docstring to correctly describe Docker semantics: "Total memory+swap limit in megabytes (Docker `MemorySwap`). When set, the effective swap available is `memorySwapMb - memoryMb`. Set equal to `memoryMb` to disable swap; set to `None` (default) to also disable swap." Changed default from `memorySwapMb: int | None = 512` to `memorySwapMb: int | None = None`. This preserves existing behavior (no swap by default) while making documentation match reality.

2. **lib/sandbox/manager.py** — In the two places where `ResourceLimits` is created for install containers (lines ~962 and ~1228), kept the explicit `memorySwapMb=installContainerConfig.memoryMb` assignment (disabling swap) but added clarifying comments: `# Set equal to memoryMb to disable swap (Docker MemorySwap == total limit)`. Decision: keep explicit value rather than changing to None, since the intent is to disable swap and the explicit assignment makes this clear.

3. **lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py** — Updated comment at the MemorySwap branch (line ~315) from "Disable swap entirely when memorySwapMb is None" to "Set MemorySwap equal to memory to disable swap (Docker MemorySwap == total limit)". The code itself was already correct (None path sets MemorySwap=Memory which disables swap); just the comment was misleading.

**Verification:** `make format lint` passes (0 errors), `make test` passes (2250 passed, 10 skipped), import succeeds, behavior confirmed correct.

title: envAllowlist removed from SecurityConfig — rationale and scope
content:
The `envAllowlist` field was removed from `SecurityConfig` (lib/sandbox/config.py) as confirmed dead code. Rationale from requirements: end users won't have access to the env dict, so validation/filtering at the config level provides no value.

The removal spanned three files:
1. **lib/sandbox/config.py** — removed field declaration, docstring reference, and `fromDict()` parsing
2. **configs/00-defaults/sandbox.toml** — removed `env-allowlist = []` key
3. **docs/plans/python-sandboxing-v1.md** — removed design doc mention

Previously documented (but now resolved) by existing memories: the field was defined and parsed from config but never consumed by `runCode` — the caller-provided `env` dict was passed through unconditionally.

title: Security Considerations section established in docs/llm/sandbox.md
content:
A dedicated "Security Considerations" section was added to docs/llm/sandbox.md, establishing the documentation pattern for sandbox security documentation. The first subsection, "Package installation is admin-only", documents that:

- End users cannot inject arbitrary package specs (URL-based, editable installs, etc.)
- Only bot administrators can install packages via bootstrap script or admin commands
- Three security layers protect the install flow:
  1. Input validation: `_validatePackageSpec()` rejects shell metacharacters and flag-like specs
  2. Controlled execution: The `pip install` command is constructed from pre-validated package names only
  3. Admin-only access: `installRuntimeLibraries()` is only called from admin contexts, never exposed to end-user code execution

A cross-reference from docs/llm/libraries.md section 11 (sandbox section) points to these security considerations.

This section was added between "Critical Coding Constraints" and "Testing" sections, placed after a horizontal rule separator. When adding future security documentation for sandbox features, place it within this section.

title: Sandbox bootstrap test: TOML config dir-mode must use 0o700 not 0700
content:
When writing integration tests for `scripts/sandbox_bootstrap.py` that use `buildConfig()` or `main()`, the sandbox config settings in test TOML files must use kebab-case keys (e.g. `dir-mode = "0o700"`, not `"0700"`). Python 3's `int(str, 0)` only auto-detects octal with the explicit `0o` prefix (PEP 3127 removed plain-leading-zero octal detection). The production config `configs/00-defaults/sandbox.toml` uses the `0o` prefix pattern.

Discovered when writing `test_buildConfigLoadsSandboxSettings` — the test attempted to use `"0700"` as the octal permission value but the production config uses `"0o700"`. Both `int("0o700", 0)` and `int("0700", 8)` work, but `int("0700", 0)` raises `ValueError` in Python 3.

Lessons applied from previous session knowledge (memories exist about the octal parsing bug and the storage root-dir requirement).

title: Config docs default for memory_swap_mb outdated after default changed to None
content: The `ResourceLimits.memorySwapMb` field default was changed from `512` to `None` in `lib/sandbox/types.py`, but `docs/llm/configuration.md:343` still shows the default as `512`. When synchronizing documentation after changing field defaults, the configuration reference docs (`docs/llm/configuration.md`) must be updated to match the new defaults.

title: fromDict memorySwapMb simplified: empty-string guard added
content:
The `ResourceLimits.fromDict()` method in `lib/sandbox/types.py` was simplified to fix a fragile pattern. The old code used `data.get("memory-swap-mb") is not None` to detect presence, which would pass an empty string `""`, causing `int("")` to raise `ValueError`.

New pattern:
```python
memorySwapMb = None
rawSwap = data.get("memory-swap-mb")
if rawSwap is not None and rawSwap != "":
    memorySwapMb = max(32, int(rawSwap))
```

This adds an explicit empty-string guard before the `int()` conversion. The `memorySwapMb = None` default is set upfront, and the condition uses both `is not None` and `!= ""` for robustness against both TOML-absent fields and empty-string edge cases.

title: HandlerResultStatus enum: return values that control message pipeline flow
content:
`HandlerResultStatus` (defined in `internal/bot/common/handlers/base.py:81-106`) is the return type of `newMessageHandler()` and controls the message processing pipeline in `HandlersManager._processMessageRec()`.

Enum values and their pipeline effect:
- **FINAL** = "final" — Message was fully processed; **stop the chain immediately** (no downstream handlers run). Use when a handler fully consumes the message (e.g., spam detected → stop).
- **SKIPPED** = "skipped" — Message is not applicable to this handler; **continue chain**. Default return value from `BaseBotHandler.newMessageHandler()`.
- **NEXT** = "next" — Handler processed the message; **continue chain** to downstream handlers. Use when handler acts but other handlers should still see the message.
- **ERROR** = "error" — Non-fatal failure; **continue chain** (the error is logged but doesn't block downstream handlers).
- **FATAL** = "fatal" — Unrecoverable error; **stop everything** (same as FINAL).

Helper methods:
- `isFinalState()` returns `True` for FINAL and FATAL — this is the check used in `_processMessageRec` to `break` out of the handler loop.
- `needLogs()` returns `True` for all statuses except SKIPPED — used by the handler manager to decide whether to log the handler result.

The iteration pattern in `_processMessageRec` (manager.py:964-983):
```python
for stepIndex, (handler, parallelism) in enumerate(self.handlers):
    # SEQUENTIAL handlers wait for previous SEQUENTIAL handler's rec step to finish
    if parallelism == HandlerParallelism.SEQUENTIAL and previousRec is not None:
        await previousRec.awaitStepDone(stepIndex)
    ret = await asyncio.wait_for(
        handler.newMessageHandler(ensuredMessage, updateObj),
        timeout=self.handlerTimeout,
    )
    if ret.isFinalState():
        break  # FINAL or FATAL stops the chain
```

title: Handler parallelism: SEQUENTIAL vs PARALLEL and iteration flow
content:
Each handler in the `HandlersManager` chain is registered as a `(HandlerClass, HandlerParallelism)` tuple pair (in `manager.py:425-534`).

`HandlerParallelism` enum has two values:
- **SEQUENTIAL** — The handler's `awaitStepDone()` must complete before the next SEQUENTIAL handler runs. This creates a dependency chain: message processor → spam → then SEQUENTIAL handlers wait for message processor's rec step. **Three** handlers use SEQUENTIAL: `MessagePreprocessorHandler` (index 0), `SpamHandler` (index 1), and `LLMMessageHandler` (last). Everything else uses PARALLEL.
- **PARALLEL** — The handler runs immediately without waiting for previous handlers' step completion. Most handlers (commands, weather, resender, divination, etc.) use PARALLEL since they don't depend on each other.

The iteration method is `_processMessageRec` (manager.py:964-983):
```python
for stepIndex, (handler, parallelism) in enumerate(self.handlers):
    match parallelism:
        case HandlerParallelism.PARALLEL:
            pass  # no wait
        case HandlerParallelism.SEQUENTIAL:
            if previousRec is not None:
                await previousRec.awaitStepDone(stepIndex)
    ret = await asyncio.wait_for(
        handler.newMessageHandler(ensuredMessage, updateObj),
        timeout=self.handlerTimeout,
    )
```

Key implication: PARALLEL handlers may run concurrently with the previous SEQUENTIAL handler's rec step. They run in sequence with each other (the loop is sequential), but they don't wait for the SEQUENTIAL pipeline to catch up. This design means PARALLEL handlers should not depend on side effects from preceding SEQUENTIAL handlers that complete after the PARALLEL handler runs.

When to use each:
- **PARALLEL** — Default. All command handlers, config-gated handlers, custom handlers. Use unless there's an explicit ordering dependency.
- **SEQUENTIAL** — Only when handler output affects a downstream handler. Currently: preprocessor, spam detection, and LLM catch-all.

title: Chat setting `long` description can trigger E501 line-too-long (120 chars) — must split string manually
content:
When adding a `_chatSettingsInfo` entry, the `long` field (verbose Russian description) frequently exceeds the project's 120-char line length limit (E501). Black does NOT auto-split string literals, so the agent must manually split the string across multiple lines using implicit string concatenation (adjacent string literals within parentheses).

Example fix (real case from `allow-sandbox` addition):
```python
ChatSettingsKey.ALLOW_SANDBOX: {
    "type": ChatSettingsType.BOOL,
    "short": "Включить песочницу для кода",
    "long": "Включает возможность выполнять код в изолированной среде (sandbox) в "
            "этом чате. Команды /run и /sandbox станут доступны.",
    "page": ChatSettingsPage.STANDART,
},
```

The `short` field (shorter Russian label) usually fits within 120 chars. Only the `long` field is affected. This must be done manually before running `make format lint`, or flake8 will fail.

title: HandlerResultStatus must be imported from internal.bot.common.handlers.base, not internal.bot.models
content:
When implementing a new bot handler, `HandlerResultStatus` is imported from `internal.bot.common.handlers.base` — NOT from `internal.bot.models`. The `internal.bot.models` package does not re-export it.

Correct import:
```python
from .base import BaseBotHandler, HandlerResultStatus
```

Wrong import:
```python
from internal.bot.models import HandlerResultStatus  # AttributeError: no such export
```

This is easy to get wrong because `ChatSettingsKey`, `CommandPermission`, `CommandCategory`, and other handler-related enums DO live in `internal.bot.models`. But `HandlerResultStatus` stays in `base.py` alongside `BaseBotHandler`.

title: getChatSettings() is a BaseBotHandler method, not on Database
content:
`getChatSettings(chatId)` is defined on `BaseBotHandler` (internal/bot/common/handlers/base.py:191), not on the `Database` class. Handlers call it via `self.getChatSettings(chatId)`.

The `Database` repository class does NOT have a `getChatSettings()` method. Attempting `self.db.getChatSettings(chatId)` will raise an AttributeError.

The BaseBotHandler method handles the full retrieval chain: cache check → DB fetch → default merging → tier filtering. It returns `Dict[ChatSettingsKey, ChatSettingsValue]` where `ChatSettingsValue = Optional[Tuple[Any, str]]` — access values via `.get(key, (default, None))[0]`.

title: RunInfo has no `error` field — only RunResult has it
content:
In the sandbox library (`lib/sandbox/types.py`), `RunInfo` (run metadata) does NOT have an `error` field. Only `RunResult` (the output of `runCode()`) has an `error` attribute.

The `RunInfo` dataclass fields are: `runId`, `sessionId`, `runtime`, `code`, `exitCode`, `stdout`, `stderr`, `createdAt`, `completedAt`, `status`. No `error` field.

The `RunResult` has: `exitCode`, `stdout`, `stderr`, `error` (str | None).

When iterating `manager.listRunsForSession()` results, each item is a `RunInfo` — access `runInfo.exitCode`, `runInfo.stdout`, `runInfo.stderr` directly (no `.error`). The `.error` field only appears on the `RunResult` returned by `manager.runCode()`.

title: SandboxHandler test fixture must patch SandboxConfig.fromDict and SandboxManager singleton
content:
When writing unit tests for SandboxHandler (in internal/bot/common/handlers/sandbox.py), the handler's __init__ passes the raw TOML config dict to SandboxManager.injectConfig(sandboxRaw). injectConfig() is a classmethod that internally calls SandboxConfig.fromDict(config) when passed a dict (see lib/sandbox/manager.py line 218). Therefore tests must patch SandboxConfig.fromDict — not because the handler calls it directly, but because injectConfig does.

The correct fixture pattern requires patching at three levels:

```python
with (
    patch("lib.sandbox.SandboxConfig.fromDict") as mockFromDict,
    patch("lib.sandbox.SandboxManager.injectConfig"),
    patch("lib.sandbox.SandboxManager.getInstance", return_value=mockManager),
):
    mockConfig = Mock(spec=SandboxConfig)
    mockFromDict.return_value = mockConfig
    handler = SandboxHandler(mockBot, sandboxConfigDict, mockConfigManager)
```

The mock SandboxConfig must have all nested config attributes that the handler accesses. Key ones to mock:
- mockConfig.storage.sessionDirDepth (default to 2)
- mockConfig.sessions.defaultTtlSeconds (default to 3600)
- mockConfig.sessions.maxSessionsPerUser (default to 3)
- mockConfig.storage.rootDir (string path)

Without these attribute mocks, tests will fail with AttributeError during handler construction or command execution. See tests/bot/test_sandbox.py for the complete working pattern.

title: Sandbox install command test needs isBotOwner mocked on bot
content:
The /sandbox install command in SandboxHandler calls self.isBotOwner(ensuredMessage.sender) which delegates to self._bot.isBotOwner(user=user) (see internal/bot/common/handlers/base.py line 446). When testing, isBotOwner must be explicitly mocked:

Mocking on the handler directly (preferred, simpler):
```python
handler.isBotOwner = Mock(return_value=True)  # admin test
handler.isBotOwner = Mock(return_value=False)  # non-admin test
```

Mocking on the mockBot fixture (alternative):
```python
bot = mockBot  # from conftest fixture
bot.isBotOwner = cast(Any, Mock(return_value=True))  # or Mock(return_value=False)
```

Note: isBotOwner is a synchronous method (not async), so use Mock not AsyncMock. The cast(Any, ...) is needed because the mockBot fixture has a specific type. This mocking is required in addition to the standard SandboxManager singleton mocking.

title: Config-gated handler registration requires `enabled` key in default config file
content:
When a handler is conditionally registered based on a config key (e.g., HandlersManager checks `configManager.get("sandbox", {}).get("enabled", False)`), the default config file in `configs/00-defaults/` MUST have the `enabled` key at the top level of that section. Without it, `configManager.get("sandbox", {}).get("enabled", False)` returns `False` (due to the fallback dict `{}`), and the handler is never registered.

Found during sandbox handler docs update: `configs/00-defaults/sandbox.toml` had `[sandbox]` section with subsections (`storage`, `backend`, `defaults`, `limits`, `features`, `commands`, `security`) but was missing `enabled = false` at the top level. The fix was adding `enabled = false` as the first key under `[sandbox]`.

This applies to any config-gated feature: division handler (checks `configManager.get("divination", {}).get("enabled", False)`), custom handlers (checks `configManager.get("custom-handlers", {}).get("enabled", False)`), sandbox, etc. The default config file must always have the `enabled` key for the gate to be meaningful.

title: SandboxHandler test mock: isBotOwner must mock handler method, not handler._bot
content:
**Convention (code review finding, since fixed in the test file):** When testing SandboxHandler install commands, mock `isBotOwner` directly on the handler, NOT on `handler._bot`.

**Right approach (currently in tests/bot/test_sandbox.py lines 603-614, 627-637):**
```python
handler._bot = Mock(spec=ExtBot)
handler._bot.id = 123456
handler.isBotOwner = Mock(return_value=True)   # mock the handler method
```

**Why this works:** `BaseBotHandler.isBotOwner()` (internal/bot/common/handlers/base.py:431-446) delegates to `self._bot.isBotOwner(user=user)`, but mocking `handler.isBotOwner` directly short-circuits that delegation. The `_bot` attribute needs to exist (SandboxHandler accesses `self._bot.id` in message formatting) but its `isBotOwner` method doesn't need mocking since `handler.isBotOwner` overrides it.

**Wrong approach (avoid):**
```python
handler._bot.isBotOwner = Mock(return_value=True)  # unnecessary indirection
```

Note: `isBotOwner` is synchronous (not async), so use `Mock` not `AsyncMock`. The `_bot` spec in tests is `ExtBot` for convenience (mockBot fixture type), not the actual `TheBot` type, but this is harmless since `handler.isBotOwner` is overridden.

title: Chat settings access: use direct indexing (chatSettings[key].toBool()), not .get()
content:
The established project pattern for accessing chat settings is direct dictionary indexing:

```python
chatSettings = await self.getChatSettings(chatId=chatId)
if not chatSettings[ChatSettingsKey.ALLOW_MENTION].toBool():
    return HandlerResultStatus.SKIPPED
```

The Phase 5 SandboxHandler code review found code using `.get()` instead:

```python
settings = await self.getChatSettings(ensuredMessage.recipient.id)
allowed = settings.get(ChatSettingsKey.ALLOW_SANDBOX)
if allowed is None or not allowed.toBool():
    return HandlerResultStatus.SKIPPED
```

While `.get()` is technically safe, it's inconsistent with the codebase pattern used by every other handler (media.py line 68, manager.py line 374, etc.). The `allowed is None` check implies uncertainty about key presence that shouldn't exist for properly configured chat settings. Use direct indexing `settings[ChatSettingsKey.X].toBool()` to match project conventions.

title: SandboxHandler output truncation: currentLength not updated between stdout and stderr sections
content:
In `internal/bot/common/handlers/sandbox.py`, the truncation logic in `run_command()` (around lines 683-755) limits sandbox output messages to 3000 characters (maxLength). The `currentLength` variable tracks total message length across sections.

**Current state (June 12 2026) — bug still present in stderr section**:

- `lines` list (line 673): static header lines "Exit code: ...", "Elapsed: ...", conditionally "Stdout:" + "```"
- Loop at line 687 builds `outputLines` from `lines`, tracking `currentLength`
- `codeBlockOpened` flag (line 694): set when `result.stdoutBytes > 0 and outputLines[-1] == "```"`
- After stdout snippet appended (line 704): `currentLength += len(stdoutSnippet) + 1` (line 705) — **updated** ✅
- After closing stdout fence: `currentLength += 4` (line 713) — **updated** ✅
- After stderr snippet appended (line 725): `currentLength` is **NOT updated** ❌ — the snippet uses `currentLength` in the slice calculation (`maxLength - currentLength - len(truncateMarker)`) but never increments it after appending.
- The stderr closing "```" fence (line 729) also does not update `currentLength`.
- The "Created files:" budget loop (lines 736-753) correctly updates `currentLength` per line, but starts from a stale value because the stderr section didn't update it.

**Impact**: When both stdout and stderr have significant output, the combined message length can exceed the 3000-character `maxLength` budget.

**Fix**: Add `currentLength` updates after the stderr snippet and its closing fence, matching the pattern used for stdout:
```python
outputLines.append(stderrText[: maxLength - currentLength - len(truncateMarker)] + truncateMarker)
currentLength += len(outputLines[-1]) + 1  # ADD: after stderr snippet
...
outputLines.append("```")
currentLength += 4  # ADD: after closing fence
```

title: RunResult has stdoutPath/stderrPath (file paths), not stdout/stderr directly
content:
`RunResult` (from `lib.sandbox.types`) does **not** have `.stdout` / `.stderr` string attributes. Instead, stdout/stderr output is redirected to files during execution, and `RunResult` has `.stdoutPath` / `.stderrPath` — relative workspace paths to the capture files (e.g., `.run/<runId>/stdout.log`).

To read the output content, call `SandboxManager.getInstance().readFile(sessionId, result.stdoutPath, maxBytes=...)` using the path from the RunResult. There is NO separate `SandboxStorage` class with `readRunStdout`/`readRunStderr` methods — `lib/sandbox/storage.py` contains only stateless utility functions (`sessionHash`, `resolveWorkspacePath`, `atomicWriteJson`, `ensureDirectoryLayout`).

The sandbox handler code (`internal/bot/common/handlers/sandbox.py`, `_llmToolRunSandboxCode`) demonstrates the correct pattern: read stdout via `manager.readFile(sessionId, result.stdoutPath, maxBytes=4096)`.

title: Two error-handling patterns for LLM tool handlers: return JSON error vs raise RuntimeError
Testing command gating: invoke command methods directly on handler instance
content:
When testing per-command access gating for handlers with `@commandHandlerV2` decorated methods, invoke the command methods directly on the handler instance in tests (e.g., `handler.run_command(em, "run", code, Mock(), None)`). This works despite commands being dispatched via the decorator mechanism in production.

Required test coverage for gated commands:
1. **Global not configured**: verify error message sent and execution skipped (mock/disable the global feature flag)
2. **Per-chat setting false**: verify `_checkSandboxAccess`-style helper returns False, sends chat-level error, and command does NOT proceed
3. **Per-chat setting true**: verify helper returns True and command proceeds to execute its core logic (e.g., `runCode` called)

Use the same shared fixtures (`testDatabase`, `mockBot`, `mockConfigManager`) and `_makeHandler` helper as other handler tests. Set up chat settings via `setChatSetting(chatId, ChatSettingsKey.ALLOW_SANDBOX, SettingValue(True/False), updatedBy="test")` before calling the command method.

This pattern was established when SandboxHandler's `run_command` and `sandbox_command` methods were tested after removing the ineffective `newMessageHandler` gating approach.

title: docs/llm/<library>.md files tend to miss bot integration documentation (commands, LLM tools, chat settings)
content:
When a complex library (lib/ subpackage) has a dedicated coding-patterns file under docs/llm/ (e.g., docs/llm/sandbox.md, docs/llm/spam.md), that file tends to document only the library API, coding patterns, and anti-patterns. It consistently misses the bot integration layer:

- Slash commands that wrap the library
- LLM tools that use the library
- Chat settings that gate the feature per-chat
- Cross-references to docs/llm/handlers.md and docs/llm/configuration.md

This was observed during the SandboxHandler documentation update (June 2026): sandbox.md existed for coding patterns but had no mention of the /run, /sandbox commands, the run_python LLM tool, or the allow-sandbox chat setting. These had to be added retroactively.

Checklist when adding a library-backed handler:
1. handlers.md — handler entry and chain order
2. index.md — handler count in file-listing table
3. docs/llm/<library>.md — bot integration section (commands, LLM tools, chat setting gating)
4. configuration.md — any new config keys (automatic per the skill, but verify)
5. developer-guide.md — any user-facing feature that needs setup/usage docs

Pattern: docs/llm/<library>.md = library API + coding patterns ONLY unless bot integration is explicitly added as a separate section.

title: Sandbox test: handler.sandboxEnabled must be set explicitly after _makeHandler
content:
When testing `SandboxHandler`, the `_makeHandler` helper constructs the handler from real dependencies and mocked config. The handler's `__init__` reads `self.sandboxEnabled` from config, but in test environments the mock config might not have the `sandbox` section, leaving `sandboxEnabled` unset.

The test helper was updated to accept a `sandboxEnabled` parameter (defaulting to `True`) and set `handler.sandboxEnabled` explicitly after construction. This avoids brittle config mocking for the handler's `__init__` and makes tests self-documenting about their gating expectations.

Usage pattern in tests:
```python
handler = await _makeHandler(..., sandboxEnabled=True)
# handler.sandboxEnabled is set explicitly after construction
```

This pattern should be followed for any handler test where the handler reads config in `__init__` that the test fixture doesn't fully mock.

title: Docker build result can be empty list — ret[-1] IndexError fix
content: In `lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py:217`, the code checks `if ret and "error" in ret[-1]` to detect Docker image build failures. The `ret and ...` truthiness guard handles both `None` and empty list `[]` (empty lists are falsy in Python), preventing `IndexError` on `ret[-1]`. The fix uses Python's truthiness check rather than the literal `len(ret) > 0` pattern — both are equivalent for empty-list protection, and the truthiness variant additionally guards against `ret = None`.

title: _tryParseJson method in LLMService recovers from LLM single-quote escaping quirks
content:
A new method `_tryParseJson(self, data: str) -> Tuple[Any, int]` was added to `LLMService` (internal/services/llm/service.py). It wraps `json.JSONDecoder().raw_decode()` with a fallback that replaces incorrectly escaped single quotes (`\'` → `'`) using regex `r"(?<=[^\\])\\'"`. This handles a common LLM quirk where models incorrectly escape single quotes inside double-quoted JSON strings.

The regex targets single-quotes preceded by a backslash that is itself not preceded by another backslash. If the fix attempt fails, the original exception is re-raised (not the fix's exception).

The method is called from three tool-call detection methods in the same file: `_matchTextForJSONToolCall`, `_matchTextForToolCallStart`, and `_matchTextForToolCallSquareBracketsAndJson`.

title: Sandbox network policy description: removed misleading line — always enabled, not configurable
content:
The sandbox tool description in `internal/bot/common/handlers/sandbox.py` was cleaned up:
1. Removed the comment `# "The code runs in an isolated environment with no network access by default. "` (was already dead code)
2. Removed the line `"Network access is available in the sandbox. "` from the tool description

Rationale: Network access is always hardcoded to `NetworkPolicy(enabled=True)` in the tool registration sites at lines 278 and 669. Since it's not configurable, describing it as a feature is misleading — users might expect to be able to control it. The description now simply states the sandbox executes code and preserves environments between calls.

title: Noisy debug logs in error recovery paths should be avoided
content:
Two instances of unnecessary debug logging in error recovery paths were identified and removed by code review:
1. `internal/services/llm/service.py:277` — Commented-out `# logger.warning(f"'resultText'")` was removed
2. `internal/services/llm/service.py:207` — A debug log `logger.debug("Trying JSON fix: replaced escaped single quotes")` was added by the software-developer agent during error formatting standardization, but was flagged by code review as noisy: it would fire on every JSON decode error, generating excessive log volume in production when LLMs produce malformed JSON.

Lesson: Debug logs in error recovery paths should only be added if they provide genuine diagnostic value that outweighs the log spam. The fix paths are already logged at warning level on failure, making additional debug logging redundant.

title: _tryParseJson does NOT handle single-quoted JSON property names — only escaped \' inside valid double-quoted JSON
content:
The `_tryParseJson` error recovery in LLMService only handles the case where an LLM incorrectly uses `\'` (escaped single quote) inside an otherwise valid double-quoted JSON string (e.g., `{"text": "It\'s a test"}`). The regex `r"(?<=[^\\])\\'"` targets `\'` sequences that are NOT preceded by another backslash, and replaces them with `'`. 

It does NOT handle malformed JSON that uses single quotes for property names or string delimiters (e.g., `{'text': 'value'}`). Such input will raise `JSONDecodeError` with no recovery attempt. This is a behavioral boundary that must be understood when testing or extending the method — attempting to test "single-quote JSON recovery" with single-quoted property names will fail, leading to confusing test results.

title: String escaping in Python test data for \' recovery tests is error-prone — use raw strings
content:
When writing Python tests that exercise the `_tryParseJson` `\'` → `'` recovery logic, constructing test strings with the correct escaping is extremely error-prone. For example, to represent the JSON string `{"text": "don\'t"}` (with a literal `\'` that the method should fix), the Python string must contain the literal characters `\'`. Using raw strings like `r'{"text": "don\\\'t"}'` or carefully constructed double-quoted strings is necessary.

The safest approach seen in practice: store the test inputs as Python raw strings or construct them with explicit byte-level escaping. The test file `tests/t_internal_llm_try_parse_json.py` demonstrates the correct patterns. A quick sanity check with `print(repr(inputStr))` before writing assertions can prevent confusion between Python string escaping and the JSON escaping the method operates on.

title: Co-located tests under internal/ subdirectories fail — use tests/ only
content: Co-located tests under `internal/services/llm/tests/` do NOT work — pytest cannot resolve imports because `internal/` is not a package in a way that allows subdirectory imports. Attempting `from internal.services.llm.service import LLMService` from a test file at `internal/services/llm/tests/test_*.py` fails. All LLM service tests MUST live under the root `tests/` directory. The AGENTS.md states tests for `internal/X/Y.py` go in `tests/X/test_Y.py` (stripping `internal/`), but in practice the naming `tests/t_internal_*_test_desc.py` has been used instead of the documented directory-mirroring convention. Check for existing naming patterns before deciding on test file location.

title: _tryParseJson confusing exception handling: inner except logs e2 but outer reraises original
content:
In `LLMService._tryParseJson()` (internal/services/llm/service.py), the error recovery logic has a confusing exception handling pattern:

- The inner `except json.JSONDecodeError as e2:` block catches the error from the regex-based fix attempt and logs `logger.warning(f"JSON fix failed: {e2!r}")`.
- The outer `except:` then reraises the *original* exception (from the first `json.JSONDecoder().raw_decode()` call), not `e2`.

This is misleading: the logged warning shows the fix-attempt failure (`e2`), but callers see the original decode error. Anyone debugging from logs would see "JSON fix failed: ..." and assume that's the error that propagated, when in fact a different error was the actual cause of the failure.

**Correct pattern options:**
1. Reraise `e2` (the meaningful error) and update the comment: `raise e2  # reraise the fix-attempt error`
2. Store the original exception explicitly and reraise it with clear naming
3. Use `raise` (bare) which reraises whatever is active — in this context it's tricky because `e2` is the last exception caught, not the original

The confusing naming/comment pattern was flagged in code review but not fixed before the session ended.

title: docker.py _buildImage includes all files in Dockerfile directory (no .dockerignore filtering)
content: In `lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py:196-201`, the `_buildImage` method creates a tar archive of the Dockerfile's parent directory using `contextDir.iterdir()` without filtering out files like `.git`, `__pycache__`, `node_modules`, or `.DS_Store`. This means the entire directory contents (including potentially large hidden directories) are sent to the Docker daemon as build context. Consider using a `.dockerignore`-like filter or only including the Dockerfile explicitly. This was flagged during code review as a potential performance and security concern for sandbox image builds.

title: Correction: TTLDict.set(key, value, ttl=None) left stale expiration behind
content:
In `lib/utils/ttl_dict.py`, method `TTLDict.set()` previously only updated `_expirations[key]` when the new TTL was not `None`. If a key already had an expiry and was then rewritten with `ttl=None` (or via `defaultTTL=None` + no explicit ttl), the old expiry remained in `_expirations`. This meant the entry would expire at the old deadline even though the caller intended it to never expire.

Fixed by adding `else: self._expirations.pop(key, None)` after the `if actualTTL is not None:` branch. Two regression tests added: `test_set_ttl_none_clears_previous_expiration` (explicit `ttl=None`) and `test_set_default_ttl_none_clears_previous_expiration` (via `defaultTTL=None` fallback).

title: ConfigManager.get() docstring falsely claims dot-notation support
content:
In `internal/config/manager.py`, method `ConfigManager.get()` has a docstring (around line 271) stating "Supports dot notation for nested keys" with examples like `config_manager.get("bot.token")` and `config_manager.get("database.type", "sqlite")`. However, the implementation at `return self.config.get(key, default)` is a plain `dict.get()` that does NOT traverse dotted paths — it looks for the literal key string in the top-level dict.

This misleading docstring was the root cause of the sandbox bootstrap bug: `configManager.get("sandbox.bootstrap", {})` always returned the default `{}` because `"sandbox.bootstrap"` is not a key in the top-level config dict. The correct pattern for nested config access is: `configManager.get("sandbox", {}).get("bootstrap", {})`.

Suggestion: either fix the implementation to support actual dotted-key traversal, or fix the docstring to say `"Does NOT support dot notation — use chained .get() calls for nested access, e.g. config.get('sandbox', {}).get('bootstrap', {})."`

title: DockerBackend watchdog timeout must accommodate inner runtime timeout + grace period
content:
In `lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py`, the `DockerBackend.runOneshot()` method's `asyncio.wait_for(container.wait(), timeout=...)` was originally set to `spec.limits.timeoutSeconds`. But the `PythonRuntime.runCommand()` already wraps execution in coreutils `timeout -s TERM -k <grace> <timeoutSeconds>` which handles graceful TERM → grace → KILL sequence and produces exit code 124 on timeout.

When the outer `wait_for` timeout matched the inner `timeoutSeconds`, the backend would fire first, SIGKILL the container, and the exit code would be 137 (128+9) instead of 124 — making the `timedOut` flag wrong and ignoring `timeoutGraceSeconds`.

Fix: set the watchdog timeout to `timeoutSeconds + timeoutGraceSeconds + 1` so the inner `timeout` wrapper always fires first and handles the full TERM→grace→KILL cycle. The backend `wait_for` serves as a last-resort watchdog only. Documented as constraint 11 in `docs/llm/sandbox.md`.

This is an evolution of the original fix (adding `wait_for` at all); the original formula using only `timeoutSeconds` was correct in intent but too tight in practice.

title: Package metadata must be refreshed after successful library install
content:
In `lib/sandbox/manager.py`, `installRuntimeLibraries()` was installing packages on disk via Docker but never updating the persisted package metadata (`packages.json`) that `listRuntimeLibraries()` reads. This meant `/sandbox install ...` would succeed, but `run_python(packages=[...])` would report the packages as missing because the metadata store was still empty.

Fixed by calling `await self._refreshPackageList(runtime, libsDir)` after a successful install, wrapped in a `try/except` so that a transient metadata refresh failure (e.g., I/O error writing `packages.json`) is logged but does NOT convert a successful install into an exception for the caller. The `_refreshPackageList` call runs inside the existing `poolLock` context manager, preventing concurrent install races.

Also: `SandboxManager.recover()` already calls `_refreshPackageList` for each runtime (line 1121), so package metadata is reconciled on startup recovery.

title: Sandbox lifecycle integration: DO_EXIT shutdown + startup recovery via cron
content:
The `SandboxHandler` in `internal/bot/common/handlers/sandbox.py` was previously missing lifecycle integration with the bot's startup/shutdown. Two additions:

1. **Shutdown (`DO_EXIT` handler)**: Registered `DelayedTaskFunction.DO_EXIT` → `_dtOnExit`, which calls `SandboxManager.getInstance().shutdown(cleanVolumes=False)`. Without this, the sandbox backend was never closed on bot shutdown, leaving orphaned containers and Docker API connections.

2. **Startup recovery**: Added a one-time `_recoveryDone` flag. On the first cron tick, before running GC, the handler calls `SandboxManager.getInstance().recover()` to reconcile stale containers, sessions, and run records from a crash. Recovery runs only once per process lifetime.

The `_dtOnExit` method should guard against being called when sandbox is disabled (defensive check), though in practice it's only registered when sandbox is enabled.

title: Gate 2 whole-work review: focus on cross-subtask interaction, not re-reviewing individual fixes
content:
Process pattern learned from the python-sandboxing fix pass: after running multiple independent fix subtasks in parallel (each with its own Gate 1 review), the Gate 2 whole-work review should focus specifically on:

1. **Cross-subtask interactions**: Do fixes in different files conflict? Do they create ordering dependencies? Do they leave intermediate inconsistent states?
2. **Pre-existing vs introduced issues**: Explicitly distinguish issues that existed in the original branch from issues introduced by the fix subtasks. The final synthesis must call out which are pre-existing to avoid confusion.
3. **Update docs with new gotchas and patterns**: After each fix pass, update `docs/llm/tasks.md` (gotchas section), `docs/llm/sandbox.md` (new constraints), `docs/llm/handlers.md` (lifecycle info), and `docs/llm/libraries.md` (new utility docs).

Key learning: The Gate 2 reviewer in this pass flagged several "issues" that were actually pre-existing in the original branch (truncated UUIDs, semaphore internals, N+1 GC queries) — these were not regressions from the fixes. Without explicit pre-existing vs introduced distinction, the synthesis would be misleading. Always document when a bug was already present and not introduced by the current fix pass.

title: Code-reviewer findings verification protocol: always verify against actual code
content: Before dispatching fixes based on a code-reviewer report, always verify each finding against the actual code working tree. In a June 2026 review of the `python-sandboxing` branch, the code-reviewer flagged 6+ issues as critical/important (bootstrap config lookup, docker timeout semantics, package metadata refresh, DO_EXIT lifecycle, TTL dict stale expiration, status run sorting). ALL were already correctly implemented in the actual working tree. The reviewer may have been working from a stale diff, misread the code, or the branch already had fixes applied. Lesson: never assume review findings are accurate — read the actual code for each flagged issue before delegating fixes.

title: Sandbox errors RuntimeError renamed to SandboxRuntimeError
content: The `lib/sandbox/errors.py` module originally had a `RuntimeError` class that shadowed Python's built-in. This was renamed to `SandboxRuntimeError` to avoid the shadowing hazard. `lib/sandbox/__init__.py` exports `SandboxRuntimeError` (not `RuntimeError`). The built-in `RuntimeError` is used in `lib/sandbox/manager.py` for `injectConfig` guard errors and session creation failures (not imported from sandbox.errors).

title: Documentation drift: docs/llm/configuration.md sandbox section had snake_case keys, phantom rows, wrong defaults
content:
The `docs/llm/configuration.md` sandbox section (lines ~304-416) had significant drift from the actual `configs/00-defaults/sandbox.toml` and `fromDict()` parsers:

1. **All config keys used snake_case** (`root_dir`, `base_url`, `run_image_tag`, etc.) but the actual config/parser uses kebab-case (`root-dir`, `base-url`, `run-image-tag`). Fixed ~30+ keys to kebab-case across all subsections.

2. **Two phantom rows existed** that don't appear in the actual TOML or `fromDict()` schema:
   - `runtime` row under `[sandbox.defaults]` — removed.
   - `interval_seconds` row under `[sandbox.gc]` — removed.

3. **Default values were wrong** for `dir-mode` and `file-mode`: docs said `"0700"`/`"0600"` but the actual TOML uses `"0o700"`/`"0o600"` (Python-style octal notation).

4. **`starter_packages`** under `[sandbox.bootstrap]` was correctly left as snake_case — it's a raw TOML key (array of strings), not processed by `fromDict()`, so kebab-case is wrong there.

This was a docs-only fix; no code changes were needed. Root cause: the sandbox section was written before the actual config schema was finalized, and never reconciled with the real TOML file.

title: DockerBackend watchdog: timeoutGraceSeconds=0 edge case makes buffer very tight
content: The `DockerBackend.runOneshot()` watchdog formula `spec.limits.timeoutSeconds + spec.limits.timeoutGraceSeconds + 1` has a tight edge case when `timeoutGraceSeconds=0`. In that case, the watchdog becomes `timeoutSeconds + 0 + 1 = timeoutSeconds + 1` — only 1 second of buffer on top of the inner coreutils `timeout -k 0` command (which sends SIGKILL immediately upon timeout with no grace period). While the system handles this correctly (the `except asyncio.TimeoutError` fallback sends SIGKILL and inspects the container), scheduling jitter or Docker API latency could cause the outer `wait_for` to fire before the inner `timeout` wrapper's SIGKILL takes effect, making it more likely to hit the fallback SIGKILL path instead of the graceful exit path. This is a design consideration, not a defect — the fallback path works correctly — but callers should be aware that setting `timeoutGraceSeconds=0` makes the two-layer timeout defense significantly tighter.

title: TTLDict setdefault() has latent stale-expiration pattern (consistency gap with set())
content:
During code review of the TTLDict stale expiration fix for `set()`, the `setdefault()` method was identified as having the same structural pattern as the fixed `set()` bug -- but currently unreachable:

```python
# setdefault() at lib/utils/ttl_dict.py:273-281:
if isinstance(self.defaultTTL, int):
    self._expirations[key] = time.time() + self.defaultTTL
# No else branch -- no cleanup of stale expirations
```

This is safe **today** because `setdefault()` guards with `if key in self: return self[key]`, so it only inserts entries for keys that don't yet exist. There is no stale `_expirations` entry to clear for a non-existent key.

However, this creates a consistency trap for future maintainers:
- `set()` explicitly cleans up stale expirations when `actualTTL is None` (via `else: self._expirations.pop(key, None)`)
- `setdefault()` silently leaves any hypothetical stale expiration in place (though currently unreachable)

Recommendation: Add a defensive `else: self._expirations.pop(key, None)` to `setdefault()` mirroring `set()` to make the invariant self-documenting: "when `actualTTL` is `None`, the key must not appear in `_expirations`." This is optional polish, not a current bug.

title: installRuntimeLibraries() _refreshPackageList call needs try/except to prevent failure propagation
content:
In `SandboxManager.installRuntimeLibraries()` (`lib/sandbox/manager.py`), the `_refreshPackageList()` call after a successful install was unprotected. If the metadata refresh failed (e.g. disk I/O error, corrupt packages.json), the exception would propagate out of `installRuntimeLibraries()`, converting an otherwise successful package install into a failure for the caller.

The fix: wrap the `_refreshPackageList()` call in try/except, logging the failure via `logger.exception()` without changing the return value. The install itself succeeded — a failed metadata refresh is non-fatal.

This is the error-handling layer on top of the previously documented fix (memory `7367d38b`) that added the `_refreshPackageList()` call in the first place.

title: TTLDict storing asyncio.Lock objects risks GC'ing active locks in FilesystemMetadataStore
content:
In `lib/sandbox/metadata/filesystem.py:28`, `FilesystemMetadataStore` uses `TTLDict[str, asyncio.Lock]` for per-key write locks:
```python
self._locks: TTLDict[str, asyncio.Lock] = TTLDict[str, asyncio.Lock]().setDefaultTTL(300).setGCTimeout(600)
```

This has a subtle correctness risk: if a write operation takes longer than 5 minutes, the lock entry could be garbage-collected by TTLDict.gc(), and a subsequent write to the same key would get a different `asyncio.Lock` object, defeating the mutual exclusion purpose. Holding the lock does NOT refresh the TTL.

This is a theoretical concern for typical sandbox operations (which complete in seconds), but could affect long-running operations like large package installs. Recommended: use a plain `dict[str, asyncio.Lock]` instead for this use case, or refresh the TTL when acquiring the lock.

title: Bug: manager.py timedOut must check both exitCode 124 AND signal SIGKILL
content: In `lib/sandbox/manager.py` line 637, the `timedOut` determination for run results originally only checked `outcome.exitCode == 124`. This misses the case where the Docker backend's own `asyncio.wait_for()` fires before the container's `timeout` wrapper — in that case the container is SIGKILL'd and `exitCode` is typically 137 (or None), not 124. The fix adds `or outcome.signal == "SIGKILL"` to the `timedOut` check, so timeout detection works regardless of which layer triggers the kill.

title: hasImage: use images.inspect() with DockerError catch, not any([...]) over images.list()
content:
In `lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py` line 148, the `hasImage` check originally used:
```python
hasImage = any([imageTag in image.get("RepoTags", []) for image in await client.images.list()])
```
This is inefficient (lists ALL images, builds intermediate list) and semantically weak (substring match in `RepoTags`). Fixed to use targeted inspection:
```python
try:
    await client.images.inspect(imageTag)
    hasImage = True
except aiodocker.DockerError:
    hasImage = False
```
Catch `aiodocker.DockerError` (not broad `Exception`) to follow existing file pattern and avoid swallowing unrelated failures like network errors.

title: ResourceLimits.fromDict() clamps timeoutSeconds to minimum 30, logs warning
content:
In `lib/sandbox/types.py` lines 109-112, `ResourceLimits.fromDict()` clamps `timeoutSeconds` to a minimum of 30 and logs a `logger.warning` when clamping occurs, informing the operator that their configured value was below the minimum.

The code:
```python
timeoutSeconds = int(data.get("timeout-seconds", 30))
if timeoutSeconds < 30:
    logger.warning("Configured timeout-seconds=%d is below minimum 30, using 30", timeoutSeconds)
    timeoutSeconds = 30
```

The dataclass field default is also 30 (line 71). `fromDict({})` produces 30 (not 60) — the default value in `data.get("timeout-seconds", 30)` matches the dataclass default. The clamp only fires when a configured value is explicitly below 30.
