title: resolveWorkspacePath normalizes absolute paths instead of rejecting them
content: `resolveWorkspacePath()` in `lib/sandbox/storage.py` now **normalizes** absolute paths rather than rejecting them. Normalization rules: `/workspace/...` → strip `/workspace` prefix; `/workspace` or `/workspace/` → empty string (workspace root); `/other/absolute` → strip leading `/`. Security is preserved via `resolve()` + `relative_to()` jail check AFTER normalization — traversal attacks like `/workspace/../../etc/passwd` (becomes `../../etc/passwd`) are still caught. The old `path == "/"` special case in `listFiles` was removed — `resolveWorkspacePath` now handles all paths uniformly. Both `listFiles` and `readFile` use `Path(record.workspacePath)` without redundant `.resolve().absolute()`, relying on `resolveWorkspacePath` for resolution.

title: RunResult.workDir: workspace-relative path, optional with default "", placed at end of dataclass
content:
As of June 2026, `RunResult.workDir` in `lib/sandbox/types.py` has the following final shape:

**Field definition (end of dataclass, after `error`):**
```python
@dataclass(slots=True)
class RunResult:
    # ... all other required fields ...
    error: Optional[str]
    workDir: str = ""   # LAST field, has default
```

**Why at the end with default `""`:**
- `@dataclass(slots=True)` requires default-valued fields to come after all required (no-default) fields.
- An earlier iteration made `workDir` required (no default) placed between `sessionId` and `runtime`. This broke external code that constructs `RunResult` directly without `workDir`.
- The final fix moved it to the end with `= ""` so existing call sites need no update.

**Path convention — workspace-relative, not host-side:**
`SandboxManager.runCode()` (lib/sandbox/manager.py:684) sets:
```python
workDir=f".run/{runId}/work"   # workspace-relative, same pattern as stdoutPath/stderrPath
```
NOT `workDir=str(workDirPath)` (the host-side absolute path like `/var/lib/.../workspace/.run/<uuid>/work`).

Using a workspace-relative path means callers can pass `result.workDir` directly to `SandboxManager.listFiles()` and `readFile()`. Note: `resolveWorkspacePath()` now **normalizes** absolute paths (strips `/workspace` prefix or leading `/`) rather than rejecting them, but workspace-relative paths are still the intended convention.

**fromDict() backward compat:** uses `data.get("workDir", "")` — old serialised data without the key deserialises correctly.

**Test helper:** `tests/bot/test_sandbox.py::_makeRunResult` already has `workDir: str = ""` keyword arg (no change needed there).

**Docstring on the field:** "Workspace-relative path to the per-run working directory, e.g. '.run/<runId>/work'."

title: Divination golden data JSON files have stale module paths after tests/→tests/lib/ reorganization
content:
All 5 golden data JSON files under `tests/lib/divination/golden/data/` still contain the stale module path `"tests.divination.golden.scenario_runner"` in their `"module"` field. The correct path should be `"tests.lib.divination.golden.scenario_runner"`.

Files affected (verified June 14, 2026):
- `runes_three_norns_health_ru.json`
- `runes_nine_grid_life_ru.json`
- `tarot_celtic_cross_relationship_ru.json`
- `tarot_three_card_career_ru.json`
- `tarot_yes_no_job_en.json`

The `git mv` of `tests/divination/golden/` → `tests/lib/divination/golden/` moved the Python files but the JSON data files were not updated to reflect the new module path. This causes golden test scenarios to fail to import the runner module. Fix: update the `"module"` field in each JSON file from `"tests.divination.golden.scenario_runner"` to `"tests.lib.divination.golden.scenario_runner"`.

This is a separate issue from the directory-move gotcha (which only covered the need to `git mv` data/ and input/ dirs explicitly) — even after the directories are correctly placed, the JSON files' internal paths are stale.

title: gc.py:81 F821 lint issue confirmed resolved — code is clean in current state
content:
Investigation of the pre-existing F821 warning at `lib/sandbox/gc.py:81` (recorded in memory 7ab174be) has been completed. The current code is lint-clean:

- Line 68: `for session in await self._metadata.loadAllSessions():` — `session` is properly bound as the loop variable.
- Line 81: `logger.debug("GC: error processing session %s: %s", session.sessionId, exc)` — `session.sessionId` is a valid attribute access on the loop variable. `exc` is bound by `except Exception as exc:` on line 80.
- No F821 or any other lint error is present in this file.

The earlier F821 report was likely a transient artifact (e.g., from a mid-edit state) or from a different version of the file. The current code is verified correct.

title: OpenRouter test gap: HTTP-Referer URL value not asserted, only header key presence checked
content:
Two OpenRouter tests (`testOpenrouterGenerateTextWithExtraHeaders` at line 404 and `testGenerateStructuredHappyPath` at line 978 in `tests/lib/ai/providers/test_openrouter_provider.py`) check for HTTP-Referer and X-Title headers, but only assert KEY PRESENCE (`"HTTP-Referer" in headers`), never the actual URL VALUE.

This means:
- The test passes even if the URL is wrong, stale, or empty.
- If someone changes the URL in production (`lib/ai/providers/openrouter_provider.py` line 172: `"HTTP-Referer": "https://notacompany.org/products/gromozeka"`), no test catches it.
- The production URL was changed from `https://sourcecraft.dev/notacompany/gromozeka` in the past, and an unexplained gap remains.

Tests affected:
- `testOpenrouterGenerateTextWithExtraHeaders` (lines 441-442)
- `testGenerateStructuredHappyPath` (line 1024)

Both should be hardened to assert the actual expected URL value, not just header existence.

title: Memory system baseline statistics (June 14, 2026): 1,623 memories by type/topic
content:
A comprehensive audit of the memory system on June 14, 2026 established a baseline for all 1,623 memories across the gromozeka project.

**Total:** 1,623 memories

**By type:**
- gotcha: 400 (24.6%)
- architecture: 348 (21.4%)  
- convention: 309 (19.0%)
- bugfix: 304 (18.7%)
- correction: 172 (10.6%)
- decision: 66 (4.1%)
- missing/no type label: 18 (1.1%)

**By topic (top 10):**
- testing: 69
- opencode-agent-config: 52
- documentation-drift: 51
- sandbox: 43
- divination-system: 43
- sandbox-docker: 42
- sandbox-manager: 36
- sandbox-testing: 29
- lib-ai-structured-output: 26
- sandbox-locks: 24

**Path coverage:** 358 memories (22%) have no path label, indicating cross-cutting or meta-topical observations.

**Top memory-dense files:**
- lib/sandbox/manager.py: 77 memories
- lib/ai/providers/basic_openai_provider.py: 55
- lib/proxy/__init__.py: 41
- lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py: 40
- internal/services/llm/service.py: 39
- lib/ai/abstract.py: 36

**Quality assessment (based on ~32% sampling / 523 memories read directly):**
- ~97.7% kept as accurate or still relevant
- 12 deleted (stale/obsolete references to removed code)
- 18 replaced with updates for accuracy
- 7 new memories added for findings discovered during verification

**Highest-drift area:** Proxy subsystem (memories referenced removed free functions `resolveProxyConfig`, `buildHttpxClientKwargs` and removed `_globalProxy` attribute)

**Correlation:** 18 memories with no type label also had no topic label, suggesting systematic gaps in tooling-enforced label requirements during memory creation.

title: Memory audit workflow at scale: sampling + parallel code verification
content:
For auditing a large memory set (1,623 memories) against actual code, the following methodology was used successfully:

**Phase 1 — Full inventory and categorization:** Load all memories from `.opencode/memories-for-review.jsonl`. Analyze distribution by type, topic, and path to identify dominant clusters.

**Phase 2 — Topic-grouped sampling:** Read ~30%+ of memories across all regions of the file (start, middle, end) to cover all major topic groups. Sampling across file regions is critical to avoid missing recently-added memories.

**Phase 3 — Parallel code verification:** Dispatch multiple subagents in parallel, each responsible for one topic area:
- Each subagent reads the relevant source files directly (not memories)
- Each subagent verifies key claims found in memories against actual code with line-number evidence
- Subagents cover non-overlapping areas (handler code, locks/lifecycle, proxy, config/testing etc.)

**Phase 4 — Decision synthesis:** For each memory, determine:
- KEEP: claim is accurate or still relevant
- DELETE: refers to removed code/patterns, is superseded, or false
- REPLACE: has factual inaccuracies but the topic is still relevant

**Phase 5 — Write artifact:** Output structured JSON with per-memory decisions, new memory creation for findings.

**Key insight:** Memory drift concentrates in subsystems that underwent class-based refactoring (e.g., proxy free functions → ProxyConfig class). Subsystems with no recent refactoring typically have high memory accuracy. This workflow differs from a documentation sync because verification targets claims against live code, not just textual drift analysis.

title: Correction: GromozekBot._shutdown() missing hasattr guard is NOT a defect — try/except provides same safety
content:
During a memory audit, a verification subagent incorrectly flagged `GromozekBot._shutdown()` in `main.py` (line 104) as having a "latent defect" because it accesses `self.llmManager` directly without a `hasattr(self, "llmManager")` guard, claiming that if `__init__` fails before `self.llmManager` is assigned (line 66), the `finally` block in `run()` would raise `AttributeError` and skip database cleanup.

**This is incorrect. The existing code is safe because:**

1. The `_shutdown()` method wraps `self.llmManager.aclose()` in `try/except Exception` (lines 103-106). `AttributeError` is a subclass of `Exception`, so it is caught and logged.
2. Database cleanup at lines 108-111 runs in its OWN separate `try/except Exception` block, independent of the LLM Manager cleanup success. 
3. Even if both `self.llmManager` and `self.database` don't exist (partial init failure), both errors are caught, logged, and `_shutdown()` completes without re-raising.

**The full safety chain:**
- `llmManager` not assigned → `AttributeError` on line 104 → caught by `except Exception` on line 105 → logged → continues to line 108
- `database` not assigned → `AttributeError` on line 109 → caught by `except Exception` on line 110 → logged → method returns normally

**Lesson:** Subagent verification can produce false positives when it assumes a defensive pattern is necessary but the existing error handling already covers the case. Always verify subagent defect claims by tracing the full exception handling chain before acting on them.

title: TOML duplicate key causes entire config layer to be silently ignored
content: `configs/common/01-bot-defaults.toml` had duplicate `condensing-model` keys (both `"openrouter/qwen3.5-flash"` and `"yc/deepseek-v4-flash"`). TOML spec forbids duplicate keys; `tomli` rejects the entire file with a parse error. Since `ConfigManager._loadConfig()` had a `try/except Exception` that silently swallowed per-file parse errors, the **entire** `common/` config layer was ignored at startup — no error, no crash, just a log warning. This caused the bot to run with only `00-defaults/` defaults, where `chat-model = "openrouter/free"` instead of the intended `"aliceai-llm"` from the common layer.

title: Config file parse errors now fatal — sys.exit(1) instead of silent skip
content:
`ConfigManager._loadConfig()` was changed so that config file parse/merge errors are no longer silently skipped. Previously the per-file loop had `try/except Exception` that logged and continued. Now the inner try/except is removed; exceptions propagate to the outer handler which calls `sys.exit(1)`. Both the main config file and per-directory file loads use `logger.exception(...); raise` to log the failing file path before exiting. This was a user-requested change to surface config issues early at startup rather than having the bot run with silently missing config layers.

Files changed:
- `internal/config/manager.py` — removed inner try/except in `_loadConfig()` per-file loop; added `logger.exception(...); raise` for both main config and per-file loads.
- `tests/config/test_manager.py` — `testInvalidTomlInConfigDir` updated from asserting successful skip to asserting `pytest.raises(SystemExit)`.

title: _findTomlFilesRecursive scan errors now re-raised instead of swallowed
content: `_findTomlFilesRecursive()` in `internal/config/manager.py` was catching exceptions from `Path.rglob()`, logging them, and returning whatever partial file list it had. This meant a filesystem/permission error during config directory scanning could silently skip part of a configured config layer — same class of failure as the TOML parse issue. Changed to `logger.exception(...); raise` so scan errors are also fatal at startup. Non-existent and non-directory paths still skip silently (intentional behavior for optional config directories).

title: Per-environment CONFIGS values and full config directory chain
content:
Each `.env.*` file defines the `CONFIGS` env var that selects which config directories are loaded (in order, after `00-defaults`):

| Environment | CONFIGS value | Full chain |
|---|---|---|
| `.env` (default) | `local-common local` | 00-defaults → local-common → local |
| `.env.local-telegram` | `common local local-telegram` | 00-defaults → common → local → local-telegram |
| `.env.local-max` | `common local local-max` | 00-defaults → common → local → local-max |
| `.env.prod-telegram` | `common prod prod-telegram` | 00-defaults → common → prod → prod-telegram |
| `.env.prod-max` | `common prod prod-max` | 00-defaults → common → prod → prod-max |

The `run.sh` script in scripts/_load_env.sh defaults CONFIGS to "local" if unset, then builds `--config-dir ./configs/$v` flags for each space-separated value. Earlier directories are loaded first; later directories override earlier ones via deep recursive merge.

title: Per-tier default model assignments (chat-model, fallback-model, etc.)
content:
Each tier has specific default model assignments defined in `[bot.tier-defaults.<tier>]` sections of `configs/common/01-bot-defaults.toml` (which fully override the stubs in `configs/00-defaults/bot-defaults.toml`). These determine which models are used by default for each purpose per tier:

**Default tier (fallback when no tier-specific override):** chat-model="aliceai-llm", fallback-model="openrouter/deepseek-chat-v3.1", summary-model="yc/deepseek-v4-flash", summary-fallback-model="openrouter/qwen3.5-flash", image-parsing-model="yc/qwen3.6-35b-a3b", image-parsing-fallback-model="openrouter/qwen3.6-plus", image-generation-model="aliceai-image-art", image-generation-fallback-model="openrouter/gemini-2.5-flash-image", condensing-model="yc/deepseek-v4-flash"

**free tier (default for groups):** chat-model="openrouter/free", fallback-model="aliceai-llm-flash", summary-model="openrouter/free", summary-fallback-model="yc/deepseek-v4-flash", image-parsing-model="openrouter/free", image-parsing-fallback-model="yc/qwen3.6-35b-a3b", image-generation-model="aliceai-image-art", image-generation-fallback-model="yandex-art", condensing-model="openrouter/free". Also sets use-tools=false, parse-attachments=false, save-attachments=false, allow-tools-commands=false.

**free-personal tier (default for private chats):** chat-model="aliceai-llm-flash", fallback-model="openrouter/free", summary-model="yc/deepseek-v4-flash", summary-fallback-model="openrouter/qwen3.5-flash", image-parsing-model="yc/qwen3.6-35b-a3b", image-parsing-fallback-model="openrouter/qwen3.5-flash", image-generation-model="aliceai-image-art", image-generation-fallback-model="yandex-art", condensing-model="yc/gpt-oss-20b"

**friend tier:** only overrides allow-sandbox=true. All model settings fall through to [bot.defaults] (i.e. friend-tier uses the same models as the base default tier).

**banned tier:** disables everything (use-tools=false, parse-attachments=false, save-attachments=false, allow-tools-commands=false, delete-denied-commands=false, allow-mention=false, allow-reply=false, random-answer-probability=0.0, admin-can-change-settings=false)

**bot-owner tier:** no explicit tier-defaults block, falls through entirely to [bot.defaults].

IMPORTANT: The model assignments in `[bot.tier-defaults.<tier>]` are independent from the `tier` tag on each model definition. A model tagged `tier="friend"` is available for manual invocation by friend-tier users, but it does NOT automatically become the default model for that tier — the default is set by the `chat-model` key in the tier defaults block.

title: Model→tier tag mapping for all models in merged config
content:
Every model definition in the TOML configs has a `tier` tag that controls which tier of user can manually invoke it. The merged config (after applying all layers) yields this mapping:

**yc-openai provider models:**
- aliceai-llm: tier=paid, enabled=true
- aliceai-llm-flash: tier=free-personal (overridden from paid in common/00-config.toml), enabled=true
- yandexgpt: tier=friend, enabled=false
- yandexgpt-lite: tier=paid, enabled=false
- yc/gpt-oss-120b: tier=paid, enabled=true (implicit)
- yc/deepseek-v32: tier=paid, enabled=false (deprecated)
- yc/deepseek-v4-flash: tier=paid, enabled=true (implicit)
- yc/gpt-oss-20b: tier=paid, enabled=true (implicit)
- yc/qwen3-235b-a22b-fp8: tier=friend, enabled=true (implicit)
- yc/qwen3.6-35b-a3b: tier=paid, enabled=true (implicit)
- aliceai-image-art: tier=paid, enabled=true (implicit)

**openrouter provider models:**
- openrouter/claude-haiku-4.5: tier=bot-owner, enabled=false (too expensive for chat bot)
- openrouter/deepseek-chat-v3.1: tier=paid, enabled=true (overridden in common/10-openrouter.toml)
- openrouter/deepseek-v4-flash: tier=paid, enabled=true (overridden in common/10-openrouter.toml)
- openrouter/qwen3-235b-a22b: tier=paid, enabled=false (disabled in common/10-openrouter.toml)
- openrouter/qwen3.5-flash: tier=paid, enabled=true (overridden in common/10-openrouter.toml)
- openrouter/qwen3-vl-235b-a22b-instruct: tier=paid, enabled=false (disabled in common/10-openrouter.toml)
- openrouter/qwen3.6-plus: tier=paid, enabled=true (overridden in common/10-openrouter.toml)
- openrouter/gemini-2.5-flash-image: tier=friend, enabled=true (overridden in common/10-openrouter.toml)
- openrouter/gemini-3-pro-image-preview: tier=friend, enabled=true (overridden in common/10-openrouter.toml)

**openrouter/free special model:**
- openrouter/free: tier=free (no tier tag in config, defaults to "free" in code), enabled=true

**yc-sdk provider models:**
- yandex-art: tier=paid, enabled=true (implicit)
- yc-yandexgpt: tier=paid, enabled=false (too slow)
- yc-yandexgpt-lite: tier=paid, enabled=false (too slow)
- yc-aliceai-llm: tier=friend, enabled=false (too slow)

Models tagged `tier=friend` (yc/qwen3-235b-a22b-fp8, openrouter/gemini-2.5-flash-image, openrouter/gemini-3-pro-image-preview) are available to friend-tier users for manual model selection but are NOT the default models for that tier (friend tier falls through to [bot.defaults] model assignments).

Models tagged `tier=bot-owner` (openrouter/claude-haiku-4.5) are available only to bot owners but is disabled.

Model tier tag is defined per-model in the individual TOML files (yc-openai-models.toml, yc-sdk-models.toml, openrouter-models.toml). The token `"free"` in openrouter-models-free.toml has no explicit tier tag, so it gets the Python-side default "free" from LLMManager.

title: getChatTier() resolution chain: Paid overrides Base, both feed into BANNED fallback
content:
`BaseBotHandler.getChatTier(chatSettings)` (internal/bot/common/handlers/base.py:339-357) determines the effective tier for a chat using this priority chain:

1. **Paid tier** — checked first: if both `PAID_TIER` and `PAID_TIER_UNTILL_TS` are present in `chatSettings` AND `PAID_TIER_UNTILL_TS >= time.time()`, that tier wins.
2. **Base tier** — fallback: if `BASE_TIER` is set in `chatSettings`, use that value.
3. **None** — if neither is present, returns `None`. The caller (`getChatSettings()` at line 264) then falls back to `ChatTier.BANNED`, but only after also checking `ChatSettingsKey.BASE_TIER` in `defaultSettings` (line 260-261).

Important: `getChatTier()` does NOT consult the cache's default settings itself — the caller (getChatSettings) passes in the already-merged chatSettings dict which may or may not include defaults. The method only looks at what's directly in the passed dict.

This means tier can be overridden by:
- A paid subscription (time-limited, from DB)
- A config-assigned base tier (from per-chat DB settings or defaults)
- The `BANNED` fallback if neither is set

title: ProxyConfig.__repr__ password masking uses 'REDACTED' not '***' (correction to stale memories)
content:
`ProxyConfig.__repr__` in `lib/proxy/__init__.py` at line 211 masks non-empty passwords as `'REDACTED'`, not `'***'`:

```python
f"password={'REDACTED' if self.password else self.password!r}, "
```

This was verified against the current code on disk. Two existing memories — `a59adc8d` (topic: proxy-test-coverage) and `59947729` (topic: proxy-repr-fstring-parsing) — factually incorrectly state that `__repr__` uses `'***'` for password masking. The memories' core observations remain valid (test coverage gap for repr output edge cases; the gotcha about `!r` applying to the entire ternary expression in f-strings), but the specific literal string referenced is stale/wrong.

The `getProxyURL(maskPassword=True)` method also uses `"REDACTED"` (confirmed in memory d4e71a4a), so `'REDACTED'` is the consistent masking string across the ProxyConfig API.

title: Per-chat setting tier filtering rules: bot-owner bypass and model tier cross-check
content:
In `BaseBotHandler.getChatSettings()` (internal/bot/common/handlers/base.py:282-304), after merging defaults and loading per-chat settings, each setting undergoes tier-based filtering before being included in the final result:

**Skip condition 1 — setting's own tier requirement:**
For each setting `k`, `settingsInfo[k]["page"].minTier()` determines the minimum tier required to use that setting. If that minTier is higher than the chat's current tier, the setting is skipped — UNLESS:
- `v.updatedBy == 0` (the setting was set by the system/default, which counts as bot-owner level), OR
- `self.isBotOwner(MessageSender(v.updatedBy, "", ""))` returns True

**Skip condition 2 — model tier cross-check (for MODEL/IMAGE_MODEL settings):**
If the setting type is `ChatSettingsType.MODEL` or `ChatSettingsType.IMAGE_MODEL`, the raw string value (model name) is looked up via `llmManager.getModelInfo(v.toStr())`. If `modelInfo` is None (unknown model), the setting is skipped. Otherwise, the model's declared tier is checked via `ChatTier.fromStr(modelInfo.get("tier", ""))`. If that model tier is higher than the chat's current tier AND `isSetByBotOwner` is False, the setting is skipped.

**Bot owner detection:**
`isSetByBotOwner` is True when `v.updatedBy == 0` OR `self.isBotOwner(MessageSender(v.updatedBy, "", ""))` is True. This means bot-owner-set settings bypass ALL tier restrictions (both the setting's own minTier and the model tier check).

These rules ensure that paid-tier features are invisible to free-tier chats, and that model settings pointing to premium-only models are silently dropped for lower tiers — preventing confusing error messages.

title: Layer-by-layer config override specifics for model/tiar settings
content:
The config system has 4 logical layers for prod-telegram (00-defaults → common → prod → prod-telegram). Each layer has specific responsibilities for model and tier settings:

**Layer 1: configs/00-defaults/ (base defaults)**
- Defines all model definitions (provider, model_id, temperature, context, support_*, tier, enabled)
- Defines model providers (yc-openai, openrouter, yc-sdk) in providers.toml
- Has stub `[bot.tier-defaults.free]` with only commented-out settings
- Has stub `[bot.tier-defaults.free-personal]` with `base-tier = "free-personal"`
- Sets default model assignments using openrouter/free and aliceai-llm-flash as primary models

**Layer 2: configs/common/ (common overrides)**
- **Fully defines all tier defaults**: free, free-personal, friend, banned blocks in 01-bot-defaults.toml
- **Overrides default model assignments** from openrouter/free to aliceai-llm as the primary chat model
- **Overrides aliceai-llm-flash tier** from "paid" to "free-personal" in 00-config.toml
- **Enables/disables specific openrouter models** in 10-openrouter.toml (enables deepseek-chat-v3.1, deepseek-v4-flash, qwen3.5-flash, qwen3.6-plus, gemini images; disables claude-haiku-4.5, qwen3-235b-a22b, qwen3-vl models)
- Sets secrets via ${VAR} substitution in 99-secrets.toml

**Layer 3: configs/prod/ (production overrides)**
- Does NOT override model assignments from common layer — the common layer's model settings survive
- Overrides only the `chat-prompt` (Disgaea penguin persona)
- Adds proxy routing per provider in 20-proxy.toml
- Sets sandbox docker backend config in 20-sandbox.toml
- ollama provider is entirely commented out (no effect)

**Layer 4: prod-telegram (Telegram-specific overrides)**
- Sets localMode=true and baseUrl for local bot API server in 00-config.toml
- Enables Grabliarium custom handler with its own AI model assignments:
  - ai-model = "yc/qwen3-235b-a22b-fp8" (friend-tier model)
  - ai-fallback-model = "openrouter/qwen3.5-flash" (paid-tier model)
- Sets Grabliarium-specific DB config

Key principle: model assignments in later layers only override if they explicitly define the same key. Since prod/01-bot-defaults.toml does NOT define model assignments (only prompt), the common layer's model assignments survive into the final merged config for prod.

title: Two independent tier mechanisms: model access tier vs per-tier default model assignments
content:
The project has TWO independent tier mechanisms that are often confused:

1. **Model tier tag** (each model's `tier` field in config): Controls which user tiers can manually invoke that model. A model tagged `tier="friend"` means only friend-tier users can select it via `/model` commands or tool calls. This is enforced by LLMManager when resolving model requests. Models tagged `tier="paid"` are available to paid/friend/bot-owner users. Models tagged `tier="free"` are available to everyone.

2. **Per-tier default model assignments** (`[bot.tier-defaults.<tier>]` sections): Controls which model is used BY DEFAULT for each purpose (chat-model, fallback-model, summary-model, etc.) for users in that tier. For example, `[bot.tier-defaults.free]` sets chat-model="openrouter/free" but that does NOT mean openrouter/free is tagged tier=free (it is, coincidentally). The friend tier has NO model overrides — it falls through to [bot.defaults] which uses aliceai-llm, a paid-tier model.

These are orthogonal: you can have a paid-tier model as the default for free-tier users (if it's cheap), and you can have friend-tier models that are never used as defaults (they're just available for manual selection). The model tier tag controls ACCESS, the tier defaults block controls DEFAULT SELECTION.

Grabliarium is a notable exception: it has its OWN model assignments (ai-model, ai-fallback-model) in configs/prod-telegram/grabliarium-defaults.toml, independent of the main tier system. It uses a friend-tier model (yc/qwen3-235b-a22b-fp8) as its primary and a paid-tier model (openrouter/qwen3.5-flash) as fallback.

title: Dual-level tier gating in getChatSettings: settings page tier + model config tier
content:
The `getChatSettings()` tier filtering in `BaseBotHandler` (base.py:278-301) applies TWO independent tier checks when merging per-chat settings, not just one:

1. **Settings page tier** (line 287): `settingsInfo[k]["page"].minTier()` — whether the chat's tier is high enough to access the settings page that the setting belongs to. If `chatTier < pageTier` and the setting wasn't set by a bot owner, the setting is dropped (falls back to default).

2. **Model-level tier** (lines 291-299): For MODEL-type and IMAGE_MODEL-type settings, an additional check reads the `tier` field from the model's config entry in `[models.models.<name>]` TOML tables:
   ```python
   modelInfo = llmManager.getModelInfo(v.toStr())
   modelTier = ChatTier.fromStr(modelInfo.get("tier", ""))
   if not isSetByBotOwner and (modelTier is None or not chatTier.isBetterOrEqualThan(modelTier)):
       continue  # Drop this setting, fall back to default
   ```
   This means even if a chat passes the page-level tier check, the specific model it selected must also be at or below the chat's tier.

This dual check is also enforced in the configuration UI (`configure.py:721-724`) when a user selects a model — the validation rejects model selections whose `tier` is too high for the chat's tier.

**Model config `tier` field**: Each model entry in `configs/00-defaults/*-models.toml` has a `tier` field (e.g., `openrouter/free` → `tier = "free"`, `aliceai-llm` → `tier = "paid"`, `aliceai-image-art` → `tier = "paid"`). This determines which chat tiers can use the model, NOT rate limiting as the docs claim.

**Implication**: A model's tier affects availability in two distinct places:
- Default resolution (no per-chat override): the model set in `[bot.defaults]` or `[bot.tier-defaults.<tier>]` is purely a config setting — any model name can be set, regardless of its tier.
- Per-chat override: when the user explicitly selects a model for their chat, the model's `tier` is validated against the chat's tier at settings-merge time.

**Note**: The existing config.md documentation at `docs/llm/configuration.md:187` describes the `tier` field as "Access tier for rate limiting" which is inaccurate — it controls model access gating, not rate limiting.

title: Config scan errors must re-raise after logging (not just log and continue)
Config parse errors must log the failing file path before re-raising
content:
In `ConfigManager._loadConfig()`, when iterating over TOML files from config directories, each file must be wrapped in a try/except that logs the file path and re-raises. The correct pattern is:

```python
for tomlFile in tomlFiles:
    try:
        with open(tomlFile, "rb") as f:
            dir_config = tomli.load(f)
        config = self._mergeConfigs(config, dir_config)
    except Exception:
        logger.exception(f"Failed to load config file {tomlFile}")
        raise
```

The key requirement is that the log message includes the file path (`{tomlFile}`) — without it, when a file fails to parse, the operator has no way to know which file caused the failure. The original code (pre-2026-06-12) silently swallowed these errors. The 2026-06-12 fix made them fatal but the code review on 2026-06-14 caught that the failing file path wasn't being logged. The pattern is catch → log file path → re-raise (never swallow).

title: Test gap: no regression test for duplicate-key TOML errors in config
content:
The config error-handling fix made TOML parse errors (including duplicate keys) fatal at startup via `sys.exit(1)`. The existing test `testInvalidTomlInConfigDir` in `tests/config/test_manager.py` asserts SystemExit for invalid TOML syntax, but does not cover the specific scenario that caused the original bug: a duplicate key (e.g., two `condensing-model` assignments in one table). A dedicated regression test with a duplicate-key fixture is needed to pin the exact regression.

File: `tests/config/test_manager.py` lines ~907-923
Severity: Recommendation — existing test covers the general class of errors but not the specific reported bug.

title: `TestErrorHandling` class docstring still says "without crashing" — outdated after config fix
content:
The `TestErrorHandling` class in `tests/config/test_manager.py` has a docstring that says errors are handled "without crashing". After the config error-handling fix (parse errors now cause `sys.exit(1)`), this docstring is no longer accurate for invalid TOML scenarios. The docstring was written for the old behavior where parse errors were silently skipped.

File: `tests/config/test_manager.py` lines ~887-890
Severity: Minor documentation drift

title: `ConfigManager.__init__` Raises section missing fatal config parse/merge failures
content:
The `Raises:` section of `ConfigManager.__init__()`'s docstring in `internal/config/manager.py` does not mention that config file parse errors or merge failures are now fatal (raised from `_loadConfig()` and caught by the outer handler which calls `sys.exit(1)`). The constructor can now exit on invalid TOML or merge errors, but this isn't documented in the Raises section.

File: `internal/config/manager.py` lines ~119-122
Severity: Minor documentation drift

title: Config: unresolved ${ENV_VAR} placeholders still pass as valid config values — silent substitution failure
content:
After the config error-handling fix making TOML parse errors fatal, `${...}` environment variable placeholders that fail to resolve (missing env var, missing dotenv file) still bypass startup validation. The mechanism has three layers of silent tolerance:

1. `load_dotenv()` in `lib/utils/utils.py` logs an error when the `.env` file doesn't exist but returns an empty dict — execution continues.
2. `replaceMatchToEnv()` in `internal/config/manager.py` leaves `${VAR}` unchanged when the env var is missing — no warning, no rejection.
3. `getBotToken()` in `internal/config/manager.py:404` only rejects `""` and `"YOUR_BOT_TOKEN_HERE"`, but not `"${BOT_TOKEN}"`.

Consequence: if `.env` is missing or `BOT_TOKEN` is unset, `bot.token` stays as the literal string `"${BOT_TOKEN}"`. The bot starts up and only fails later at bot application init (when it tries to use the token). This partially defeats the goal that "config issues surface immediately at startup."

The shipped defaults (`configs/00-defaults/00-config.toml:8`) use `token = "${BOT_TOKEN}"`, which is normally resolved from `.env`, so this is a realistic failure mode.

Suggestion: after substitution, scan required config fields (at least `bot.token`) for the unresolved placeholder pattern `\$\{[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_-]*\}` and reject/fail if found.

title: Corrected model resolution: common config overrides global default to aliceai-llm, friend tier inherits it
content:
When tracing model resolution for a specific config stack, you must check ALL config layers in the correct merge order.

For the "common+prod+prod-telegram" stack, the merge order is: `00-defaults` → `common` → `prod` → `prod-telegram`.

**Effective `chat-model` resolution per tier** (with common config merged):

| Layer | chat-model | Source |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| Global default (after common override) | `"aliceai-llm"` | `common/01-bot-defaults.toml:29` overrides `00-defaults/bot-defaults.toml:145` (`"openrouter/free"`) |
| `free` tier | `"openrouter/free"` | `common/01-bot-defaults.toml:91` — explicit override |
| `free-personal` tier | `"aliceai-llm-flash"` | `common/01-bot-defaults.toml:104` — explicit override |
| `friend` tier | **inherits global default** = `"aliceai-llm"` | `common/01-bot-defaults.toml:115-116` — only `allow-sandbox = true`, NO `chat-model` override |
| `banned` tier | inherits global default | `common/01-bot-defaults.toml:63` — only `admin-can-change-settings`, no model overrides |

`prod/01-bot-defaults.toml` and `prod-telegram/` configs do NOT override `chat-model`.

**Key takeaways for debugging model resolution:**
1. Don't assume `00-defaults` values are final — `common` (and later layers) override them.
2. A tier with NO `chat-model` override inherits the global default AFTER all config layers are merged.
3. If a friend-tier chat gets `"openrouter/free"` despite the above, check: per-chat DB setting (`chat_settings` table), wrong tier resolution (`BASE_TIER` vs actual), or a different config chain.

**Common footgun**: The `free` tier's `chat-model = "openrouter/free"` in `common/01-bot-defaults.toml:91` can be confused with the global default from `00-defaults/bot-defaults.toml:145`. They have the same value but different scopes — one is tier-specific, the other is global.

title: No regression test for _findTomlFilesRecursive exception path (directory scan errors)
content:
The `_findTomlFilesRecursive()` bug fix (exceptions from `Path.rglob()` now re-raised after logging instead of swallowed) has no regression test. All existing `TestErrorHandling` tests cover:

- `testInvalidTomlSyntax` — main config file parse error
- `testInvalidTomlInConfigDir` — config-dir file parse error
- `testNonExistentConfigDirectory` — missing dir (skipped, no crash)
- `testConfigDirIsFile` — non-directory path (skipped, no crash)

None of these force `Path.rglob()` to raise an exception (e.g., via monkeypatch or mock) to verify the new re-raise behavior in `_findTomlFilesRecursive()` at `internal/config/manager.py:168-170`.

Per project convention ("Regression tests on every bug fix"), this is a coverage gap. A targeted test should patch `Path.rglob` to raise an `OSError` and assert `SystemExit`.

title: testPermissionError is effectively a no-op — doesn't simulate permission errors
content: The `TestErrorHandling.testPermissionError` test in `tests/config/test_manager.py` (line 955) creates a normal config file and just verifies the manager can be created. It does not actually simulate a permission error (e.g., by setting file permissions to unreadable). The test's own docstring note acknowledges this: "This test is platform-dependent and may not work on all systems. Just verifies the manager can be created." The test name is misleading — it does not test the permission-error path at all. Consider either removing the test, renaming it to clarify it's a smoke test, or implementing a proper permission-error simulation with os.chmod + cleanup that works on the target platform.

title: Main config file load failure logging IS correctly implemented — review claimed otherwise in error
content: A subsequent code-review session incorrectly claimed that the main config file in `ConfigManager._loadConfig()` doesn't log the failing file path (citing line 236 of `internal/config/manager.py`). This was a false positive — lines 237-243 already wrap the main config file load in a try/except that calls `logger.exception(f"Failed to load main config file {configFile}")` and re-raises, exactly mirroring the per-dir-file pattern at lines 253-261. Both the main config and config-dir file paths are now correctly logged on failure.

title: Three separate truncation limits in sandbox handler: maxBytes=4096 (output reads), maxLength=3000 (message budget), maxBytes=3000 (user read)
content:
The sandbox handler (`internal/bot/common/handlers/sandbox.py`) uses three distinct truncation limits with different scopes. They are easily confused because two share the numeric value 3000 but serve different purposes:

1. **`maxBytes=4096`** (lines 315, 326, 752, 774) — Byte limit for reading stdout/stderr output files from the sandbox backend via `manager.readFile()`. Applied per-stream (stdout and stderr each read up to 4 KB). This is a FILE READ limit, not a message size limit. It controls how much of the output capture file is loaded into memory.

2. **`maxLength=3000`** (line 738) — Character budget for the CONSTRUCTED MESSAGE that `run_command()` builds from stdout, stderr, and created-files sections. Not related to file reads. The `currentLength` variable tracks cumulative character count across message sections. When stdout + stderr content exceeds this budget, sections are truncated with a `... (truncated)` marker.

3. **`maxBytes=3000`** (line 1020) — Byte limit for the `/sandbox read` command, which reads an arbitrary workspace file at the user's request. This is separate from both output-read logic and the message budget. Only applies to the command handler, not the LLM tool versions.

Key insight: The value 3000 appears in two unrelated places (message budget and user read command) with different meanings (characters vs bytes). The 4096 value for stdout/stderr reads is not defined as a named constant — it's a bare magic number.

title: _llmToolRunSandboxCode return dict has undocumented keys: files, stdout-len, stderr-len
content:
The `_llmToolRunSandboxCode()` method in `internal/bot/common/handlers/sandbox.py` returns a dict with several keys that are NOT documented in its docstring (lines 234-247). The docstring only documents: `done`, `exitCode`, `elapsedMs`, `stdout`, `stderr`, `errorMessage`, `oomKilled`, `timedOut`, `signal`.

Missing from the docstring:
- `"files"` (line 344): List of dicts with `path`, `sizeBytes`, `isDirectory` — conditionally added when `result.workDir` exists and `listFiles()` returns non-empty results.
- `"stdout-len"` (line 319): `int` — `result.stdoutBytes`, set when stdout text is non-empty. Raw byte count before maxBytes=4096 truncation.
- `"stderr-len"` (line 331): `int` — `result.stderrBytes`, set when stderr text is non-empty. Raw byte count before maxBytes=4096 truncation.

These keys are useful for the LLM caller to understand full output sizes beyond the truncated text. An agent relying only on the docstring will miss them. The docstring should be updated to include these.

title: workDir subdirectory created with mkdir only — no os.chmod applied, unlike workspacePath
content:
In `SandboxManager.runCode()` (lib/sandbox/manager.py, lines 568-570), the per-run `work/` subdirectory is created with plain `mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)`:

```python
workDirPath = runDir / "work"
workDirPath.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
```

**No `os.chmod(workDirPath, self._config.storage.dirMode)` is called.** This contrasts with the parent `workspacePath` which DOES have its permissions reset on every `runCode()` call (line 564: `os.chmod(workspacePath, self._config.storage.dirMode)` — see memory 0b0befa8 for that fix).

This means `work/` inherits the process's umask permissions at creation time, not the strict `dirMode` configured in storage config. If the parent workspace directory was created with stricter permissions (via chmod), the `work/` subdirectory may have different (potentially looser) permissions since `mkdir` does not inherit parent directory permissions on most Unix filesystems — it uses the process umask.

This is a potential security/permissions gap: if `config.storage.dirMode` is set tighter than the default umask, the per-run work directory could be more accessible than intended. The workspacePath chmod resets permissions on every use as a deliberate security measure (memory bfcf88f8), but workDirPath is excluded from that treatment.

**Implication for future fixes:** If `work/` needs restricted permissions, add `os.chmod(workDirPath, self._config.storage.dirMode)` after the `mkdir` call in `runCode()`, matching the pattern used for workspacePath.

title: No end-to-end integration test verifying user files land in workDir after container execution
content:
The existing sandbox tests for workDir behavior (`tests/lib/sandbox/test_manager.py`) all **mock the Docker backend** (`manager._backend = _makeMockBackend(mockOutcome)`), so they verify:

- Directory creation on the host filesystem (`workDirPath.is_dir()`)
- Path format (`workDir == f".run/{runId}/work"`)
- Workspace-relative path invariants (no leading `/`, starts with `.run/`, ends with `/work`)

But **no test** actually writes a file inside a real Docker container and confirms it appears in `workDir` on the host filesystem. The round-trip tests (`test_types_roundtrip.py`) only verify serialisation, not file placement.

This means the following behaviors are untested end-to-end:

1. That the `cd /workspace/.run/{runId}/work &&` prefix in the shell command (runtime.py:80) actually causes the container process's CWD to be the per-run work directory.
2. That files created by user code (e.g., `open("output.txt", "w")` inside the container) appear on the host under the correct `workDir` path.
3. That `workDir` on the host can be successfully scanned by `listFiles()` after the container finishes.

A true end-to-end test would require a real Docker daemon available in the test environment. The Colima/Docker sharing gotcha (memory 1d8c717d) means the test tmp_path must be under a home directory for Docker-on-Mac compatibility, which complicates setup. See `tests/lib/sandbox/test_manager.py` lines 204-281 for the existing mocked tests.

title: developer-guide.md §10 exact line ranges for StrEnum and import conventions
content:
The developer-guide.md §10 Code Quality section (lines 1704-1908) contains the two key project conventions at these exact locations:

- **StrEnum convention**: Lines 1875-1891. States: "Use `StrEnum` for all string-based enumerations — do not use `typing.Literal[\"a\", \"b\"]` or bare strings." Includes code example with `HandlerResultStatus(StrEnum)` and reaffirms it as project-wide convention referencing `ChatSettingsKey` and `ProxyType`.

- **Import placement / _AVAILABLE pattern**: Lines 1893-1908. States: "All imports belong at the top of the file. For optional dependencies that may not be installed... use a module-level `try/except ImportError` block." Includes the `_HTTPX_SOCKS_AVAILABLE` code example. Explicitly forbids imports inside methods/functions except for unavoidable cyclic dependencies.

Verified on 2026-06-14 during documentation organization audit. AGENTS.md and developer-guide.md §10 are the two reliable authoritative sources for both conventions.

title: tasks.md StrEnum usage is example-only, not codified as convention
content:
In docs/llm/tasks.md, StrEnum appears only in a code example at line 469 (`class ChatSettingsKey(StrEnum):`) within §4.1 Chat Settings Registration. It is NOT stated as a project convention ("use StrEnum, not Literal"). The convention note in that section (line 489) is about naming style (UPPER_CASE Python name ↔ kebab-case string value ↔ kebab-case TOML key), not about the enum type to use.

Additionally, the import-at-top/_AVAILABLE pattern is completely absent from tasks.md - no mention anywhere in the file. This extends the known doc sync gap (memory id 672c9585) with specific detail about StrEnum being present-but-uncodified rather than entirely missing.

Verified on 2026-06-14. The two sources that reliably codify both conventions are AGENTS.md (hard rules section) and docs/developer-guide.md (§10 Code Quality).

title: tasks.md §3 Gotchas table has 21 rows; §4 has 2 subsections
content:
As of 2026-06-14, docs/llm/tasks.md structure:

- **§3 Subtle Gotchas** (line 430-455): Contains a Markdown table with 21 data rows covering gotchas like MessageId wrapping int|str, chatId sign convention, DEFAULT_THREAD_ID=0, bot_owners dual-type, getChatSettings() tuple return, setChatSetting requires updatedBy, etc.

- **§4 Lessons Learned & Common Pitfalls** (line 458-601): Contains exactly 2 subsections:
  - §4.1 Chat Settings Registration (CRITICAL) — lines 462-545
  - §4.2 Strict JSON Schema Format for Structured Output — lines 548-601

This structure was verified during documentation organization audit. Useful reference for any future restructuring or gap analysis of tasks.md.

title: ResourceLimits.fromDict() field-by-field guard minimums reference
content:
Consolidated reference of all `ResourceLimits.fromDict()` minimum value guards in `lib/sandbox/types.py` (lines 97-120). Each numeric field has a minimum enforced at deserialization time:

| Field | Guard Type | Minimum | `fromDict({})` default | dataclass default |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| memoryMb | `max(32, …)` | 32 MB | 512 | 512 |
| memorySwapMb | `max(32, …)` | 32 MB | None | None |
| cpuCount | `max(0.1, …)` | 0.1 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| pidsLimit | `max(8, …)` | 8 | 64 | 64 |
| timeoutSeconds | `if < 30: logger.warning + clamp` | 30 | 30 | 30 |
| timeoutGraceSeconds | `max(0, …)` | 0 | 5 | 5 |

Key design properties:
- `fromDict({})` defaults **match** dataclass field defaults for all fields — no discrepancy between constructing from empty dict vs. no-args constructor.
- `timeoutSeconds` is the only field with a **logged** clamp (uses `logger.warning`), because it's the field operators are most likely to misconfigure. All others silently clamp.
- `memorySwapMb` default of `None` means "no swap limit" in Docker semantics (effective swap = memoryMb × 2). When absent from config dict, `None` passes through unguarded.
- `cpuCount` uses `float` guard (`max(0.1, …)`) — the minimum is fractional (0.1 CPU), not integer 1.

title: sandbox.toml memorySwapMb=512 disables swap when memoryMb=512
content:
In `configs/00-defaults/sandbox.toml`, the `[sandbox.limits]` section sets `memory-mb = 512` and `memory-swap-mb = 512`. Because Docker's `MemorySwap` is the *total* memory+swap limit, setting both to 512 means the effective swap available to the container is `512 - 512 = 0 MB` — swap is effectively disabled. This is the intended default for sandboxed code execution: no swap, preventing unbounded swap usage and protecting host disk I/O.

The `memorySwapMb` dataclass default is `None` (which Docker interprets as "no swap limit" = double memory), but the TOML config always provides an explicit value (512), so fromDict() produces `memorySwapMb=512` from config. The `None` default only takes effect when fromDict() is called with an empty dict or when ResourceLimits() is constructed without config.

title: Contradictory ResourceLimits.fromDict() memories resolved — stale ones deleted
content:
Two contradictory memories about `ResourceLimits.fromDict()` validation in `lib/sandbox/types.py` existed:

1. **Memory 2b798c5c** (CORRECT): Claimed fromDict() WAS updated with minimum value guards (max() wrapping) for memoryMb, cpuCount, timeoutSeconds, etc.
2. **Memory 337b9fcf** (OUTDATED, now deleted): Claimed fromDict() did NOT have validation and accepted negative/zero values.

The code was verified on 2026-06-14. The current implementation (lib/sandbox/types.py lines 95-120) confirms **memory 2b798c5c is accurate** — fromDict() uses max() guards for all numeric fields, with a logged clamp for timeoutSeconds. Memory 337b9fcf described the pre-fix state and has been deleted.

Additionally, memory **e49c81bf** (claimed fromDict({}) default of 60 for timeoutSeconds mismatched dataclass default of 30) was also stale — current fromDict() uses `data.get("timeout-seconds", 30)`, making fromDict({}) produce 30, matching the dataclass. It has been deleted.

**Lesson:** When a fix is applied, the memory describing the pre-fix state becomes stale. Consider adding a `resolved` label or the date of the fix to the original memory so future agents can quickly determine relevance without re-reading the code.

title: Sandbox type conversion: single-type architecture with toDict/fromDict on data classes
content:
The sandbox module (lib/sandbox/) uses a single-type architecture — there is NO split between internal "record" types and public "info" types. The same dataclass serves both as the persistence record and the public API type.

Actual architecture:
- `SessionInfo` (types.py:432-478) — has toDict() and fromDict() classmethod. Used directly by metadata store for persistence AND as public API type. No separate SessionRecord class exists.
- `RunInfo` (types.py:163-200) — has toDict() and fromDict() classmethod. Used directly by metadata store AND as public API type. No separate RunRecord class exists.
- `RunResult` (types.py:246-301) — has toDict() and fromDict(). Used for runCode() output.
- `ResourceLimits` (types.py:74-96) — has toDict() and fromDict(). Nested inside SessionInfo/RunInfo.
- `PackageInfo` (types.py:352-361) — has toDict() and fromDict().

Conversion flow:
  dataclass → toDict() → JSON dict → disk
  disk → JSON dict → fromDict() → dataclass

SandboxManager (manager.py) has NO private conversion helper methods like `_runResultToDict`, `_recordToSessionInfo`, or `_recordToRunInfo`. Its only private methods are:
- `_getLibPoolPath` (line 463) — path resolution
- `_touchSessionInternal` (line 476) — timestamp update
- `_validatePackageSpec` (line 1241) — input validation
- `_refreshPackageList` (line 1266) — container operation

The manager creates SessionInfo/RunInfo/RunResult instances directly (manager.py:348-357, 611-619, 681-699) and passes them to the metadata store, which serializes via instance.toDict() and deserializes via ClassName.fromDict(). All conversion logic already lives on the data classes — exactly the pattern recommended by AGENTS.md (prefer class-based designs with cohesive methods).

title: Correction: Memory fd118f83 fundamentally wrong — Database class has NO close() method at all
content:
Memory fd118f83 ("Database.close() can raise AttributeError in standalone scripts during cleanup") is fundamentally incorrect. Verification against actual code (internal/database/database.py) found:

- The `Database` class has NO `close()` method whatsoever — not conditionally, not at all.
- The class only has `__aexit__` (line 273) which calls `self.manager.closeAll()`.
- The `closeAll()` exists on `DatabaseManager` (manager.py:189), not on `Database`.
- Calling `.close()` on a Database instance would ALWAYS raise AttributeError, not just "sometimes" as the memory implies.

The real pattern for shutdown is: `await database.__aexit__(...)` (via async context manager) or `await database.manager.closeAll()`.

This memory should be treated as incorrect. The root cause was likely a verification script that tried `Database.close()` when the correct call would have been `database.manager.closeAll()` or using the async context manager.

title: EnsuredMessage: replyText and related attributes are NOT constructor params — set post-construction by factory methods
content:
The `EnsuredMessage.__init__` constructor (line 413 in `internal/bot/models/ensured_message.py`) takes only 4 required keyword-only params (`sender`, `recipient`, `messageId`, `date`) and 4 optional ones (`messageText`, `messageType`, `formatEntities`, `metadata`, `mediaGroupId`).

Critically, the following commonly-used attributes are NOT constructor parameters — they are initialized to defaults (None/False) and set later by factory methods (`fromTelegramMessage`, `fromMaxMessage`):

- `replyId` (Optional[MessageId], default None)
- `replyText` (Optional[str], default None)
- `isReply` (bool, default False)
- `isQuote` (bool, default False)
- `quoteText` (Optional[str], default None)
- `threadId` (Optional[int], default None)
- `isTopicMessage` (bool, default False)
- `mediaList` (initialized to empty list)
- `messagePrefix` (str, default "")

This means if you construct an EnsuredMessage directly (e.g., in tests), these attributes will NOT be populated unless you set them after construction. This is a common gotcha — tests that directly construct EnsuredMessage instances need to explicitly set `replyText` etc. if they test reply-related logic.

title: Sandbox /run command code extraction: args first, then reply (quoteText over replyText)
content:
When the `/run` command is invoked in `SandboxHandler.run_command()` (internal/bot/common/handlers/sandbox.py, around line 644), the code to execute is extracted with this priority:

1. **args.strip()** — the text after `/run` in the message text (e.g., `/run print(1)` → `args = "print(1)"`). If non-empty, this is used.
2. **Replied-to message text** — if args is empty AND `ensuredMessage.replyText` is truthy:
   - First tries `ensuredMessage.quoteText` (line 647-648)
   - Falls back to `ensuredMessage.replyText` (line 650)
3. If still empty after stripping, an error message is returned asking the user to provide code.

This means `/run` with no arguments checks the replied-to message for code, preferring quoted text over plain reply text. This is a non-obvious design decision that affects how users interact with the sandbox feature.

title: chat# format divergence: sandbox session IDs use literal "chat#" prefix vs MessageRecipient.__str__ uses dynamic chatType
content:
The sandbox handler's `getSessionId()` method (sandbox.py line 188-189) produces session IDs in the format `chat#<int>`, using the literal prefix `chat#` regardless of the actual chat type:

```python
def getSessionId(self, ensuredMessage: EnsuredMessage) -> str:
    return f"chat#{ensuredMessage.recipient.id}"
```

However, `MessageRecipient.__str__` (ensured_message.py line 130-137) formats using the dynamic `chatType.value`:

```python
def __str__(self) -> str:
    return f"{self.chatType.value}#{self.id}"
```

This means `getSessionId()` always produces `chat#123`, while `str(recipient)` produces e.g. `private#123`, `group#123`, or `channel#123`. These two formats are NOT interchangeable. The `chat#` format is also used as a debug/logging prefix in several other files (base.py line 277, react_on_user.py lines 118/121, llm/service.py lines 757/854). If someone assumes `getSessionId()` output matches `str(recipient)`, it will cause bugs in session lookups.

title: SandboxHandler uses raw config dict, not a typed SandboxConfig class
content:
The `SandboxHandler` does NOT construct or use a `SandboxConfig` class/object. Instead, it loads the entire `[sandbox]` TOML section as a raw dict via `self.configManager.get("sandbox")` (sandbox.py lines 82-83) and passes it directly to `SandboxManager.injectConfig(sandboxRaw)` (line 88). The handler reads individual keys like `"enabled"` from this raw dict (line 84) and stores it as `self.sandboxConfig`.

This means there is no type validation or schema enforcement for sandbox config at the handler level — the config structure is only validated when used by the sandbox library internals.

title: Topic-level mass staleness: 12/13 test-reorganization-stale-refs memories were already outdated
content:
During a memory audit of the "test-reorganization-stale-refs" topic (June 14, 2026), 12 out of 13 memories (92%) were found to be already outdated — the stale test path references they claimed existed had already been corrected in the source files before the memories were created (or shortly after).

The surviving memory (cf51dc01) was the one documenting the actual fix being applied to `tests/lib/sandbox/backends/test_docker.py` and `docs/llm/sandbox.md`. The other 12 memories all described stale paths that no longer existed.

This is a distinct pattern from the "fixes never applied" problem (memory d0a696e5). In that case, memories claimed fixes that were never committed. Here, the fixes WERE applied, but the memory system continued to record "issues exist" observations from sessions that didn't detect the corrections.

Quality signal: when a documentation sweep or reorganization completes, previously created "this file has stale X" memories become stale en masse. Agents should verify current file state before creating "stale reference" memories — grep the actual file rather than relying on prior session context.

title: Session-analyser can create factually-wrong bugfix memories from code-review recommendations (mechanism identified)
content:
The session-analyser agent can produce `type=bugfix` memories that describe bugs that DON'T EXIST in the actual code, by confusing code-review RECOMMENDATIONS with APPLIED fixes.

The mechanism:
1. The code-reviewer agent produces a review listing recommended fixes, using past-tense/commit-message language (e.g., "the httpx client was missing a timeout", "the Content-Type header was not checked", "BadRequestError was not caught") — describing what SHOULD be fixed.
2. The session-analyser reads this output and creates `type=bugfix` memories describing the bugs as if they exist and the fix was applied.
3. But the fixes were never actually committed — they were only recommendations from the review.

This is distinct from memories that become stale over time (where code evolves after the memory is written). These memories are factually WRONG at creation time.

CONFIRMED INSTANCES:
1. `lib/sandbox/backends/docker.py` — multiple bugfix memories from session-analyser describing fixes never committed (memories documented in memory d0a696e5)
2. `lib/ai/providers/basic_openai_provider.py` — 4 out of 9 memories in topic "image-generation-api-transport" were factually wrong, describing bugs that already had fixes in place. Git log confirmed zero commits to this file since June 8, 2026, yet the memories were created June 10-11. The deleted memories described: missing Content-Type header for URL downloads, missing httpx timeout/error handling, missing token extraction on error paths, missing BadRequestError handling — all of which existed in the code at the time.

EFFECT: Bug-fix memories from session-analyser (especially when derived from code-review) must be verified against actual code before being trusted. The verification technique is: `git log -- <file>` to check if the file was modified since the memory's creation date. If the file hasn't changed and the memory claims bugs were fixed, the memory was wrong at creation.

title: Contradictory memories: improvements.md DatabaseWrapper "fix" claimed but never applied
content:
During a June 14, 2026 documentation-drift audit, two memories about the same file gave opposite claims:

- Memory 7c1cc48c (filed 2026-06-10 12:32): "improvements.md DatabaseWrapper references corrected across suggestions #5, #6, #10, #12, and #21"
- Memory 9e74c205 (filed 2026-06-10 12:28): "improvements.md still references DatabaseWrapper in 5 suggestions"

Source code verification confirmed memory 9e74c205 was correct — all 4+ stale references remain at lines 290, 438, 996, 1394. Memory 7c1cc48c was factually wrong and was deleted.

Root cause analysis: The agent that created memory 7c1cc48c likely relied on a "Status Review Note" in improvements.md (line 1422) which says fixes SHOULD be applied, without actually reading the file to verify. This is a distinct failure mode: an agent creates a false memory by reading metadata/planned-fix notes about a file instead of reading the file's actual content.

Lesson: Never infer a fix was applied from notes/to-do markers. Always verify the actual file content before recording a memory about a fix. This is consistent with the project's memory-audit workflow that mandates source-code verification.

title: tests/lib/rate_limiter/__init__.py exports initRateLimiter test helper
content:
`tests/lib/rate_limiter/__init__.py` (48 lines) exports a reusable async test helper:

```python
async def initRateLimiter(limiterName: str = "default", slow: bool = False) -> RateLimiterManager
```

This function creates/configures a sliding window rate limiter via the singleton `RateLimiterManager.getInstance()`. It's idempotent — if a limiter with the given name already exists (checked via `manager.listRateLimiters()`), it returns the existing manager without re-registering. Two modes:
- `slow=False` (default) → 1000 requests/second (permissive, for normal tests)
- `slow=True` → 1 request/second (restrictive, for testing rate limiting behavior)

The function lives in a package `__init__.py` (not a standalone `test_*.py` file), making it importable as `from tests.lib.rate_limiter import initRateLimiter`. This pattern — using test package `__init__.py` for shared test utilities — is used across the 13 `tests/lib/*/__init__.py` files in the project.

Usage example in a test:
```python
from tests.lib.rate_limiter import initRateLimiter

async def test_something():
    manager = await initRateLimiter("test_api")
    # ... use the manager or pass it to the service under test
```

title: Dual token extraction blocks in _generateImageViaImagesApi with different defensive styles
content:
In `BasicOpenAIModel._generateImageViaImagesApi()` (`lib/ai/providers/basic_openai_provider.py`), there are two separate token extraction blocks that extract `input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, and `total_tokens` from `response.usage` — one on the empty/no-data error path and one on the success path. They use different defensive styles:

**Empty/no-data error path (~line 766-772)** — fires when response is not an `ImagesResponse` or has no `.data`:
```python
if hasattr(response, "usage") and response.usage is not None:
    inputTokens = getattr(response.usage, "input_tokens", None)
    outputTokens = getattr(response.usage, "output_tokens", None)
    totalTokens = getattr(response.usage, "total_tokens", None)
```
- Uses `hasattr()` guard before checking `is not None` (defensive: the variable `response` may not be an `ImagesResponse` instance on this path).
- Uses `getattr(obj, attr, None)` with three arguments (provides default `None` if the attribute is missing).

**Success path (~line 792-798):**
```python
if response.usage is not None:
    inputTokens = response.usage.input_tokens
    outputTokens = response.usage.output_tokens
    totalTokens = response.usage.total_tokens
```
- Relies on `is not None` check only (no `hasattr` — safe because `response` is guaranteed to be an `ImagesResponse` on this path).
- Uses direct attribute access (no `getattr` fallback — faster, assumes the fields exist).

**Why two blocks exist:** Token extraction was moved before image-data branching (see memory `8305064d`). The error-path block at ~line 766 fires for the "no image data in response" case and must defensively handle non-standard response objects; the success-path block at ~line 792 is called after the `ImagesResponse` type has been confirmed.

title: BadRequestError path in _generateImageViaImagesApi skips token extraction entirely
content:
In `BasicOpenAIModel._generateImageViaImagesApi()` (`lib/ai/providers/basic_openai_provider.py`, ~line 755-762), the `except BadRequestError` path returns a `ModelRunResult` with `inputTokens`, `outputTokens`, and `totalTokens` all left as `None` (i.e. the defaults from `ModelRunResult.__init__`). No attempt is made to extract usage from the error response.

This is asymmetric with the empty/no-data error path (~lines 766-772) which *does* attempt to extract token usage defensively (see related memory about dual token extraction blocks). The BadRequestError path's `ModelRunResult` is constructed as:
```python
return ModelRunResult(
    rawResult=e,
    status=ModelResultStatus.ERROR,
    error=e,
)
```

If downstream logic (e.g., LLM service token persistence) expects non-None token values from error paths, this gap may cause issues. Compare with the empty/no-data error path which explicitly passes extracted `inputTokens`/`outputTokens`/`totalTokens` into its `ModelRunResult`.

title: dropSession bypasses _globalLimiter — asymmetry with createSession
content:
`SandboxManager.dropSession()` (lib/sandbox/manager.py lines 398-461) does NOT call `self._globalLimiter.acquire()` or `self._globalLimiter.runSlot()` at any point. The only lock it acquires is the per-session lock via `self._lockRegistry.acquire(sessionId)`.

This is an asymmetry with session creation: `createSession` (around line 535-537) nests `self._lockRegistry.sessionLock(sessionId)` inside `self._globalLimiter.runSlot()`, meaning session creation IS throttled by the global rate limiter. But `dropSession` bypasses the global limiter entirely.

Design rationale: session teardown should always be possible regardless of global concurrency limits — cleanup must not be blocked when the limiter is saturated. Adding global limiter checks to `dropSession` would break this invariant.

Force path ordering in `dropSession`:
1. Container removal via `removeContainer(force=True)` — no `killContainer` call
2. `self._lockRegistry.forceCancel(sessionId)` — pre-cancels queued waiters
3. `self._lockRegistry.acquire(sessionId)` — attempt to acquire; if it fails and force=True, exception is swallowed
4. Cleanup: delete run records, delete workspace, delete metadata
5. Finally: `release()` and `clearCancelled()` — unconditional, no lock-acquired guard flag

title: Broken "Dynamic Cache Tables" cross-reference anchor in database-README.md and database-schema.md ToC
content:
Two stale references to "Dynamic Cache Tables" heading that no longer exists:

1. **database-README.md line 80**: `- [Dynamic Cache Tables](database-schema.md#dynamic-cache-tables) - API response caching` — link points to a heading in database-schema.md that doesn't exist (the section was removed when cache was unified into a single table with namespace column in migration_012).

2. **database-schema.md ToC line 32**: `- [Dynamic Cache Tables](#dynamic-cache-tables)` — self-reference to a heading that no longer exists in the document body. The actual section now uses `### cache` (line 682) and describes the unified cache table ("Unified cache table for all cache types (replaces separate cache tables from migration_012)").

These are broken cross-document and intra-document anchor references left over from the pre-migration_012 per-type cache tables era. The anchor removal was missed when the Dynamic Cache Tables section was removed during the June 2026 documentation cleanup.

title: database-README.md repository method name mismatches at specific line numbers
content:
Four method name mismatches persist in `docs/database-README.md` (verified June 14, 2026). All document `save*` or `set*` names but the actual repository classes use `add*`, `update*`, or `setCacheEntry`:

1. **Line 418**: `saveChatUser()` — actual method is `updateChatUser()` in internal/database/repositories/chat_users.py:46
2. **Line 446**: `saveSpamMessage()` — actual method is `addSpamMessage()` in internal/database/repositories/spam.py:75
3. **Line 451**: `saveDelayedTask()` — actual method is `addDelayedTask()` in internal/database/repositories/delayed_tasks.py:121
4. **Line 456**: `setCache()` — actual method is `setCacheEntry()` in internal/database/repositories/cache.py:243 (note: `setCacheStorage()` does exist at cache.py:97)

Additionally, line 448 still documents `getBayesToken()` which does not exist in SpamRepository (covered by separate memory 8433f0b2). These are in the Repository Architecture section block (lines 411-467) describing each repository's methods.

title: 4 flagged doc drifts remain unresolved after 4 days — follow-through gap in docs maintenance
content:
A June 14, 2026 documentation-drift verification re-checked 11 claims originally flagged on June 10, 2026. Results:

**7 claims FIXED** (Claims 2, 3, 4, 8, 9, 10 — CommandCategory values, getCurrentTimestamp(), sqlProvider.transaction(), setDefaultChatSettings, DivinationHandler, testing.md golden data)

**4 claims STILL UNRESOLVED**:
1. memory_swap_mb default (Claim 1, memory 9b65c1fb, severity=minor): doc still shows `512` on line 495 of docs/llm/configuration.md; code default is `None` (lib/sandbox/types.py:68), though config file override sets it to 512
2. getCachedChatServices return type (Claim 5, memory a87a464f, severity=must-fix): doc line 33 of docs/llm/services.md still shows `ChatSettingsDict` (non-Optional); actual return is `Optional[Dict[...]]` (internal/services/cache/service.py:557-559)
3. env var substitution missing from config loading order (Claim 6, memory f3e8ca3c, severity=must-fix): docs/llm/configuration.md Section 1 still lacks the `${VAR}` substitution step
4. resender config keys wrong (Claim 7, memory f7a220b3, severity=must-fix): docs/llm/configuration.md resender section still missing 6 actual keys (`dataSource`, `sourceTheadId`, `messageTypes`, `messagePrefix`, `messageSuffix`, `lastMessageDate`) and shows 1 spurious key (`mediaGroupDelaySecs`)

This means 3 must-fix and 1 minor severity documentation drifts were flagged 4 days prior but none have been corrected. This indicates a follow-through gap: memories flagged as drifts are not being actioned in subsequent sessions.

content:
During June 14, 2026 verification, the line-count table in docs/llm/index.md (Section 4.4) was spot-checked. The drift has worsened since the previous audit (memory ff9893bc, June 10):

- Doc was partially updated to 241 (matching old actual)
- June 14: doc still says 241, actual is 280 (now +39)

cache/service.py:
- Doc says 796, actual is 1,333 (drift of +537 lines)

database.py:
- Doc says 297, actual is 297 (correct — was updated since last audit when it was 191→297)

This confirms that occasional partial updates are insufficient — the table needs either automated generation or a stated policy of updating only on meaningful structural changes, not chasing raw line counts.

title: tests/database/test_db_wrapper.py exists — SKILL.md reference is valid
content:
The add-database-migration SKILL.md (.agents/skills/add-database-migration/SKILL.md, line 196) references `tests/database/test_db_wrapper.py` as the location for repository-level tests. This file DOES exist in the codebase as of June 14, 2026 — it is NOT a stale or broken reference. The earlier drift-claim memory (42ada230) implied the path might be stale, but that concern is unfounded: the file is real and the SKILL.md reference is correct.

Note: the SKILL.md path is `tests/database/test_db_wrapper.py`, NOT `tests/test_db_wrapper.py` (the original memory misstated the path). The file at `tests/database/test_db_wrapper.py` is valid.

title: docs/llm/index.md Python version is "3.12+", not "3.12 only"
content: Memory 329bff45 claimed that `docs/llm/index.md` Python version was corrected to "3.12 only", but the file (line 34) actually says `| Python | 3.12+ |`. This is consistent with AGENTS.md which says "Python 3.12 or newer" (line 12). The "3.12 only" version was never the actual state — the docs correctly say "3.12+", meaning Python 3.12 or any newer version is supported. No correction needed; the memory's claim was inaccurate.

title: testing.md: mockDatabaseWrapper fixture descriptions still say "Mocked database wrapper"
content:
Despite the DatabaseWrapper-class-reference fix in docs/llm/testing.md, the fixture descriptions for `mockDatabaseWrapper` on lines 173 and 404 still say "Mocked database wrapper" rather than "Mocked Database" (or similar). Specifically:

- Line 173: `mockDatabaseWrapper: Mocked database wrapper` (in a handler fixture docstring)
- Line 404: `mockDatabaseWrapper: Mocked database wrapper fixture` (in a test method docstring)

The fixture name `mockDatabaseWrapper` is intentionally kept (it's a fixture name from conftest.py, not a class reference), but the descriptive text still uses the old "wrapper" language. This is a minor documentation polishing issue — no functionality impact, but the descriptions are not yet fully aligned with the post-rename naming convention.

content:
As of June 14, 2026 verification, emojis remain present in three non-archive documentation files (contradicting AGENTS.md no-emoji policy). Updated line numbers from verification:

title: Correction: lib-cache-design-v0.md acknowledges ValueConverter in Current Implementation Note
content:
Memory 0deee0a8 claims the cache design doc (docs/design/lib-cache-design-v0.md) has "Missing ValueConverter protocol" as a gap, but verification on 2026-06-14 shows the file HAS a "Current Implementation Note" at lines 3-10 that explicitly lists ValueConverter as an API not documented in the original design. The note reads: "The implemented system includes these current APIs not documented in the original design: - `ValueConverter` protocol — for converting values to/from strings - `StringValueConverter` — pass-through string converter - `JsonValueConverter` — JSON serialization converter."

The body of the design doc does not include ValueConverter in its code examples (only shows KeyGenerator), which is consistent with the Implementation Note stating these were additions. The drift is acknowledged, not silently omitted. The memory 0deee0a8 should be read with this nuance — the doc has a disclaimer pointing to the current code.

title: ai-aurumentation-design.md has stale "Draft for Review" at line 1478 despite ✅ Implemented at line 3
content:
docs/design/ai-aurumentation-design.md has a mixed status situation: line 3 correctly shows "Status: ✅ Implemented (in lib/aurumentation)" (added during June 2026 docs sync per memory 57f8fff7), but line 1478 at the end of the file still contains the original document metadata: "Status: Draft for Review" with "Questions for Review" and "Next Steps" sections.

This means a reader who jumps to the end of the document will see "Draft for Review" and may incorrectly conclude the design is not implemented. The old metadata block at the bottom (~lines 1470-1482) was never removed when the status header was updated.

title: simplification.md quick wins table has stale line-number references verified
content:
docs/suggestions/simplification.md, lines 1166-1173 (Quick Wins table), has line-number references that have drifted due to subsequent code changes. Two specific stale references verified on 2026-06-14:

Line 1171: `lib/ai/manager.py:51-53` — references the f-string-without-f-prefix bug ("Provider type is not specified for provider {provider_name}"), but line 51 in the current file is inside the LLMManager class docstring, not the bug location. title: multi-source-advanced.toml has dead links to archived MySQL/PostgreSQL example files
content:
docs/examples/multi-source-advanced.toml lines 210-212 contain dead links to mysql-configuration.toml and postgresql-configuration.toml:

```
# For more configuration options, see:
# - mysql-configuration.toml
# - postgresql-configuration.toml
```

These .toml files no longer exist in docs/examples/ — they were archived to docs/archive/examples/mysql-configuration.toml and docs/archive/examples/postgresql-configuration.toml. The links should either be updated to point to the archive location or removed.
